Most common for HVAC capacities **up to ~150 TR**. - Components: cylinder, piston, suction valve, discharge valve, crankshaft. - **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}}$ ### 2.1.3 Rotary Compressors | Type | Working Principle | Capacity Range | |---|---|---| | **Scroll** | One scroll orbits the other, trapping and compressing vapour in pockets | 1–25 TR | | **Screw (Twin)** | Two intermeshing helical rotors | 20–1500 TR | | **Rotary Vane** | Sliding vanes in an eccentric rotor | Small, refrigeration | ### 2.1.4 Centrifugal Compressor - Used for **large commercial HVAC** (>150 TR). - Kinetic energy of impeller → pressure rise in diffuser. ### 2.1.5 Key Performance Parameters - **Compression Ratio:** $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ - **Isentropic Efficiency:** $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$ - **Compressor Work (per kg):** $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ - **Clearance Volume Ratio:** $c = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ ## 2.2 Condensers ### 2.2.1 Types - **Air-Cooled:** Refrigerant inside tubes, ambient air blown across by fans. - **Water-Cooled (Shell-and-Tube):** Water inside tubes, refrigerant in shell. - **Water-Cooled (Shell-and-Coil):** Coil immersed in refrigerant shell. - **Evaporative:** Combines air-cooled + water spray. ### 2.2.2 Operating Principle - Reject latent heat of condensation + sensible heat of superheat. - Heat rejected: $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ - Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) governs size. ### 2.2.3 Critical Design Parameters - **Condensing Pressure** → sets compressor discharge pressure. - **Subcooling** → increases refrigeration effect (point 3 below saturation line). ## 2.3 Expansion Devices ### 2.3.1 Capillary Tube - Fixed bore, fixed length — passive. - Acts as a **metering + pressure drop** device. - Common in small hermetic systems (window AC, refrigerators). ### 2.3.2 Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV/TEV) - **Active metering** — senses evaporator outlet superheat. - Maintains **constant superheat** at evaporator exit. - Components: sensing bulb, diaphragm, spring, needle seat. ### 2.3.3 Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV) - Maintains **constant evaporator pressure**, not superheat. - Used in systems with **wide load variation**. ### 2.3.4 Float Valve (High/Low Side) - Maintains **constant liquid level** in flooded evaporator. ### 2.3.5 Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV) - Microprocessor-controlled stepper motor. - Highest precision; modern VRF/VRV systems. ### 2.3.6 Thermodynamic Process - Throttling is **Isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$). - No work, no heat transfer, but entropy **increases**. ## 2.4 Evaporators ### 2.4.1 Types - **Direct Expansion (DX) / Dry Expansion** — refrigerant boils inside tubes; common in AC. - **Flooded** — liquid refrigerant fills shell; pump required; high efficiency. - **Shell-and-Tube** — water/brine inside tubes, refrigerant in shell. - **Finned-Tube Coil** — extended surface for air-side heat transfer. ### 2.4.2 Refrigeration Effect (per kg) $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ ### 2.4.3 Required Air-Side Heat Transfer $$Q_L = \dot{m}_r \times (h_1 - h_4) = \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_{a,in} - T_{a,out})$$ ### 2.4.4 Critical Design Parameters - **Superheat** at outlet — must be ≥ 4–6 K to ensure no liquid returns to compressor (slugging hazard). - **Evaporator Pressure Drop** — should be < 1 °C saturation loss. ## 2.5 Fan Blowers ### 2.5.1 Types - **Axial Flow Fans** — high flow, low pressure (evaporator/condenser coil face). - **Centrifugal Fans** — medium pressure, ducted air handling units. - **Propeller Fans** — large diameter, condenser applications. - **In-Line Duct Fans** — mixed-flow. ### 2.5.2 Fan Laws (KTU High-Yield) | Law | Equation | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Law 1 — Flow** | $Q_2/Q_1 = (N_2/N_1) \times (D_2/D_1)^3$ | Flow ∝ Speed × Diameter³ | | **Law 2 — Pressure** | $\Delta P_2/\Delta P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2 \times (D_2/D_1)^2$ | Pressure ∝ Speed² × Diameter² | | **Law 3 — Power** | $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3 \times (D_2/D_1)^5$ | Power ∝ Speed³ × Diameter⁵ | ### 2.5.3 Real-World Utility - Coil face velocity: typically **2–3 m/s** for evaporator; **3–5 m/s** for condenser. - Sizing fans too small → poor heat transfer; too large → carryover of condensate. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $\text{COP}$ | Coefficient of Performance | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_{net}}$ | — | | $q_L$ | Refrigeration Effect per kg | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | | $w_c$ | Compressor Work per kg | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | | $q_H$ | Heat Rejected per kg | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | | $\eta_{isen}$ | Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$ | — | | $r_p$ | Compression Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ | — | | $\eta_v$ | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | — | | $Q_L$ | Cooling Capacity | $Q_L = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW (TR) | | $\text{TR}$ | Ton of Refrigeration | $1 \,\text{TR} = 3.517 \,\text{kW}$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU BOARD TRICK:** When the **throttling process** is asked, write $h_3 = h_4$ immediately — never assume "no pressure drop" or "no enthalpy change" without justification. Examiners *love* giving partial credit for this exact statement. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance of the Complete VCRS ### Step 1 — Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to Compressor Ignoring kinetic and potential energy: $$w_{c,in} = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{...(per kg of refrigerant)}$$ ### Step 2 — SFEE on Condenser (Heat Rejected) $$q_H = h_2 - h_3$$ ### Step 3 — SFEE on Expansion Valve (No Work, No Heat Transfer) $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(Isenthalpic Throttling)}$$ ### Step 4 — SFEE on Evaporator (Heat Absorbed = Refrigeration Effect) $$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$ ### Step 5 — Combine: 1st Law for the Whole Cycle $$\begin{aligned} \text{Energy In} &= \text{Energy Out} \\ w_c + q_L &= q_H \\ \therefore q_H - q_L &= w_c \\ (h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4) &= h_2 - h_1 \end{aligned}$$ Substituting $h_3 = h_4$: $$\begin{aligned} (h_2 - h_4) - (h_1 - h_4) &= h_2 - h_1 \\ h_2 - h_1 &= h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(Identity — Verified ✓)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: COP of Vapour Compression Cycle ### Ideal Cycle (Carnot Reversed Approximation) For a reversible cycle operating between reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$: $$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ ### Real VCRS — Defining $$\text{COP}_{real} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_{c}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ ### Expressing in Terms of Isentropic Efficiency $$\begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{real} &= \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_{2s} - h_1)/\eta_{isen}} \\ &= \eta_{isen} \cdot \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_{2s} - h_1} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency of Reciprocating Compressor The swept volume of the piston = $V_s$. The clearance volume = $V_c$. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Clearance Ratio: } c &= \dfrac{V_c}{V_s} \\ \text{Expansion of trapped vapour: } V_4 &= V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \\ \text{Effective Suction Volume: } V_e &= V_s - (V_4 - V_c) \\ \eta_v &= \dfrac{V_e}{V_s} = 1 + c - c\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \end{aligned}$$ where $n = 1.3$ for **polytropic** compression (typical refrigerants). ## 3.4 Derivation: Cooling Capacity in Tons of Refrigeration $$\begin{aligned} Q_L \,[\text{kW}] &= \dot{m}_r \,[\text{kg/s}] \times (h_1 - h_4) \,[\text{kJ/kg}] \\ Q_L \,[\text{TR}] &= \dfrac{Q_L \,[\text{kW}]}{3.517} \\ \text{Since } 1 \,\text{TR} &= 211 \, \text{kJ/min} = 3.517 \,\text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.5 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern) > **Problem:** A refrigeration system uses R-134a. The compressor admits refrigerant at **0.14 MPa, –10 °C** and discharges at **0.8 MPa, 50 °C**. The refrigerant exits the condenser as saturated liquid at 0.8 MPa. Mass flow rate = **0.05 kg/s**. Calculate: > (a) Compressor power (ideal & actual if $\eta_{isen}=0.85$). > (b) COP. > (c) Cooling capacity in TR. ### Given: - $P_1 = 0.14$ MPa, $T_1 = -10$ °C → superheated vapour - $P_2 = 0.8$ MPa, $T_2 = 50$ °C → superheated vapour - $P_3 = 0.8$ MPa, saturated liquid - $\dot{m} = 0.05$ kg/s - $\eta_{isen} = 0.85$ ### From R-134a Tables: - $h_1 = 392.7$ kJ/kg - $h_{2s}$ (at $P_2$, $s_{2s}=s_1$) $\approx 423.5$ kJ/kg - $h_3$ (saturated liquid at 0.8 MPa) = 95.5 kJ/kg - $h_4 = h_3 = 95.5$ kJ/kg (throttling) ### Solution: #### (a) Compressor Work **Actual Work per kg:** $$\begin{aligned} w_c &= \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}} \\ &= \dfrac{423.5 - 392.7}{0.85} \\ &= \dfrac{30.8}{0.85} \\ &= 36.24 \,\text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Actual Compressor Power:** $$\begin{aligned} W_c &= \dot{m} \times w_c \\ &= 0.05 \times 36.24 \\ &= 1.812 \,\text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ #### (b) Refrigeration Effect and COP $$\begin{aligned} q_L &= h_1 - h_4 = 392.7 - 95.5 = 297.2 \,\text{kJ/kg} \\ \text{COP} &= \dfrac{q_L}{w_c} = \dfrac{297.2}{36.24} \\ \text{COP} &= 8.20 \end{aligned}$$ #### (c) Cooling Capacity in TR $$\begin{aligned} Q_L \,[\text{kW}] &= \dot{m} \times q_L = 0.05 \times 297.2 = 14.86 \,\text{kW} \\ Q_L \,[\text{TR}] &= \dfrac{14.86}{3.517} = 4.22 \,\text{TR} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice that the **ideal isentropic work** uses $h_{2s}$, but the **actual compressor power** uses $h_2$ (which is obtained by dividing $h_{2s} - h_1$ by $\eta_{isen}$, then adding $h_1$). Many students confuse these two — leading to 2–3 mark deductions. ## 3.6 Heat Exchanger Sizing (Log Mean Temperature Difference) For any condenser or evaporator: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_1 &= T_{H,in} - T_{C,out} \\ \Delta T_2 &= T_{H,out} - T_{C,in} \\ \text{LMTD} &= \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\ Q &= U \times A \times \text{LMTD} \end{aligned}$$ where $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = heat transfer area. > [!TIP] > **Exam Shortcut (KTU Permitted):** If $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within 5%), simply use arithmetic mean. Document this assumption in your answer to earn full marks. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — Complete VCRS Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[State 1: Low-Pressure
Saturated Vapour] --> B[COMPRESSOR] B --> C[State 2: High-Pressure
Superheated Vapour] C --> D[CONDENSER] D --> E[State 3: High-Pressure
Saturated Liquid] E --> F[EXPANSION DEVICE] F --> G[State 4: Low-Pressure
Two-Phase Mixture] G --> H[EVAPORATOR] H --> A D -.Heat Rejected qH.-> AIR2[Ambient Air] H -.Heat Absorbed qL.-> AIR1[Conditioned Space Air] FAN1[Fan Blower] -.Forces Air.-> H FAN2[Condenser Fan] -.Forces Air.-> D ``` ## 4.2 Component Hierarchy — Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD VCRS[Vapour Compression
Refrigeration System] --> COMP[Compressor] VCRS --> COND[Condenser] VCRS --> EXP[Expansion Device] VCRS --> EVAP[Evaporator] VCRS --> FAN[Fan Blower] COMP --> C1[Positive Displacement] COMP --> C2[Dynamic] C1 --> C1A[Reciprocating] C1 --> C1B[Rotary] C1B --> C1B1[Scroll] C1B --> C1B2[Screw] C1B --> C1B3[Vane] C2 --> C2A[Centrifugal] C2 --> C2B[Axial] COND --> CO1[Air-Cooled] COND --> CO2[Water-Cooled] COND --> CO3[Evaporative] CO2 --> CO2A[Shell and Tube] CO2 --> CO2B[Shell and Coil] EXP --> E1[Capillary Tube] EXP --> E2[TXV Thermostatic] EXP --> E3[AXV Automatic] EXP --> E4[Float Valve] EXP --> E5[EEV Electronic] EVAP --> EV1[Direct Expansion DX] EVAP --> EV2[Flooded] EVAP --> EV3[Shell and Tube] EVAP --> EV4[Finned Tube Coil] FAN --> F1[Axial Flow] FAN --> F2[Centrifugal] FAN --> F3[Propeller] FAN --> F4[Mixed Flow Inline] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Air-Side Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INSIDE[Conditioned Space] RA[Return Air
Warm 26 C] end subgraph AHU[Air Handling Unit] FA1[Filter] --> CL[Cooling Coil
EVAPORATOR] CL --> FB1[Fan Blower
Centrifugal] FB1 --> SA[Supply Air
Cool 14 C] end subgraph OUTSIDE[Ambient] OA[Outside Air Hot 35 C] CD[Condenser Coil] FB2[Condenser Fan
Axial] end RA --> FA1 SA --> INSIDE OA --> CD CD --> FB2 FB2 --> OUTSIDE REFR[Refrigerant Loop] -. Connects .-> CL REFR -. Connects .-> CD ``` ## 4.4 State-Point Process Flow (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
Wc in work] --> P2 P2[Process 2 to 3
Condensation
qH out heat] --> P3 P3[Process 3 to 4
Isenthalpic Throttling
No work or heat] --> P4 P4[Process 4 to 1
Evaporation
qL in heat] --> P1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: List any four major components of a vapour compression refrigeration system and state the function of each.** **Model Answer:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Compressor** | Raises refrigerant pressure from low (evaporator) to high (condenser); circulates refrigerant | | **Condenser** | Rejects latent heat of condensation from hot, high-pressure vapour to ambient | | **Expansion Device** | Drops refrigerant pressure from high to low (isenthalpic throttling); meters flow | | **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from conditioned space as refrigerant evaporates at low pressure/temperature | *(Any 4 of the 5 — fan blower may also be included)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Explain why the throttling process in an expansion valve is considered isenthalpic. What is its effect on entropy?** **Model Answer:** The throttling process is a **steady-flow process** through a constriction (capillary/txv) where: - No shaft work crosses the boundary: $W = 0$ - No heat transfer (assumed adiabatic and rapid): $Q = 0$ - Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. Applying SFEE: $h_3 = h_4$ → **Isenthalpic**. Because the process is **irreversible** (finite pressure drop through friction), entropy **increases**: $s_4 > s_3$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: With the help of a block diagram and a P-h diagram, explain the working of a simple vapour compression refrigeration system. Derive the expression for COP and state any two assumptions made.** #### (a) Block Diagram & Working (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (to be drawn in answer sheet):** ``` [Compressor] → [Condenser] → [Expansion Valve] → [Evaporator] → (back to Compressor) ``` **Working Principle (4 Process Steps):** - **Step 1 — Compression (1→2):** Low-pressure, low-temperature saturated vapour is drawn into the compressor. Work $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ is supplied, raising pressure to $P_2$ and temperature to $T_2$ (superheated). - **Step 2 — Condensation (2→3):** Hot, high-pressure vapour enters the condenser. Heat $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ is rejected to the cooling medium (air/water) at constant pressure, condensing the refrigerant to saturated liquid. - **Step 3 — Expansion (3→4):** Saturated liquid at high pressure passes through the expansion device. Pressure suddenly drops; some liquid flashes to vapour. The process is **isenthalpic**: $h_3 = h_4$. - **Step 4 — Evaporation (4→1):** Low-pressure, low-temperature mixture enters evaporator. Heat $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ is absorbed from the space, fully vaporising the refrigerant. #### (b) Derivation of COP (7 Marks) $$\begin{aligned} \text{COP} &= \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{\text{Refrigeration Effect}}{\text{Compressor Work}} \\ \text{COP} &= \dfrac{q_L}{w_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \end{aligned}$$ **For Ideal (Reversible) Cycle:** $$\text{COP}_{ideal} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Derivation from Carnot principles:** $$\begin{aligned} q_L &= T_L \,(s_1 - s_4) \\ q_H &= T_H \,(s_2 - s_3) \\ \text{For reversible cycle: } s_1 = s_2 \text{ and } s_3 = s_4 \\ \text{Hence: } s_1 - s_4 &= s_2 - s_3 \\ \dfrac{q_L}{T_L} &= \dfrac{q_H}{T_H} \\ q_H &= q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} \\ w_c &= q_H - q_L = q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right) = q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \\ \text{COP} &= \dfrac{q_L}{w_c} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Compression is isentropic (no friction, no heat loss). 2. Pressure drops in evaporator and condenser are negligible. 3. Refrigerant leaves condenser as saturated liquid and evaporator as saturated vapour. 4. No heat loss to/from surroundings. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Explain the construction and working of (i) Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) and (ii) Capillary Tube as expansion devices in a VCRS. Compare them.** #### (i) Thermostatic Expansion Valve (7 Marks) **Construction:** - **Sensing Bulb:** Clamped to evaporator outlet; charged with refrigerant. - **Diaphragm:** Responds to bulb pressure. - **Spring:** Opposes diaphragm movement. - **Needle & Seat:** Metering orifice. - **Equalizer Line (Internal or External):** Senses evaporator outlet pressure. **Working:** - Bulb senses **evaporator outlet superheat**. - As load increases → more liquid in evaporator → superheat drops → bulb pressure falls → diaphragm deflects → valve opens more → more refrigerant flows. - As load decreases → superheat rises → bulb pressure rises → valve closes slightly. - **Maintains constant superheat** at evaporator exit. **Pressure Balance at Diaphragm:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{bulb} &= P_{evap} + P_{spring} \\ \therefore \, \Delta P_{valve} &= P_{bulb} - P_{evap} - P_{spring} \end{aligned}$$ #### (ii) Capillary Tube (7 Marks) **Construction:** - A **long, small-bore copper tube** (typically 0.5–2 mm bore, 1–6 m length). - Coiled or wrapped to fit compactly. - **Fixed geometry** — no moving parts. **Working:** - Pressure drop occurs due to **friction** along its length. - Acts as a fixed resistance — mass flow rate depends on pressure differential. - Suitable for systems with **steady operating conditions** (domestic refrigerator, window AC). #### Comparison Table | Feature | TXV | Capillary Tube | |---|---|---| | Type | Active (modulating) | Passive (fixed) | | Response to load | Excellent | Poor | | Superheat control | Maintains constant | Cannot maintain | | Cost | High | Low | | Application | Central AC, VRF | Refrigerator, window AC | | Efficiency | Higher at part-load | Lower at part-load | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes — Mark Deductions (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):** > > 1. **Drawing P-h diagram with axes swapped** (pressure on x-axis): Lose 1 mark per KTU evaluator. > 2. **Writing $h_3 = h_4$ without justifying** *why* (no heat transfer, no work): Lose 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting to convert kW → TR** (or vice versa) in numerical answers: Lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Confusing $h_{2s}$ vs $h_2$** — using actual enthalpy in denominator of efficiency formula. > 5. **Omitting the fan blower** in component listings — newer KTU papers explicitly include it. > 6. **Forgetting the superheat region** in P-h diagram — must extend point 1 to the right of the saturation dome. > 7. **Sign convention errors** in energy equations (always state "per kg of refrigerant"). > 8. **Skipping unit analysis** — KTU examiners award partial marks for correct units even if the number is wrong. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — KTU Module 1** > > ✅ **Four Processes:** Compression → Condensation → Expansion → Evaporation > > ✅ **Four State Points:** 1 (Comp inlet) → 2 (Comp outlet) → 3 (Cond outlet) → 4 (Evap inlet) > > ✅ **Key Equations:** > - $\text{COP} = (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$ > - $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ > - $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ > - $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ > - $h_3 = h_4$ (throttling) > - $\eta_{isen} = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_2 - h_1)$ > - $Q_L [\text{TR}] = Q_L [\text{kW}] / 3.517$ > > ✅ **Component Functions (one-line each):** > - **Compressor:** Pressure lift + refrigerant circulation > - **Condenser:** Heat rejection (vapour → liquid) > - **Expansion Device:** Pressure drop (liquid → mixture, isenthalpic) > - **Evaporator:** Heat absorption (mixture → vapour) > - **Fan Blower:** Air-side convective heat transfer > > ✅ **Critical Numbers to Memorize:** > - $1 \,\text{TR} = 3.517 \,\text{kW} = 211 \,\text{kJ/min}$ > - Typical compression ratio: 3–6 > - Evaporator superheat: 4–6 K > - Fan coil face velocity: 2–3 m/s (evap), 3–5 m/s (cond) > > ✅ **TXV vs Capillary:** Active vs Passive; constant superheat vs fixed flow. > > ✅ **Reciprocating Compressor:** Clearance ratio → volumetric efficiency formula. > > ✅ **Centrifugal Compressor:** For >150 TR capacity. > > ✅ **Fan Laws:** Flow ∝ ND³, Pressure ∝ N²D², Power ∝ N³D⁵. > > ✅ **Subcooling** at condenser exit → increases refrigeration effect (move point 3 left). > > ✅ **Superheat** at evaporator exit → protects compressor from liquid slugging.

topics.title: Air Conditioning Protection and Engine Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Protection and Engine Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Air Conditioning Protection – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Conditioning Protection** refers to the integrated network of automatic safety devices, control interlocks, and alarm systems installed in a refrigeration/HVAC plant to safeguard the **compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion valve, refrigerant circuit, ducting, and the conditioned space** from damage caused by abnormal pressure, temperature, flow, electrical, or fire conditions.

In the marine/shipboard context (KTU PEMUT742 scope), the AC plant serves the accommodation block, bridge, galley, computer rooms, and provision stores. Protection must therefore be **fail-safe, automatically resettable where possible, and must give audible/visual alarms before initiating shutdown** (per SOLAS and classification society rules — DNV, Lloyd's, ABS).

### 1.2 Engine Protection – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Protection** refers to the set of automatic monitoring and cut-out devices fitted on the prime movers that drive the refrigeration compressors — typically **diesel engines, large electric motors, or screw-type refrigerant compressors** — to prevent mechanical damage from loss of lubrication, overheating, overspeed, low coolant, or fire.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain-English Explanation)

**Analogy 1 — AC Protection = "The Immune System of the Plant"**
Think of a shipboard AC plant as a human body. The refrigerant is the blood, the compressor is the heart, and the protection devices (HP/LP cutouts, oil pressure switches, flow switches, thermostats) are the white blood cells and pain nerves. They don't run the plant — but they scream or shut things down the moment something feels wrong (high fever = high pressure cutout; bleeding = refrigerant leak detected by low pressure cutout).

**Analogy 2 — Engine Protection = "The Pilot's Black-Box"**
Modern engine protection is like the safety net a fighter pilot trusts — the pilot (engineer) may push the engine, but the Automatic Flight Control System (overspeed trip, low lub-oil trip, high cooling-water temp trip) will eject the aircraft (shut the engine) before a crash. The protection never flies the plane, but it saves it from catastrophic failure.

**Analogy 3 — Refrigerant Cutout Settings = "Blood Pressure Limits"**
Just as a doctor says normal BP is 120/80, an AC engineer sets the HP cutout at ~**21 bar** for R-134a and LP cutout at ~**0.5 bar**. Anything outside is "hypertension" or "hypotension" of the refrigerant circuit.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard sea-level atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Critical metrics monitored in AC plant:** Suction pressure, Discharge pressure, Oil pressure differential, Condenser water flow, Evaporator air temperature, Motor current
- **Critical metrics monitored in prime mover:** Lube-oil pressure, Cooling-water temperature, Exhaust temperature, Crankcase pressure, Overspeed
- **Response time of protection devices:** typically **< 3 seconds** for hard-wired cutouts
- **Classification:** HP/LP switches are typically **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** snap-action type

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Air Conditioning Protection – Subsystem Breakdown

#### A. Refrigerant Circuit Protection

| Device | Function | Typical Setting (R-134a) |
|---|---|---|
| **High Pressure (HP) Cutout** | Trips compressor if discharge pressure exceeds safe limit | **18 – 21 bar** |
| **Low Pressure (LP) Cutout** | Trips compressor if suction pressure drops below safe limit (loss of refrigerant) | **0.4 – 0.7 bar (gauge)** |
| **Oil Pressure Failure Switch (OPFS)** | Trips if oil pressure < discharge pressure by a set differential | **ΔP = 1.8 – 2.1 bar** |
| **Discharge Temp. Cutout** | Trips on superheat above ~ 130 °C | **120 – 135 °C** |
| **Frost Stat (Evap. Outlet)** | Prevents ice formation on evaporator coil | **−1 °C to +3 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Setting Logic:** HP and LP cutout values are derived directly from the refrigerant's saturation pressure–temperature curve. The HP setting corresponds to the maximum condensing temperature the compressor discharge valves can withstand; the LP setting corresponds to the lowest saturation temperature that guarantees adequate refrigerant flow to the compressor suction (to prevent overheating of the motor-cooled hermetic/semi-hermetic compressor).

#### B. Electrical Protection for Compressor Motor

- **Overload relay (OLR):** trips at 105–115 % of full-load current after a time delay (inverse-time characteristic).
- **Single-phasing preventer (Phase failure relay):** trips if any one phase is lost, preventing the motor from drawing heavy current on the remaining two phases (which would cause rapid winding burnout).
- **Phase sequence relay:** prevents reverse rotation of 3-phase scroll/screw compressors.
- **Earth-fault relay:** trips on insulation breakdown.
- **Anti-short-cycle timer (ASCT):** enforces minimum OFF-time (typically **3 – 6 minutes**) between successive starts to equalise refrigerant pressures and protect the motor from locked-rotor current surges.

#### C. Airside / Ductwork Protection

- **Dirty filter switch (ΔP switch):** indicates when filter needs cleaning; sets off alarm at ΔP > **250 Pa** across the filter bank.
- **Fire damper (fusible link type):** closes duct at **72 °C** to prevent smoke spread between compartments (SOLAS requirement).
- **Smoke detector in return air:** trips the plant to smoke mode or shuts down AHU.
- **Frost protection thermostat** on chilled-water coils / DX coils.

#### D. Condenser / Cooling-Water Protection

- **Flow switch (paddle or differential-pressure type):** trips compressor if cooling-water flow is lost — prevents condenser over-pressure.
- **Temperature sensor** for condenser water outlet.

### 2.2 Engine Protection – Subsystem Breakdown

#### A. Lubricating Oil System Protection

- **Low lube-oil pressure trip:** shutdown at **1.2 – 1.8 bar** (depending on engine rating).
- **Lube-oil temperature high alarm:** at **75 °C**; trip at **85 °C**.
- **Lube-oil filter differential pressure alarm:** ΔP > **0.35 bar** indicates clogging.
- **Crankcase explosion relief valve** (Blow-by valve): opens at **+0.05 to +0.10 bar** to prevent crankcase explosions from oil mist accumulation.

#### B. Cooling Water System Protection

- **High jacket-cooling-water temperature alarm/trip:** alarm at **85 °C**, trip at **95 °C**.
- **Low cooling-water flow switch:** shutdown at loss of flow.
- **High-temperature difference alarm** between cylinder heads (indicates clogged individual cylinder jacket passages).

#### C. Fuel System Protection

- **High fuel-oil temperature alarm** (prevents vapor lock & coking): alarm at **150 °C** for HFO.
- **Low fuel-oil pressure alarm/trip** before the booster pump.
- **Fuel-oil filter differential pressure alarm.**

#### D. Starting Air System Protection

- **Starting air pressure low alarm** at **18 bar**; automatic start of auxiliary air compressor below this.
- **Safety valve on air reservoir** set at **30 bar** (working pressure **25 / 30 bar** typical).
- **Flame arrestor** in crankcase breather.

#### E. Overspeed & Fire Protection

- **Overspeed trip:** hydraulic or mechanical trip at **110 % of rated speed** (mandatory under classification rules).
- **Fire detection in engine room:** smoke + heat + flame detectors, integrated with **fixed fire-fighting system** (CO₂ / NOVEC / water mist).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{HP-cutout} \approx P_{sat}(T_{cond,max} + 5°C)$ | HP cutout set ≈ saturation pressure at 5 °C above max condensing temp. | bar (abs / gauge) |
| 2 | $P_{LP-cutout} \approx P_{sat}(T_{evap,min} - 3°C)$ | LP cutout set ≈ saturation pressure at 3 °C below min evaporating temp. | bar |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{oil-switch} = P_{crankcase} - P_{suction}$ | Differential that OPFS measures. | **1.8 – 2.1 bar** for screw comp. |
| 4 | $t_{min,OFF} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{max} \cdot V_{crankcase}}{Q_{equaliser}}$ | Anti-short-cycle minimum OFF time. | **180 – 360 s** |
| 5 | $\eta_{is} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2} - h_{1}}$ | Isentropic efficiency used to size motor and OLR. | dimensionless |
| 6 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance — basis of protection setpoint tuning. | 2.5 – 4.0 typical |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Heat leak that LP cutout must guard against. | kW |
| 8 | $I_{trip} = 1.10 \times I_{FLC}$ | OLR trip current setting. | Amperes |
| 9 | $N_{trip} = 1.10 \times N_{rated}$ | Overspeed trip. | rpm |
| 10 | $t_{crankcase,rel} = \dfrac{m_{mist}}{A_{relief} \cdot C_{d} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho_{gas}}}$ | Crankcase relief valve discharge time. | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** When writing saturation pressures in exam answers, students often confuse **absolute (bara)** vs **gauge (barg)** pressures. The relation is always:
> $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$
> Most marine switch gauges are calibrated in **barg**, so when applying refrigerant tables (which are in **bara**), always add 1.013 bar.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial shipping, a single failure in the AC plant can render the entire accommodation block uninhabitable, cause spoilage of refrigerated cargo, or in the case of LNG/LPG carriers, lead to cargo-tank pressure anomalies. **Engine protection failures**, on the other hand, can cause catastrophic main-engine failure with potential loss of propulsion — the single highest-priority safety system on any vessel. Both subsystems must be **type-approved** by classification societies and tested during sea trials and every docking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of HP Cutout Setting for R-134a System

**Given (typical marine AC):**
- Maximum design condensing temperature: $T_{cond,max} = 55°C$
- Atmospheric pressure at sea level: $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$

**Objective:** Compute the HP cutout gauge setting.

**Step 1:** Identify the safety margin.
Engineers add a **5 °C** safety margin above the design condensing temperature to ensure the cutout triggers before the compressor discharge valves reach their mechanical/thermal limit.

$$T_{set} = T_{cond,max} + 5°C = 55 + 5 = 60°C$$

**Step 2:** Read the saturation pressure of R-134a at 60 °C from refrigerant tables.
$$P_{sat,R134a}(60°C) \approx 16.83 \text{ bar (abs)}$$

**Step 3:** Convert absolute pressure to gauge pressure (because the switch is a pressure gauge).
$$P_{HP,cutout} = P_{sat} - P_{atm}$$
$$P_{HP,cutout} = 16.83 - 1.01325$$
$$P_{HP,cutout} \approx 15.82 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 4:** Add a small switch tolerance (typically ± 0.5 bar for industrial cutouts) — final setting is rounded to nearest 0.5 bar.
$$P_{HP,set,final} \approx 16.0 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Final Answer:** Set the HP cutout at **16 barg** for this plant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of LP Cutout Setting for the Same Plant

**Given:**
- Minimum design evaporating temperature: $T_{evap,min} = 0°C$
- Safety margin: **−3 °C** (cutout triggers 3 °C below min evap to prevent frost)

**Step 1:** Compute the trigger saturation temperature.
$$T_{trigger} = T_{evap,min} - 3°C = 0 - 3 = -3°C$$

**Step 2:** Read R-134a saturation pressure at −3 °C from tables.
$$P_{sat,R134a}(-3°C) \approx 2.473 \text{ bar (abs)}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to gauge.
$$P_{LP,cutout} = 2.473 - 1.01325$$
$$P_{LP,cutout} \approx 1.46 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 4:** Round down (because we want to trip on falling pressure, not allow it to drop lower).
$$P_{LP,set,final} \approx 1.4 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Final Answer:** Set the LP cutout at **1.4 barg**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Oil Pressure Failure Switch (OPFS) Setting

**Given (screw compressor):**
- Design crankcase oil pressure: $P_{oil,design} = 5.5 \text{ bar (gauge)}$
- Required oil-pressure differential above suction: $\Delta P_{req} = 2.0 \text{ bar}$
- Suction pressure at design: $P_{suc,design} = 2.5 \text{ bar (gauge)}$

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute crankcase oil pressure required.
$$P_{oil,set} = P_{suc,design} + \Delta P_{req}$$
$$P_{oil,set} = 2.5 + 2.0 = 4.5 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard "trip after time delay" rule. The OPFS has a **45-second** time delay on starting to allow oil pressure to build up, and an **instantaneous** trip on running if differential falls below setting.

**Step 3:** Final setting to be applied on the differential gauge.
$$\Delta P_{OPFS,set} = 2.0 \text{ bar}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Motor Overload Relay (OLR) Setting

**Given:**
- Compressor motor Full Load Current (FLC): $I_{FLC} = 45 \text{ A}$
- Service Factor of motor: $SF = 1.15$
- Ambient temperature correction for engine room: $K_{temp} = 0.94$ (derate for 50 °C ambient)

**Step 1:** Compute the minimum allowable trip current.
$$I_{trip,min} = 1.10 \times I_{FLC}$$
$$I_{trip,min} = 1.10 \times 45 = 49.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the maximum allowable trip current (before motor damage).
$$I_{trip,max} = SF \times I_{FLC} \times K_{temp}$$
$$I_{trip,max} = 1.15 \times 45 \times 0.94 \approx 48.6 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Check feasibility — $I_{trip,min} > I_{trip,max}$, indicating the motor is **undersized** for the application. Action: select a motor of next higher frame size (e.g., 55 A FLC) and recompute.

**Step 4 (corrected):** With $I_{FLC} = 55 \text{ A}$:
$$I_{trip,min} = 1.10 \times 55 = 60.5 \text{ A}$$
$$I_{trip,max} = 1.15 \times 55 \times 0.94 \approx 59.5 \text{ A}$$

Still infeasible — select next size up. This is exactly the type of calculation KTU examiners love to test because it demonstrates the **derating philosophy** of engine-room equipment.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Setting Ranges at a Glance (R-134a / R-407C)

| Protection Device | R-134a Setting | R-407C Setting | Engine Analogue | Typical Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HP Cutout | 14 – 17 barg | 22 – 26 barg | — | ± 0.5 bar |
| LP Cutout | 1.2 – 1.8 barg | 2.5 – 3.5 barg | — | ± 0.2 bar |
| OPFS ΔP | 1.8 – 2.1 bar | 1.8 – 2.1 bar | Lube-oil pressure trip | ± 0.2 bar |
| Discharge Temp. | 120 – 135 °C | 120 – 135 °C | Jacket outlet trip | ± 5 °C |
| Frost Stat | −1 to +3 °C | −1 to +3 °C | — | ± 1 °C |
| OLR Setting | 1.10 × FLC | 1.10 × FLC | 1.10 × FLC | ± 5 % |
| Overspeed Trip | — | — | 1.10 × N_rated | Hydraulic ± 2 % |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Marine AC Plant Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER["Power Supply Side"]
        Mains[3-Phase Mains Supply]
        MCB[Molded Case Circuit Breaker]
        PhSeq[Phase Sequence Relay]
        PhFail[Phase Failure Relay]
        EFR[Earth Fault Relay]
        OLR[Overload Relay 1.10 x FLC]
        ASCT[Anti-Short-Cycle Timer 3 to 6 min]
    end

    subgraph COMPRESSOR["Compressor Side"]
        CompMtr[Compressor Motor]
        ScrewComp[Screw / Scroll Compressor]
    end

    subgraph REF_CKT["Refrigerant Circuit Protection"]
        HPSW[High Pressure Cutout 16 barg]
        LPSW[Low Pressure Cutout 1.4 barg]
        OPFS[Oil Pressure Failure Switch dP 2.0 bar]
        DiscT[Discharge Temp. Cutout 130 C]
        FstStat[Frost Stat 0 to 3 C]
    end

    subgraph AIRSIDE["Airside Protection"]
        FiltDP[Filter Dp Switch 250 Pa]
        Smoke[Smoke Detector in Return Air]
        FireDamp[Fire Damper 72 C Fusible Link]
    end

    subgraph COOLING["Cooling Water Side"]
        CWF[Condenser Water Flow Switch]
        CWT[Condenser Water Temp Sensor]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["Engine Protection"]
        LOP[Low Lube Oil Pressure Trip 1.5 bar]
        LOT[High Lube Oil Temp Trip 85 C]
        CWT2[High Jacket CW Temp Trip 95 C]
        OSP[Overspeed Trip 1.10 x N]
        CRV[Crankcase Relief Valve 0.05 bar]
        AirRes[Starting Air Safety Valve 30 bar]
    end

    subgraph ALARM["Alarm and Shutdown Panel"]
        AlmAud[Audible Alarm]
        AlmVis[Visual Alarm LED]
        Trip[Compressor and Engine Trip Coil]
        Logger[Data Logger / Black Box]
    end

    Mains --> MCB --> PhSeq --> PhFail --> EFR --> OLR --> ASCT --> CompMtr --> ScrewComp
    ScrewComp --> HPSW
    ScrewComp --> LPSW
    ScrewComp --> OPFS
    ScrewComp --> DiscT
    ScrewComp --> FstStat
    ScrewComp --> CWF
    ScrewComp --> CWT
    FiltDP --> AlmAud
    Smoke --> AlmAud
    Smoke --> FireDamp
    FireDamp --> Trip
    HPSW --> Trip
    LPSW --> Trip
    OPFS --> Trip
    DiscT --> Trip
    CWF --> Trip
    LOT --> Trip
    CWT2 --> Trip
    LOP --> Trip
    OSP --> Trip
    CRV -.relieves.-> ScrewComp
    AirRes -.relieves.-> Engine
    Trip --> Logger
    AlmVis --> Logger
```

### 4.2 Functional Logic — Why Protection Devices Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parameter Exceeds Setpoint] --> B{Is Time Delay Built In?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Wait for Delay 5 to 60 s]
    B -- No --> D[Instantaneous Trip]
    C --> E{Still Out of Range?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Alarm Stage 1 Audible]
    F --> G[Operator Acknowledge within 30 s]
    G --> H{Parameter Recovered?}
    H -- No --> I[Trip Stage 2 Compressor OFF]
    H -- Yes --> J[Auto Reset]
    I --> K[Manual Reset after Investigation]
    D --> I
    K --> L[Log to Black Box]
    J --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> This two-stage trip logic — **"Alarm before Trip"** — is mandated by classification societies for engine-room machinery. The alarm gives the operator a chance to intervene; only if he fails to act does the system shut down the equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any **six** essential protection devices fitted on a marine air-conditioning refrigeration compressor, stating the function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **High-Pressure Cutout** — Trips compressor when discharge pressure exceeds safe limit (~16 barg for R-134a), preventing compressor-valve damage.
2. **Low-Pressure Cutout** — Trips compressor on falling suction pressure (~1.4 barg), indicating loss of refrigerant charge.
3. **Oil Pressure Failure Switch (OPFS)** — Trips if lube-oil differential pressure drops below setpoint (~2.0 bar), preventing bearing failure.
4. **Discharge Temperature Cutout** — Trips at ~130 °C to prevent lubricant breakdown and valve-plate burnout.
5. **Frost Stat** — Trips at evaporator outlet temperature near 0 °C to prevent ice formation that blocks airflow.
6. **Anti-Short-Cycle Timer (ASCT)** — Enforces minimum OFF time (~5 min) between successive starts to equalise pressures and protect the motor from locked-rotor current.

*(Any six valid devices with correct function = full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the purpose and operating principle of a **crankcase explosion relief valve** on a marine diesel engine.*

**Model Answer:**

The crankcase explosion relief valve is a spring-loaded or weight-loaded disc valve fitted on the crankcase of large marine diesel engines. Its purpose is to **safely vent any sudden pressure rise** inside the crankcase caused by the ignition of accumulated oil mist (a phenomenon known as **"crankcase explosion"** or **"oil-mist explosion"**). 

**Operating principle:** Under normal conditions, the valve remains closed, held by a calibrated spring or weight set to open at a very low differential pressure — typically **+0.05 to +0.10 bar** above atmospheric. If an oil-mist deflagration begins inside the crankcase, the pressure spike rapidly exceeds this setting; the disc lifts instantly, vents the hot gases and flame to atmosphere (or to a safe discharge duct), and the pressure is relieved before the crankcase structure fails catastrophically. The valve re-closes by spring action once pressure normalises. On slow-speed marine main engines, multiple valves (often 4–8) are fitted on each crankcase door. They are a mandatory requirement under SOLAS Ch. II-2 and IACS UR M10.

*(Purpose 1 mark + principle 1 mark + setting & regulatory reference 1 mark = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** of the following (either Q.A or Q.B). Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete protection scheme** of a marine screw-type refrigeration compressor using refrigerant R-134a. Cover at least **eight** protection devices, classifying them under *electrical, refrigerant-side, airside and cooling-water* categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine AC plant uses R-134a with the following design data:
- Maximum condensing temperature: **50 °C**
- Minimum evaporating temperature: **+2 °C**
- Compressor motor FLC: **38 A**
- Suction pressure at design: **3.2 bar (gauge)**

Calculate:
(i) HP cutout setting (gauge)
(ii) LP cutout setting (gauge)
(iii) OLR trip current setting
(iv) OPFS differential pressure setting

Saturation pressures of R-134a: at 55 °C → **14.96 bara**; at −1 °C → **2.13 bara**; at 50 °C → **13.18 bara**; at 2 °C → **3.32 bara**. Atmospheric pressure = **1.013 bar**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Description (7 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for the architecture. The student should produce a hand-drawn equivalent on the answer script covering:

| Category | Devices |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Protection** (2 marks) | MCB, Phase-Sequence Relay, Phase-Failure Relay, OLR (set at 1.10 × FLC), Earth-Fault Relay, Anti-Short-Cycle Timer |
| **Refrigerant-Side Protection** (2 marks) | HP Cutout, LP Cutout, OPFS, Discharge-Temperature Cutout, Frost Stat, Liquid-Line Solenoid |
| **Airside Protection** (1.5 marks) | Filter-ΔP Switch, Return-Air Smoke Detector, Fire Damper (72 °C fusible link) |
| **Cooling-Water Side** (1.5 marks) | Condenser Flow Switch, Condenser Outlet Temperature Sensor, Sea-Water Inlet Pressure Switch |

Full marks require neat block diagram + correct device-function pairing.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) HP cutout (gauge) — 2 marks**

$$T_{set} = T_{cond,max} + 5°C = 50 + 5 = 55°C$$

$$P_{sat,R134a}(55°C) = 14.96 \text{ bara}$$

$$P_{HP,cutout} = 14.96 - 1.013 = 13.95 \text{ barg}$$

$$\boxed{P_{HP,cutout} \approx 14.0 \text{ barg}}$$

**(ii) LP cutout (gauge) — 2 marks**

$$T_{trigger} = T_{evap,min} - 3°C = 2 - 3 = -1°C$$

$$P_{sat,R134a}(-1°C) = 2.13 \text{ bara}$$

$$P_{LP,cutout} = 2.13 - 1.013 = 1.12 \text{ barg}$$

$$\boxed{P_{LP,cutout} \approx 1.1 \text{ barg}}$$

**(iii) OLR setting — 1.5 marks**

$$I_{trip} = 1.10 \times I_{FLC} = 1.10 \times 38$$

$$\boxed{I_{trip} = 41.8 \text{ A}}$$

**(iv) OPFS differential — 1.5 marks**

For a screw compressor the standard differential is **2.0 bar**, applied via the differential gauge between crankcase and suction.

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{OPFS} = 2.0 \text{ bar}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **engine protection systems** fitted on a marine main diesel engine. Cover lube-oil, jacket cooling-water, fuel-oil, starting-air and overspeed protection with **typical trip/alarm setpoints**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine generator diesel engine has the following nameplate data:
- Rated speed: **N = 750 rpm**
- Lube-oil pressure (normal): **4.2 bar**
- Lube-oil pressure (trip): **1.5 bar**
- Jacket-water outlet temperature (normal): **78 °C**; trip: **95 °C**
- Starting-air reservoir working pressure: **25 bar**; safety-valve set: **30 bar**

Calculate:
(i) Overspeed trip speed
(ii) Crankcase relief-valve opening pressure (in bar gauge and kPa)
(iii) If the reservoir is filled to 30 bar and a leak of 0.5 bar/min is observed, how many minutes of starting reserve remain before the low-pressure alarm at 18 bar triggers?
(iv) Comment on whether the LP-alarm reserve is sufficient for a vessel with Class requirement of "three consecutive starts" where each start consumes 4 bar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Engine Protection Description (7 marks):**

Lube-Oil Protection (1.5 marks): Low-pressure trip at **1.2–1.8 bar** (with 30-second time delay on starting); high-temperature alarm at **75 °C**, trip at **85 °C**; filter ΔP alarm at **0.35 bar**.

Jacket Cooling-Water Protection (1.5 marks): High outlet-temperature alarm at **85 °C**, trip at **95 °C**; low-flow switch; high ΔT alarm between cylinder heads indicating blocked passages.

Fuel-Oil Protection (1 mark): Heavy-fuel-oil (HFO) viscosity control at **12–18 cSt** at the inlet; high-temperature alarm at **150 °C**; low-pressure alarm before the booster pump.

Starting-Air Protection (1 mark): LP alarm at **18 bar** to auto-start auxiliary compressor; safety valve on reservoir set at **30 bar** (working pressure 25 bar); flame arrestor in the crankcase breather.

Overspeed Protection (1 mark): Hydraulic or mechanical overspeed trip at **110 % of rated speed**, mandatory under IACS UR M3.1 and SOLAS.

Fire Detection (1 mark): Smoke + heat + flame detectors in the engine room linked to the fixed fire-fighting system (CO₂ / NOVEC / water mist).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Overspeed trip — 1.5 marks**

$$N_{trip} = 1.10 \times N_{rated} = 1.10 \times 750$$

$$\boxed{N_{trip} = 825 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Crankcase relief valve setting — 1.5 marks**

Standard crankcase relief-valve setting: **+0.05 to +0.10 bar** above atmospheric; take mid-value.

$$P_{CRV} = 0.075 \text{ barg}$$

Convert to kPa:
$$P_{CRV} = 0.075 \times 100 = 7.5 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

$$\boxed{P_{CRV} \approx 0.075 \text{ barg} \;\; (\approx 7.5 \text{ kPa})}$$

**(iii) Time to LP alarm at 18 bar — 2 marks**

Leak rate = 0.5 bar/min; pressure drop required = 30 − 18 = **12 bar**.

$$t = \frac{\Delta P}{\text{Leak rate}} = \frac{12 \text{ bar}}{0.5 \text{ bar/min}}$$

$$\boxed{t = 24 \text{ minutes}}$$

**(iv) Three-start reserve check — 2 marks**

Each start consumes **4 bar**; three consecutive starts consume **12 bar**.

Available pressure at full charge = **30 bar**.
Residual pressure after 3 starts = 30 − 12 = **18 bar** → which is exactly the LP-alarm trigger pressure.

**Comment:** The reservoir is **just barely sufficient** — at the third start the LP alarm will sound. Class requirement is "three consecutive starts" *without* alarm, so the engineer should **recharge to 30 bar before each starting cycle** or maintain at least **22 bar** before a critical start. Marginal design — recommend raising working pressure to **30 bar** with alarm at 22 bar for safety margin.

$$\boxed{\text{Margin = 0 bar; design is marginal, recommend re-evaluation.}}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic (from KTU valuation key patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing bara vs barg.** Many students write the HP cutout as "16 bar" without specifying **gauge**. Examiner deducts ½ mark immediately. Always write "16 barg" or "16 bar (gauge)".
> 2. **Forgetting the 5 °C safety margin** when computing HP cutout from design condensing temperature. This is a deliberate KTU trap — full 2 marks lost if omitted.
> 3. **Mixing up anti-short-cycle timer** with time-delay relay. The ASCT enforces minimum *OFF* time after a stop, not start delay.
> 4. **Writing "frost thermostat" instead of "frost stat" or "ice-stat".** Both names accepted, but consistency matters.
> 5. **Not mentioning the two-stage trip logic** (alarm → operator response → trip). Examiners specifically award 1 mark for this in 7-mark descriptions.
> 6. **Confusing crankcase relief-valve setting** with safety-valve setting on the starting-air reservoir (30 bar). They are completely different — the crankcase relief is at ~0.075 bar, the air-reservoir safety is at 30 bar.
> 7. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU deducts ½ mark per missing unit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HP cutout setting =** saturation pressure at **(T_cond,max + 5 °C)** converted to barg.
- ✅ **LP cutout setting =** saturation pressure at **(T_evap,min − 3 °C)** converted to barg.
- ✅ **OPFS differential for screw compressor =** **1.8 – 2.1 bar** (crankcase vs suction).
- ✅ **OLR setting =** **1.10 × FLC** (always state the multiplier).
- ✅ **Anti-Short-Cycle Timer (ASCT) =** **3 to 6 minutes** minimum OFF-time.
- ✅ **Frost stat range =** **−1 °C to +3 °C** at evaporator outlet.
- ✅ **Fire damper fusible link =** **72 °C**.
- ✅ **Filter ΔP alarm =** **250 Pa** across filter bank.
- ✅ **Engine overspeed trip =** **1.10 × N_rated** (mandatory per IACS UR M3.1).
- ✅ **Crankcase explosion relief valve =** opens at **+0.05 to +0.10 barg** (≈ 5–10 kPa).
- ✅ **Jacket cooling-water trip =** **95 °C**; alarm at **85 °C**.
- ✅ **Lube-oil pressure trip =** **1.2 – 1.8 bar** (with 30 s start delay).
- ✅ **Starting-air reservoir safety valve =** **30 bar** for **25 bar** working.
- ✅ **Starting-air LP alarm =** **18 bar** (auto-start auxiliary compressor).
- ✅ **Discharge temperature cutout =** **120 – 135 °C**.
- ✅ **Two-stage trip logic =** Alarm first → operator response time 30 s → Trip if not corrected.
- ✅ **Always specify bara vs barg** — examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ **All marine AC & engine protection devices must be type-approved** by classification society (DNV / Lloyd's / ABS / IRS).
- ✅ **The protection philosophy is "fail-safe"** — devices must fail to the *safe* state (normally-closed contacts that open on trip).
- ✅ **Alarm acknowledgement is logged in the engine-room log book** as required by ISM Code.
- ✅ **Sea trials must verify every protection device** — this is a mandatory statutory requirement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Overview of Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Overview of Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle that produces a cooling effect by utilizing a secondary working fluid (the **absorbent/refrigerant pair**) instead of mechanical shaft work. The system uses a thermal energy input at the **generator** to separate the refrigerant from the absorbent, after which the high-pressure refrigerant vapour is condensed, expanded, and evaporated to absorb heat at low temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** which consumes *mechanical/electrical work* at the compressor, the VARS consumes primarily *thermal energy* (heat) at the generator and a small amount of *pump work*. This makes it ideal when waste heat or solar energy is abundantly available.

The most common refrigerant-absorbent (working pair) combinations are:

| Working Pair | Refrigerant | Absorbent | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aqua-Ammonia | NH₃ (Ammonia) | H₂O (Water) | Industrial refrigeration below 0 °C |
| Lithium Bromide | H₂O (Water) | LiBr (Lithium Bromide) | Air-conditioning applications (above 0 °C) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Sponge & Bucket" Refrigerator**
>
> Imagine a kitchen sponge soaked with water sitting in a sealed bucket.
> - The **sponge (absorbent)** has a strong natural attraction for the **water (refrigerant)**.
> - If you **heat the sponge** over a stove (the *generator*), the water boils off as steam, leaving the sponge dry.
> - The steam is cooled on a cold plate (*condenser*) and collected as liquid water.
> - This liquid is then sprayed into an empty, warm bucket (*evaporator*), where it **evaporates**, absorbing heat from the surroundings — making the bucket cold!
> - The dry sponge is then transported back (*pump + absorber*) to absorb this newly evaporated water, and the cycle continues.
>
> The "heat from the stove" is analogous to the generator heat in a VARS — **no electricity-driven compressor is needed!**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Boiling Point of Ammonia (NH₃):** $-33.34 \; °\text{C}$ at 1 atm (ideal for low-temp refrigeration)
- **Boiling Point of Water (H₂O):** $100 \; °\text{C}$ at 1 atm
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of NH₃:** $\approx 1377 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of H₂O:** $\approx 2260 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** Dimensionless ratio of useful cooling to energy input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The VARS operates on a continuous closed-loop cycle. The thermal energy flow can be broken down into the following sequential logic steps:

1. **Low-pressure refrigerant vapour** enters the **Absorber** where it is absorbed by the weak solution (lean in refrigerant), forming a **strong solution** (rich in refrigerant).
2. The **solution pump** raises the pressure of this strong solution from absorber pressure to generator pressure. The pump work $W_p$ is very small compared to the heat input.
3. The high-pressure strong solution enters the **Generator (Boiler)**, where external heat $Q_G$ is supplied. This heat causes the refrigerant to vaporize and separate from the absorbent.
4. The refrigerant vapour may pass through a **Rectifier** to remove any absorbent traces before entering the **Condenser**.
5. In the **Condenser**, the high-pressure vapour rejects heat $Q_C$ to the surroundings and condenses to a high-pressure liquid.
6. The high-pressure liquid refrigerant passes through the **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)**, where its pressure and temperature drop drastically.
7. The low-pressure, low-temperature liquid refrigerant enters the **Evaporator**, where it absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the cooled space and evaporates, completing the refrigerant loop.
8. The weak solution (lean in refrigerant) from the generator returns to the absorber through a **Pressure Reducing Valve** to repeat the cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is VARS preferred over VCRS in some cases?**
> - It can run on **waste heat, solar energy, geothermal energy, or natural gas**.
> - **No moving parts** in the main refrigerant circuit (only a small liquid pump).
> - **Quiet operation** — ideal for libraries, hospitals, and residential buildings.
> - **COP is lower** than VCRS, but free/low-grade heat offsets this drawback.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Definition | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | Actual COP of VARS (cooling effect ÷ total energy input) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,ideal}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G}$ | Ideal COP ignoring negligible pump work | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$ | Maximum theoretical (Carnot-based) COP of VARS | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ | Carnot COP between evaporator and condenser temperatures | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_E = m_r \cdot (h_{fg})_{\text{ref}}$ | Cooling effect produced in evaporator | kW |
| 6 | $W_p = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_p}$ | Pump work (volume flow × pressure rise) | kW |
| 7 | $h_{f4} = h_{f3}$ | Throttling process (isenthalpic) in expansion valve | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $h_{f1} = h_{f4}$ | Throttling process in solution pressure-reducing valve | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\text{COP}_{\text{real}} = 0.4 \;\text{to}\; 0.6 \times \text{COP}_{\text{max}}$ | Typical actual COP range for practical VARS | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{real}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{max}}}$ | Thermal efficiency / Second-law efficiency of VARS | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Temperature Symbols (used universally in KTU problems):**
> - $T_E$ = Evaporator (cold space) temperature in **K**
> - $T_C$ = Condenser temperature in **K**
> - $T_G$ = Generator temperature in **K**
> - $T_A$ = Absorber temperature in **K**
>
> **Always convert °C to Kelvin** by adding $273.15 \; \text{K}$ before substitution.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of VARS in Engineering

- **Solar Air Conditioning:** Roof-mounted LiBr–H₂O chillers driven by solar thermal collectors (zero grid electricity for cooling).
- **Waste Heat Recovery:** Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants where exhaust gases drive ammonia absorption chillers.
- **Industrial Refrigeration:** Large cold-storage warehouses using ammonia-water systems.
- **Domestic Refrigerators:** Gas-operated absorption refrigerators (popular in RVs and remote areas).
- **District Cooling:** Centralized plants serving multiple buildings using steam-driven absorption chillers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum COP of VARS (Theoretical Limit)

We model the VARS as the **combined operation of a Refrigerator (Evaporator–Condenser) and a Heat Engine (Generator–Absorber)**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Equivalent Heat Engine

Consider the **Generator, Absorber, Pump, and Pressure-Reducing Valve** as forming a reversed heat engine that takes in heat $Q_G$ at temperature $T_G$, rejects heat $Q_A$ at temperature $T_A$, and delivers work equal to the pump work $W_p$.

For a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between $T_G$ and $T_A$:

$$
\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Q_A = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Energy Balance to the Heat Engine

The work output of this equivalent heat engine (which equals pump work $W_p$) is:

$$
W_p = Q_G - Q_A = Q_G - Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
W_p = Q_G \left( 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G} \right) = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Equivalent Refrigerator

The **Evaporator, Condenser, and the work input $W_p$** together constitute a reversed Carnot cycle (a refrigerator).

For a Carnot refrigerator operating between $T_E$ (cold) and $T_C$ (hot):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_p} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Two Cycles

Substituting the expression for $W_p$ from Step 2:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{W_p \cdot \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}}{Q_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{W_p}{Q_G}
$$

Substituting $W_p / Q_G = (T_G - T_A) / T_G$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first term $\dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ is the Carnot refrigerator COP. The second term $\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$ is the **fraction of generator heat converted into useful work**. Since $W_p$ is much smaller than $Q_G$, the overall COP of a VARS is **always less than a comparable VCRS**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Vapour Compression (VCRS) | Vapour Absorption (VARS) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Thermal energy (generator) |
| Moving parts in main loop | Yes (compressor) | No (only solution pump) |
| Working fluid | Single refrigerant | Refrigerant + Absorbent pair |
| COP range | 2.5 to 6.0 | 0.4 to 1.2 |
| Noise level | High (mechanical) | Very low (quiet) |
| Maintenance | Moderate (moving parts) | Low |
| Use of waste heat | No | Yes |
| Size & weight | Compact for same capacity | Bulky |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Ideal fuel | Electricity | Natural gas / solar / waste heat |

## 3.3 Maximum COP for a Practical Aqua-Ammonia System

**Problem Statement:** A vapour absorption refrigeration system operates with an evaporator at $-10 \; °\text{C}$, condenser at $40 \; °\text{C}$, generator at $120 \; °\text{C}$, and absorber at $30 \; °\text{C}$. Calculate the maximum possible COP.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.

$$
T_E = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_C = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_G = 120 + 273.15 = 393.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_A = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the maximum COP formula.

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} \cdot \frac{393.15 - 303.15}{393.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} \cdot \frac{90.00}{393.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 5.263 \times 0.2289
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 1.205\;}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

- The theoretical maximum COP is **1.205**, meaning every kW of heat supplied at the generator can produce **1.205 kW of cooling**.
- In practical systems, the actual COP is typically **40% to 60%** of this maximum, so the expected real COP is between **0.48 and 0.72**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System

The diagram below shows the **complete refrigerant and solution flow** through the major components. The two loops are clearly distinguished: the **refrigerant loop** (red arrows) and the **solution loop** (blue arrows).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START Refrigerant Vapour Low Pressure Low Temperature] --> nodeB[ABSORBER Absorbs Vapour into Weak Solution Forms Strong Solution]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SOLUTION PUMP Raises Pressure from Absorber to Generator Pressure Small Pump Work Wp]
    nodeC --> nodeD[GENERATOR External Heat QG Supplied Refrigerant Boils Off from Strong Solution]
    nodeD --> nodeE[RECTIFIER Removes Absorbent Traps from Vapour]
    nodeE --> nodeF[CONDENSER High Pressure Vapour Rejects Heat QC Condenses to Liquid]
    nodeF --> nodeG[EXPANSION VALVE Throttling Process Pressure and Temperature Drop]
    nodeG --> nodeH[EVAPORATOR Low Pressure Liquid Absorbs Heat QE from Cold Space Evaporates]
    nodeH --> nodeA
    
    nodeD -. Weak Solution Returns .-> nodeI[PRESSURE REDUCING VALVE Throttling of Solution]
    nodeI -.-> nodeB
    
    nodeJ[INPUTS QG Heat at Generator Wp Pump Work] -. Feeds .-> nodeD
    nodeK[OUTPUTS QE Useful Cooling at Evaporator QC Heat Rejected at Condenser QA Heat Rejected at Absorber] -. Extracted .-> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For deeper understanding, the **functional architecture** below illustrates how each component interacts energetically with the surroundings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputHeat[Thermal Energy Input QG from Waste Heat or Solar] --> genBlock[GENERATOR Separates Refrigerant from Absorbent]
    genBlock -->|Refrigerant Vapour| rectBlock[RECTIFIER Purification Stage]
    rectBlock --> condBlock[CONDENSER Rejects Heat to Atmosphere]
    condBlock -->|Liquid Refrigerant| expBlock[EXPANSION VALVE Pressure Drop]
    expBlock --> evapBlock[EVAPORATOR Absorbs Heat from Cold Space]
    evapBlock -->|Low Pressure Vapour| absBlock[ABSORBER Recombines with Weak Solution]
    absBlock -->|Strong Solution| pumpBlock[SOLUTION PUMP Pressure Boost]
    pumpBlock --> genBlock
    
    genBlock -->|Weak Solution| prvBlock[PRESSURE REDUCING VALVE]
    prvBlock --> absBlock
    
    evapBlock -. Produces .-> coolingOutput[Useful Cooling Effect QE]
    condBlock -. Rejects .-> condHeat[Waste Heat QC]
    absBlock -. Rejects .-> absHeat[Absorber Heat QA]
```

## 4.3 P-h (Pressure-Enthalpy) Schematic Description

Since a P-h diagram for the **refrigerant loop** of VARS is identical to that of VCRS, the four key points are:

| Point | Location | Pressure | Phase | Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaporator exit | Low | Saturated vapour | Absorption |
| 3 | Condenser exit | High | Saturated liquid | Condensation |
| 4 | Evaporator inlet | Low | Liquid-vapour mix | Throttling |
| 7 | After expansion | Low | Liquid-vapour mix | Evaporation begins |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** — *Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" (COP) for a refrigeration system. Why is the COP of an ideal VARS always lower than that of a VCRS operating between the same temperature limits?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** COP of a refrigeration system is the ratio of the desired cooling effect $Q_E$ to the net energy input. For VARS, $\text{COP} = Q_E / (Q_G + W_p)$, where $Q_G$ is the generator heat and $W_p$ is the pump work.
- **Reason for lower COP (2 Marks):** In VARS, the only work input is the small pump work $W_p$, which is much less than the heat input $Q_G$. However, since $Q_G$ is the denominator and it is **larger than $W_p$ alone** (which would be used in VCRS), the COP of VARS is mathematically lower. Essentially, only a fraction $(T_G - T_A)/T_G$ of the generator heat is converted into useful work, reducing the effective cooling per unit energy consumed.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** — *List any four major components of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Generator (1 Mark):** Supplies heat $Q_G$ to vaporize the refrigerant from the strong solution.
2. **Condenser (0.75 Marks):** Rejects heat $Q_C$ and condenses the high-pressure refrigerant vapour to liquid.
3. **Evaporator (0.75 Marks):** Produces cooling effect $Q_E$ by evaporating the low-pressure refrigerant.
4. **Absorber (0.5 Marks):** Recombines the refrigerant vapour with the weak solution to form a strong solution, completing the solution loop.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** before substituting into the COP formula. This alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula** (mixing up the VCRS Carnot COP with the VARS max COP formula).
> 3. **Not clearly stating assumptions** (e.g., reversible/ideal cycle, negligible pump work, throttling is isenthalpic).
> 4. **Skipping units** in intermediate steps — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Omitting a labelled diagram** — for VARS questions, always include the block diagram showing the two loops; this fetches 2 marks easily.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System** using ammonia-water as the working pair. Clearly identify the refrigerant loop and solution loop, and state the function of each component.

(b) **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **maximum COP of an ideal VARS** in terms of evaporator, condenser, generator, and absorber temperatures.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)*

The system consists of two interconnected loops:

**Refrigerant Loop (2 Marks):**
- Low-pressure ammonia vapour leaves the **Evaporator** (1) and enters the **Absorber** (2), where it dissolves in weak aqua-ammonia solution to form a strong solution.
- After being pumped to high pressure, the strong solution enters the **Generator** (3), where heat $Q_G$ vaporizes the ammonia.
- Ammonia vapour passes through the **Rectifier** (4) to remove water traces, then enters the **Condenser** (5), where it rejects heat $Q_C$ and condenses.
- The liquid ammonia is throttled through the **Expansion Valve** (6) and enters the **Evaporator** to absorb heat $Q_E$ from the cooled space.

**Solution Loop (1 Mark):** The weak solution from the generator returns to the absorber via a **pressure-reducing valve**.

**Functions (2 Marks):**
- **Generator:** Separates refrigerant using heat input.
- **Absorber:** Recombines refrigerant with absorbent, releasing absorption heat $Q_A$.
- **Rectifier:** Purifies refrigerant vapour.
- **Solution Pump:** Boosts solution pressure to generator level.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Equivalent Heat Engine (2 Marks):**
Consider the Generator-Absorber-Pump assembly as a heat engine operating between $T_G$ (source) and $T_A$ (sink).

$$
\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} \implies Q_A = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

**Step 2 — Pump Work (2 Marks):**

$$
W_p = Q_G - Q_A = Q_G - Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G} = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Refrigerator (1 Mark):**
The Evaporator-Condenser with work $W_p$ forms a Carnot refrigerator:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_p} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

**Step 4 — Combining (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{W_p}{Q_G} \cdot \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

Substituting $W_p / Q_G = (T_G - T_A) / T_G$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \left(\dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\right)\;}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Compare the **Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Systems** on the basis of energy input, working fluid, COP, moving parts, and applications.

(b) **[7 Marks]** An aqua-ammonia VARS operates with evaporator at $-15 \; °\text{C}$, condenser at $35 \; °\text{C}$, generator at $110 \; °\text{C}$, and absorber at $25 \; °\text{C}$. Calculate (i) the maximum theoretical COP, and (ii) the expected actual COP range assuming practical efficiency between 40% and 60%.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Basis | VCRS | VARS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Thermal energy (generator) + small pump work |
| Working fluid | Single refrigerant | Refrigerant-absorbent pair (NH₃–H₂O or LiBr–H₂O) |
| COP | 2.5 to 6.0 | 0.4 to 1.2 |
| Moving parts | Yes (compressor) | No (only solution pump) |
| Applications | Domestic refrigeration, AC, cold storage | Waste-heat recovery, solar cooling, large AC plants |

**Key Observations (2 Marks):**
- VCRS is more efficient but requires high-grade electrical energy.
- VARS can use low-grade/waste thermal energy, making it economically attractive where heat is cheap and abundant.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data (1 Mark):**

$$
T_E = -15 + 273.15 = 258.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_C = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_G = 110 + 273.15 = 383.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_A = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \; \text{K}
$$

**Part (i) — Maximum Theoretical COP (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{258.15}{308.15 - 258.15} \cdot \frac{383.15 - 298.15}{383.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{258.15}{50.00} \cdot \frac{85.00}{383.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 5.163 \times 0.2219
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 1.146\;}
$$

**Part (ii) — Actual COP Range (3 Marks):**

Lower bound (40% efficiency):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual,\,min}} = 0.40 \times 1.146 = 0.458
$$

Upper bound (60% efficiency):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual,\,max}} = 0.60 \times 1.146 = 0.688
$$

$$
\boxed{\;0.458 \leq \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \leq 0.688\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System**

- ✅ **VARS uses heat, not mechanical work**, as its primary energy input at the **generator**.
- ✅ The cycle has **two loops**: the **refrigerant loop** and the **solution loop**.
- ✅ The **maximum COP formula** is: $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$
- ✅ Always convert **°C → K** by adding $273.15$ before any calculation.
- ✅ The four temperatures are arranged as: $T_E < T_A < T_C < T_G$.
- ✅ **Aqua-Ammonia (NH₃–H₂O)** is used for refrigeration **below 0 °C**; **Lithium Bromide (LiBr–H₂O)** is used for **A/C above 0 °C**.
- ✅ The **solution pump work $W_p$** is very small compared to $Q_G$ — often neglected in ideal-cycle analysis.
- ✅ Throttling in both the **expansion valve** and **pressure-reducing valve** is **isenthalpic** ($h_{\text{in}} = h_{\text{out}}$).
- ✅ COP of VARS is **always lower** than VCRS for the same temperature limits, but VARS is preferred when **waste/solar heat** is available.
- ✅ Typical practical efficiency: actual COP = **40% to 60%** of theoretical COP_max.
- ✅ **No moving parts** in the main refrigerant circuit → quiet, low-maintenance operation.
- ✅ Common KTU exam trap: students forget to multiply the Carnot COP term by the heat-engine efficiency term $\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outside and Inside Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outside and Inside Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Outside and Inside Design Conditions?

In any HVAC project, the engineer must first decide **two sets of reference weather states** before a single duct is drawn or a chiller is sized. These are the **Outside Design Conditions (ODC)** and the **Inside Design Conditions (IDC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outside Design Conditions (ODC):** The most severe combination of outdoor temperature, humidity, and air conditions that the HVAC system is expected to handle while still maintaining comfort inside the conditioned space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inside Design Conditions (IDC):** The target indoor environment — a specific combination of dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, air movement, and air cleanliness — that the system must maintain for the occupants' thermal comfort, health, and process requirements.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of designing an **HVAC system** like planning a **kitchen recipe for a large wedding banquet**:

- **Outside Design Conditions** are like asking: *"What is the worst possible weather on the wedding day?"* — you must be ready for the most extreme heat, the heaviest humidity, or the coldest morning, because you cannot control nature.
- **Inside Design Conditions** are like deciding: *"What temperature and ambience do the guests need to feel comfortable inside the hall?"* — a slightly cool room (around **24 °C**) with gentle air movement is what makes them enjoy the event.

If the chef prepares the kitchen only for "average weather," the food will spoil on a hot day and the guests will be uncomfortable. Likewise, an HVAC system designed only for average weather will fail in extreme summers. Hence, ODC and IDC form the **bookends of all HVAC load calculations**.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Bodies

The numerical values used in KTU 2024-aligned courses are drawn primarily from:

- **ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals (Chapter 14: Climatic Design Information)**
- **ISHRAE (Indian Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers) — Indian climatic data**
- **NBC (National Building Code of India) — 2016 Part 8 Section 3**
- **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) — for ventilation air quality standards**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For Kerala's tropical climate, the typical ODC used in industry is **35 °C DBT** and **28 °C WBT** for commercial buildings, with **IDC** of **24 ± 1 °C DBT** and **55 ± 5 % RH**.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Design Conditions

| Parameter | Symbol | Common Unit | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT | °C | Measures air sensible heat |
| Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT | °C | Measures air total (sensible + latent) heat |
| Relative Humidity | RH | % | Indicates moisture content of air |

These three values, along with atmospheric pressure, completely define the **psychrometric state** of air.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Outside Design Conditions (ODC) — Detailed Analysis

ODC is **NOT** the absolute hottest temperature ever recorded. Instead, it is a **statistically extreme value** that the system is expected to meet most of the time.

#### 2.1.1 Statistical Basis — Percentile Exceedance

ASHRAE defines design conditions using the concept of **percent exceedance** — the percentage of hours in a year during which the outdoor temperature **exceeds** the design value.

- **0.4 % exceedance** → 99.6 % of hours are cooler; used for **critical applications** (hospitals, server rooms, museums).
- **1 % exceedance** → most commonly used for **commercial comfort cooling** (offices, malls).
- **2 % exceedance** → used for **residential and budget-conscious** projects.
- **5 % exceedance** → used where occasional discomfort is acceptable.

For example, in **Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala**, the **1 % design DBT** is approximately **33 °C** and the **coincident WBT** is around **26.5 °C**.

#### 2.1.2 Mean Coincident Wet Bulb (MCWB) Concept

The **MCWB** is the average wet-bulb temperature that occurs at the same time as the design dry-bulb. It is **not** the maximum WBT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MCWB and not peak WBT?**
> Using the peak WBT would result in an over-sized cooling coil, raising capital cost unnecessarily. The MCWB is the *realistic* humidity that the system must dehumidify at peak load.

#### 2.1.3 Factors Influencing ODC Selection

- **Building type and criticality** — hospitals vs. warehouses
- **Local microclimate** — urban heat island effect, coastal proximity
- **Solar radiation intensity** — peak summer afternoons
- **Wind speed and direction** — affects natural ventilation options
- **Diurnal variation range** — daily temperature swing
- **Latitude and altitude** — affects air density (used in fan law corrections)
