### 2.2 Inside Design Conditions (IDC) — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Thermal Comfort Parameters

The **PMV–PPD model** (Fanger's model) adopted by ASHRAE Standard 55 defines comfort using six parameters:

1. **Air temperature** (DBT)
2. **Mean radiant temperature (MRT)**
3. **Air velocity**
4. **Relative humidity**
5. **Metabolic rate (Met)** — activity level of occupants
6. **Clothing insulation (Clo)** — what the occupants are wearing

#### 2.2.2 Recommended IDC for Various Applications

| Building Type | DBT (°C) | RH (%) | Air Velocity (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Offices (general) | 23 – 26 | 40 – 60 | 0.10 – 0.25 |
| Hospitals (wards) | 22 – 25 | 40 – 60 | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| Server rooms / Data centers | 20 – 22 | 40 – 55 | — |
| Museums / Archives | 20 – 22 | 45 – 55 | < 0.10 |
| Cleanrooms (ISO 7) | 21 – 23 | 30 – 50 | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| Retail / Shopping malls | 23 – 26 | 50 – 65 | 0.10 – 0.25 |
| Auditoriums | 22 – 24 | 50 – 60 | < 0.15 |

#### 2.2.3 Factors Influencing IDC Selection

- **Activity level (Met)**: A sleeping person (~0.7 Met) needs warmer air than a sportsman (~6 Met).
- **Clothing (Clo)**: A person in a winter suit (1.5 Clo) needs cooler air than one in a shirt (0.5 Clo).
- **Local health regulations**: Hospitals, operation theatres, pharmaceutical plants.
- **Process requirements**: Textile mills, paper storage, semiconductor fabs.
- **Acoustic constraints**: Auditoriums prefer lower air velocities.
- **Energy codes (ECBC in India)**: Set minimum efficiency and indoor quality.
- **Adaptive comfort model**: Allows wider temperature bands for naturally ventilated spaces.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{env} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer through building envelope | W or kW |
| 2 | $Q_{s,\text{person}} = N \cdot q_{s}$ | Sensible heat gain from occupants | W |
| 3 | $Q_{l,\text{person}} = N \cdot q_{l}$ | Latent heat gain from occupants | W |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{lights}} = W_{\text{lamp}} \cdot F_{u} \cdot F_{b}$ | Heat from lighting (with usage & ballast factors) | W |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{equipment}} = \sum P_{i} \cdot F_{u} \cdot F_{r}$ | Heat from electrical equipment | W |
| 6 | $Q_{v,s} = \dot{m}_{v} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Ventilation sensible heat | W |
| 7 | $Q_{v,l} = \dot{m}_{v} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Ventilation latent heat | W |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{v} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow of ventilation air | kg/s |
| 9 | $ET \approx T_{a} + f(v) \cdot (T_{a} - T_{w})$ | Effective Temperature (simplified) | °C |
| 10 | $\text{PMV} = f(M, W, I_{cl}, T_{a}, \bar{T}_{r}, v, p_{a})$ | Fanger's Predicted Mean Vote | dimensionless |

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient ($\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}$)
- $A$ = surface area ($\text{m}^{2}$)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between inside and outside (K)
- $N$ = number of occupants
- $q_{s}, q_{l}$ = sensible and latent heat per person (W)
- $\dot{m}_{v}$ = mass flow rate of ventilation air (kg/s)
- $c_{p}$ = specific heat of air (≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (≈ **2501 kJ/kg**)
- $\Delta W$ = humidity ratio difference (kg water/kg dry air)
- $\rho$ = density of air (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at standard conditions)
- $f(v)$ = velocity-dependent factor in ET equation

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

These conditions are not academic — they directly determine:

- **Chiller tonnage** in TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration, **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW**)
- **Boiler capacity** in kW for heating systems
- **Duct and pipe sizing**
- **Pump and fan power consumption**
- **Building Energy Performance Index (BEPI)** in kWh/m²/year

A wrong assumption in ODC/IDC can cause a **30–50 % deviation** in equipment sizing, leading to either wasteful over-investment or chronic under-performance of the HVAC system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: ODC vs. IDC Comparison

| Aspect | Outside Design Condition (ODC) | Inside Design Condition (IDC) |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Local meteorological data (IMD, ASHRAE) | Comfort theory + standards (ASHRAE 55, ISHRAE) |
| Frequency | Statistical (0.4 %, 1 %, 2 %) | Comfort band (PMV between −0.5 and +0.5) |
| Variability | Strongly climate-dependent | Largely building/use-dependent |
| Typical Kerala DBT | 33 – 35 °C | 23 – 26 °C |
| Typical Kerala RH | 60 – 85 % | 40 – 60 % |
| Purpose | Determine peak load | Determine setpoint for control |
| Effect of error | Wrong equipment sizing | Poor comfort or high energy use |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation : Selecting Design DBT for a Commercial Office in Kochi

**Problem Statement:**
A 10-storey commercial office building is to be air-conditioned in Kochi, Kerala. The building has a glazed façade on the south side. Determine the **design outdoor DBT and WBT** for the HVAC system, and the corresponding **indoor design conditions** following ASHRAE recommendations.

#### Step 1: Gather Climatic Data
From ASHRAE climatic design information for **Kochi (Lat 9.97 °N, Long 76.27 °E, Elevation 3 m AMSL)**:

$$
\text{Design DBT at } 1\% \text{ exceedance} = 33.0 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\text{Mean Coincident WBT (MCWB)} = 26.5 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\text{Daily Range} = 7.0 \, \text{K}
$$

#### Step 2: Adjust for Local Conditions
Since the building is in a dense urban area with significant **heat island effect**, add a correction:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{urban}} = +1.0 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\text{Adjusted ODC (DBT)} = 33.0 + 1.0 = 34.0 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

#### Step 3: Determine Outside Air Enthalpy
Using psychrometric relations, for air at **34 °C DBT** and **26.5 °C WBT** at sea level:

$$
p_{ws,\text{DBT}} = 5.32 \, \text{kPa} \quad \text{(saturation pressure at 34 °C)}
$$

$$
p_{ws,\text{WBT}} = 3.47 \, \text{kPa} \quad \text{(saturation pressure at 26.5 °C)}
$$

The wet-bulb relation gives humidity ratio:

$$
W = \frac{0.622 \cdot (p_{ws,\text{WBT}} - p_{\text{atm}} \cdot (T_{DBT} - T_{WBT}) \cdot A)}{p_{\text{atm}} - p_{ws,\text{WBT}}}
$$

With $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa and psychrometric constant $A = 0.00066$:

$$
W = \frac{0.622 \cdot (3.47 - 101.325 \cdot 7.5 \cdot 0.00066)}{101.325 - 3.47}
$$

$$
W = \frac{0.622 \cdot (3.47 - 0.5015)}{97.855} = \frac{0.622 \cdot 2.9685}{97.855} = 0.01886 \, \text{kg/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{W = 18.86 \, \text{g/kg of dry air}}
$$

#### Step 4: Select Inside Design Conditions
For a general office with sedentary work (1.2 Met) and summer clothing (0.5 Clo):

$$
\text{Recommended IDC: } T_{\text{in}} = 25 \pm 1 \, ^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{DBT}, \quad \text{RH} = 50 \pm 5 \%
$$

From psychrometric chart, at **25 °C DBT** and **50 % RH**:

$$
W_{\text{in}} \approx 9.9 \, \text{g/kg}
$$

$$
h_{\text{in}} \approx 50.5 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute ΔT for Heat Load Calculation

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} = 34.0 - 25.0 = 9.0 \, \text{K}
$$

#### Step 6: Compute Moisture Difference for Latent Load

$$
\Delta W = W_{\text{out}} - W_{\text{in}} = 18.86 - 9.90 = 8.96 \, \text{g/kg}
$$

#### Step 7: Final Design Parameters for Coil Selection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Entering Air (Return)} & : 25.0 \, ^\circ\text{C} \text{ DBT}, \, 50 \% \text{ RH} \\
\text{Entering Air (Outdoor)} & : 34.0 \, ^\circ\text\text{C DBT}, \, 26.5 \, ^\circ\text{C WBT} \\
\text{Mixed Air (30 \% OA)} & : \text{calculated at 27.7 °C DBT} \\
\text{Leaving Air at Coil} & : 13.0 \, ^\circ\text{C} \text{ DBT}, \, 95 \% \text{ RH (ADP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This becomes the basis for selecting the cooling coil **ADP (Apparatus Dew Point)** and the **Bypass Factor (BPF)**.

### 3.3 Verification Using Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR)

For the conditioned space:
- Sensible Heat Gain (SHG) = envelope + lights + people sensible + ventilation sensible
- Latent Heat Gain (LHG) = people latent + ventilation latent

$$
\text{SHR} = \frac{\text{SHG}}{\text{SHG} + \text{LHG}}
$$

A typical office in Kerala gives **SHR ≈ 0.7 to 0.8**. This ratio is critical for selecting the appropriate cooling coil that can handle both sensible and latent loads simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart : Selection of Design Conditions for an HVAC Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start HVAC Project] --> B{Identify Building Type}
    B -->|Residential| C[Use 2 percent ODC]
    B -->|Commercial| D[Use 1 percent ODC]
    B -->|Critical| E[Use 0.4 percent ODC]
    C --> F[Apply Urban Heat Island Correction]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Adjusted ODC DBT]
    G --> H[Lookup MCWB from ASHRAE]
    H --> I[Determine Inside Design DBT and RH]
    I --> J{Process Application?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Special IDC Standards]
    J -->|No| L[Use ASHRAE 55 Comfort Band]
    K --> M[Final Design Conditions Locked]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Proceed to Load Calculation]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram : Factors Influencing ODC and IDC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ODCFactors
        A1[Climate Data] --> A2[Local Latitude]
        A2 --> A3[Solar Radiation]
        A3 --> A4[Wind Patterns]
        A4 --> A5[Altitude and Pressure]
    end
    subgraph IDCFactors
        B1[Occupant Activity] --> B2[Clothing Insulation]
        B2 --> B3[Air Velocity]
        B3 --> B4[Radiant Asymmetry]
        B4 --> B5[Process Needs]
        B5 --> B6[Energy Code Limits]
    end
    ODCFactors --> X((Final HVAC Load))
    IDCFactors --> X
    X --> Y[Equipment Selection]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram : Stepwise Cooling Load Calculation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Final ODC and IDC] --> B[Envelope Heat Gain]
    A --> C[Internal Heat Gains]
    A --> D[Ventilation Load]
    B --> E[Sensible Heat Gain]
    C --> E
    C --> F[Latent Heat Gain]
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G[Total Cooling Load]
    F --> G
    G --> H[SHR Computation]
    H --> I[Chiller and AHU Sizing]
```

### 4.4 Concept Map : Comfort Triangle Linking ODC, IDC, and Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ODC[Outside Design Condition] -->|Drives| Load[Cooling and Heating Load]
    IDC[Inside Design Condition] -->|Determines| Setpoint[Room Setpoint]
    Load --> Equip[Equipment Capacity]
    Setpoint --> Equip
    Equip --> Energy[Annual Energy Use]
    Energy --> Cost[Operating Cost]
    Load --> Cost
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A : Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. (3 Marks) Define Outside Design Conditions (ODC) and Inside Design Conditions (IDC). Why is it essential to select these correctly before sizing HVAC equipment?**

**Model Answer:**

**Outside Design Conditions (ODC):** ODC refers to the extreme combination of outdoor temperature, humidity, and wind conditions that an HVAC system is designed to handle. It is statistically derived using percentile exceedance data (typically 0.4 %, 1 %, or 2 % of annual hours).

**Inside Design Conditions (IDC):** IDC refers to the target indoor environment — temperature, relative humidity, air movement, and cleanliness — that the system must maintain for human comfort or process requirements.

**Importance of correct selection:**
- ODC underestimation → chronic system under-performance during peak weather
- ODC overestimation → wasteful capital expenditure, oversized equipment
- IDC incorrect → poor thermal comfort or excessive energy bills
- Both together form the **boundary conditions for all load calculations** (sensible and latent), chiller sizing, duct design, and energy estimation.

*(3 marks: 1 for ODC definition, 1 for IDC definition, 1 for importance)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. (3 Marks) List the factors that influence the selection of Inside Design Conditions for a conditioned space.**

**Model Answer:**

The factors influencing IDC selection are:

1. **Activity level of occupants (Met)** — Sedentary office work (~1.2 Met) versus heavy industrial work (~4 Met).
2. **Clothing insulation (Clo)** — Light summer wear (~0.5 Clo) versus heavy winter clothing (~1.5 Clo).
3. **Air velocity** — Higher air movement allows higher setpoint temperature (Energy-saving strategy).
4. **Mean radiant temperature (MRT)** — Affects radiant heat exchange with surroundings.
5. **Process or application requirements** — Hospitals, museums, cleanrooms, server rooms have stricter conditions.
6. **Building codes and energy standards** — ECBC, ASHRAE 55, ISHRAE guidelines.
7. **Outdoor air ventilation rate** — Required for IAQ, affects load.

*(3 marks: roughly half-mark per major factor, capped at 6 factors)*

---

### 5.2 Part B : Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Outside Design Conditions in detail. Discuss the significance of mean coincident wet bulb temperature (MCWB) and the various statistical percentiles (0.4 %, 1 %, 2 %) used for ODC selection.**

**(b) [7 Marks] A commercial complex is to be built in Thiruvananthapuram. The design outdoor DBT at 1 % exceedance is 33 °C and MCWB is 26 °C. The recommended indoor conditions are 25 °C DBT and 50 % RH. Calculate the temperature difference (ΔT) and humidity ratio difference (ΔW) for HVAC load estimation. Use atmospheric pressure of 101.325 kPa.**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Definition:** ODC is the extreme combination of outdoor air parameters used as a reference for designing the cooling/heating load of an HVAC system.

**Statistical percentiles:**
- **0.4 % exceedance (≈ 35 hours/year):** Used for **critical** facilities (operating theatres, data centers, museums) where any failure is unacceptable.
- **1 % exceedance (≈ 88 hours/year):** The most widely used value for **general commercial comfort cooling** in India. Balances comfort and cost.
- **2 % exceedance (≈ 175 hours/year):** Used for **residential** and **budget-sensitive** projects where minor discomfort during a few peak hours is acceptable.

**MCWB Significance:**
- The WBT is **not independent** of DBT. On the day the peak DBT occurs, the WBT may not be at its absolute maximum.
- MCWB is the **average WBT that coincides with the design DBT** hours.
- Using MCWB avoids over-sizing the latent cooling capacity.
- Critical for selecting cooling coil **ADP** and **Bypass Factor (BPF)**.

**Example for Thiruvananthapuram:**
- 1 % DBT = 33 °C; MCWB = 26 °C; Daily range = 7 K
- Adding +1 K for urban heat island effect gives Adjusted ODC = 34 °C DBT, 26.5 °C MCWB.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for percentile explanation, 3 for MCWB concept)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- Outdoor DBT = 33 °C, MCWB = 26 °C
- Indoor DBT = 25 °C, RH = 50 %
- Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa

**Step 1: Temperature difference (ΔT)**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} = 33 - 25 = 8 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step 2: Saturation pressure at outdoor WBT (26 °C)**
From steam tables: $p_{ws}(26^\circ\text{C}) = 3.361 \, \text{kPa}$

**Step 3: Humidity ratio of outdoor air (using WBT relation)**

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \frac{0.622 \cdot (p_{ws,\text{WBT}} - A \cdot p_{\text{atm}} \cdot (T_{\text{DBT}} - T_{\text{WBT}}))}{p_{\text{atm}} - p_{ws,\text{WBT}}}
$$

Where $A = 0.00066$ (psychrometric constant for air-water at standard pressure):

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \frac{0.622 \cdot (3.361 - 0.00066 \cdot 101.325 \cdot 7)}{101.325 - 3.361}
$$

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \frac{0.622 \cdot (3.361 - 0.468)}{97.964}
$$

$$
W_{\text{out}} = \frac{0.622 \cdot 2.893}{97.964} = 0.01837 \, \text{kg/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Humidity ratio of indoor air (25 °C, 50 % RH)**
At 25 °C, $p_{ws} = 3.169$ kPa, so $p_{w} = 0.50 \times 3.169 = 1.585$ kPa

$$
W_{\text{in}} = \frac{0.622 \cdot 1.585}{101.325 - 1.585} = \frac{0.986}{99.74} = 0.00988 \, \text{kg/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Humidity ratio difference**

$$
\Delta W = W_{\text{out}} - W_{\text{in}} = 0.01837 - 0.00988 = 0.00849 \, \text{kg/kg} \approx 8.5 \, \text{g/kg}
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 8 \, \text{K}, \quad \Delta W = 8.49 \, \text{g/kg}}
$$

These values are used to compute the sensible and latent components of the cooling load.

*(7 marks: 2 for ΔT, 3 for $W_{\text{out}}$, 1 for $W_{\text{in}}$, 1 for ΔW)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the six parameters governing thermal comfort as per Fanger's PMV-PPD model. How do these parameters interact to define the comfort zone on the ASHRAE psychrometric chart?**

**(b) [7 Marks] A conference hall of 200 m² floor area and 100 occupants is to be conditioned. Indoor design: 24 °C DBT, 55 % RH. Outdoor design: 34 °C DBT, 60 % RH. Ventilation air supplied at 25 L/s per person. Calculate (i) the ventilation sensible load and (ii) the ventilation latent load.**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Fanger's PMV-PPD Model — Six Parameters:**

1. **Air Temperature (Tₐ):** The dry-bulb temperature of the surrounding air.
2. **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT, $\bar{T}_r$):** The uniform temperature of an imaginary enclosure in which radiant heat transfer from the human body equals that in the actual non-uniform environment.
3. **Air Velocity (v):** The mean velocity of air relative to the body, affecting convective heat loss.
4. **Relative Humidity (RH):** Influences evaporative heat loss from skin; affects latent cooling.
5. **Metabolic Rate (Met):** Heat produced by the body due to activity (1 Met = 58.2 W/m²). Sedentary office ≈ 1.2 Met; walking ≈ 2 Met.
6. **Clothing Insulation (Clo):** Thermal resistance of clothing (1 Clo = 0.155 m²·K/W). Summer wear ≈ 0.5 Clo; winter suit ≈ 1.0 Clo.

**Comfort Zone on ASHRAE Chart:**

- The comfort zone is plotted as a **polygon on the psychrometric chart** with DBT on x-axis and RH on y-axis.
- Boundaries shift based on season: **Summer zone** (23–26 °C, 30–60 % RH) versus **Winter zone** (20–24 °C, 30–60 % RH).
- For **PMV between −0.5 and +0.5**, 90 % of occupants feel comfortable.
- **Air velocity curves** overlay the chart — higher air velocity allows higher DBT for the same comfort.
- **MRT line** (often equal to DBT in well-insulated buildings) is a simplifying assumption.

The interaction of all six parameters defines an **envelope of acceptability**, not a single point.

*(7 marks: 1 per parameter = 6, 1 for chart concept)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- Floor area = 200 m², occupants = 100
- Indoor: 24 °C DBT, 55 % RH
- Outdoor: 34 °C DBT, 60 % RH
- Ventilation flow: 25 L/s per person

**Step 1: Total ventilation air volume flow**

$$
\dot{V} = 100 \times 25 = 2500 \, \text{L/s} = 2.5 \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}
$$

**Step 2: Mass flow rate of ventilation air**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 2.5 = 3.0 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3: Humidity ratios**

At outdoor: 34 °C DBT, 60 % RH → $W_{\text{out}} \approx 0.0190$ kg/kg
At indoor: 24 °C DBT, 55 % RH → $W_{\text{in}} \approx 0.0103$ kg/kg

$$
\Delta W = 0.0190 - 0.0103 = 0.0087 \, \text{kg/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Sensible ventilation load (i)**

$$
Q_{v,s} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T = 3.0 \times 1.005 \times (34 - 24)
$$

$$
Q_{v,s} = 3.0 \times 1.005 \times 10 = 30.15 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5: Latent ventilation load (ii)**

$$
Q_{v,l} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 3.0 \times 2501 \times 0.0087
$$

$$
Q_{v,l} = 65.276 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{v,s} \approx 30.15 \, \text{kW} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{v,l} \approx 65.28 \, \text{kW}}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for sensible, 2 for latent, 1 for unit conversion / final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Using peak WBT instead of MCWB:** This inflates latent load by 15–25 % and leads to over-sized cooling coils. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Ignoring indoor latent gain from occupants:** Always remember humans release both sensible AND latent heat. A seated person emits ~75 W sensible + ~55 W latent.
> 3. **Confusing DBT with WBT in load equations:** Ventilation **sensible** load uses DBT difference; ventilation **latent** load uses humidity ratio difference. Mixing them up is the most common mistake in Part B.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert L/s to m³/s or to kg/s:** Units inconsistency is a silent mark-killer.
> 5. **Not applying urban heat island correction for Kerala cities** like Kochi, Kozhikode, and Thiruvananthapuram — at least +1 °C correction is expected for dense urban areas.
> 6. **Writing "1 % exceedance" without explaining the meaning** — examiners expect you to state that it represents ≈ 88 hours/year.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **ODC** is the extreme outdoor condition used to design the HVAC load.
- **IDC** is the target indoor condition for comfort or process.
- ODC is based on **statistical percent exceedance** — 0.4 %, 1 %, 2 %, 5 %.
- **MCWB (Mean Coincident WBT)** is preferred over peak WBT for realistic latent load estimation.
- The **six comfort parameters** (per Fanger): Tₐ, MRT, v, RH, Met, Clo.
- **ASHRAE Standard 55** defines the comfort zone on the psychrometric chart.
- **PMV** between −0.5 and +0.5 = comfortable for 90 % occupants.
- **Adaptive comfort model** allows wider bands in naturally ventilated buildings.
- **Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR)** = SHG / (SHG + LHG) — drives coil selection.
- For **Kerala**: typical 1 % DBT ≈ 33–35 °C, MCWB ≈ 26–28 °C.
- Standard **IDC** for offices: 23–26 °C DBT, 40–60 % RH.
- Always apply **+1 K urban heat island correction** for dense urban Indian cities.
- Critical applications: hospitals, data centers → use **0.4 % ODC**.
- Standard atmospheric pressure assumed = **101.325 kPa** unless otherwise stated.
- Use $c_{p} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ (standard reference values).
- Ventilation load has **two parts** — sensible (uses ΔT) and latent (uses ΔW).
- ECBC 2017 (India) sets **minimum energy performance** including recommended indoor temperature of **24–26 °C** for conditioned commercial spaces.
- ASHRAE 62.1 sets **minimum outdoor air rates** — typically 5–10 L/s per person for offices, 8–10 L/s for classrooms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Forming the Load on Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Forming the Load on Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Cooling Load (Q_total)** of a refrigeration or air-conditioning system is defined as the *rate at which heat energy must be removed from a conditioned space (or refrigerated enclosure) in order to maintain the prescribed inside design conditions of dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, and air purity against all internal and external heat gains.*

Mathematically, it is the time-derivative of the cumulative heat entering the space:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{dQ}{dt} \quad \text{[kW] or [TR] or [kJ/hr]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Standard:**
> - $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} = 12660 \text{ kJ/hr}$
> - $1 \text{ kW} = 3412 \text{ BTU/hr}$
> - Cooling load is **NOT** the same as Refrigeration Effect (RE); RE includes compressor work. $RE = Q_{evaporator} + W_{compressor}$.

### Classification of Loads on an HVAC&R System

Loads are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**(A) By Origin of Heat:**

| Origin | Sub-Category |
|---|---|
| External (Envelope) Loads | Transmission through walls/roof/floor, Solar gain through glass, Infiltration, Ventilation air |
| Internal Loads | Occupants, Lighting, Equipment/Appliances, Process loads |

**(B) By Thermal Nature:**

| Type | Definition | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensible Heat Load (SHL)** | Heat that changes dry-bulb temperature only | Raises/lowers temperature |
| **Latent Heat Load (LHL)** | Heat that changes moisture content only (at constant dry-bulb) | Adds/removes humidity |

> [!NOTE]
> $Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}$
> Always compute and report **both** components separately in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaking Bucket" Model

Imagine a **bucket with a tap at the bottom** that you are trying to keep **exactly half-full** of water.

- The water level inside the bucket = desired room temperature (e.g., **25 °C**).
- Water keeps *leaking in* from many small holes — sun beating on the roof, hot outside air seeping through the door, people breathing inside, bulbs glowing.
- The drain at the bottom is the **evaporator coil** of your AC — it pumps water out.
- The flow rate through the drain must **equal the total inflow** to keep the level constant.

The **cooling load = sum of all the leaks (inflows)**, and the **AC capacity = drain rate**. If the drain is too small, the bucket overflows (room gets hotter/humid).

> [!TIP]
> Just like a bucket cannot distinguish *where* the water comes from, the AC handles **all** loads together. But an engineer MUST distinguish them, because each source requires a different mitigation strategy (insulation, shading, fresh-air dampers, LED lighting, occupancy schedules, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Visualisation of the Load Concept

A conditioned space is in continuous **thermal equilibrium**:

$$Q_{generated} = Q_{removed} = Q_{cooling\,load}$$

Any imbalance causes:
- $\sum Q_{in} > Q_{out}$ → Space **warms** (or humidity rises).
- $\sum Q_{in} < Q_{out}$ → Space **over-cools** (wasted energy).

The design of an HVAC&R plant begins with a **precise enumeration** of every load component, evaluated at peak design conditions.

---

## 1.4 Units, Conventions & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{air}$ | Density of air | **1.204 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, 1 atm) |
| $c_{p,air}$ | Specific heat of air | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| $h_{fg}$ | Latent heat of vaporisation (water) | **2501 kJ/kg** (at 0 °C) |
| $U$ | Overall heat-transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |
| $R$ | Thermal resistance | m²·K/W |
| $SHGF$ | Solar Heat Gain Factor | W/m² |
| $SC$ | Shading Coefficient | dimensionless, $0 < SC \leq 1$ |
| $CLTD$ | Cooling Load Temperature Difference | °C or K |
| $TR$ | Ton of Refrigeration | 3.517 kW |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of All Cooling-Load Components

The total instantaneous cooling load on a building/enclosure is the **algebraic sum** of six principal streams:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{1,wall} + Q_{2,roof} + Q_{3,glass} + Q_{4,vent+inf} + Q_{5,internal} + Q_{6,misc}$$

Each of these is broken down below.

---

### 2.1.1 EXTERNAL / ENVELOPE LOADS

#### (1) Heat Conduction Through Opaque Surfaces — *Transmission Load*

Heat flows from the higher-temperature side (outdoor, $T_o$) to the lower-temperature side (indoor, $T_i$) through walls, roof, partitions, and floor by **conduction**.

$$Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{overall}$$

with

$$\Delta T_{overall} = (T_o - T_i) + \text{solar/correction factor (CLTD)}$$

and the **overall U-value** for a multi-layered wall:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{x_k}{k_k} + \frac{1}{h_o} = R_i + \sum R_k + R_o$$

where $x_k$ is the thickness and $k_k$ the thermal conductivity of the $k$-th layer, $h_i$ inside air-film coefficient, $h_o$ outside air-film coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> For most KTU problems the solar/CLTD correction is absorbed into the design $\Delta T$. Use the simpler $Q = U A \Delta T$ form unless CLTD tables are explicitly supplied.

#### (2) Heat Gain Through Glazing — *Solar + Conduction*

Glass is **transparent**, so two heat streams enter simultaneously:

$$Q_{glass} = \underbrace{A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC}_{solar\;radiation} + \underbrace{U_{glass} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}_{conduction}$$

- $SHGF$ — peak solar intensity (W/m²) striking the glass for a given orientation, latitude, month, and hour (from tables).
- $SC$ — fraction of incident solar radiation admitted after passing through the glazing and internal shading (e.g., SC ≈ 0.88 for single clear glass, 0.40 for tinted, 0.25 for reflective).

#### (3) Infiltration Load

Uncontrolled leakage of outdoor air through **cracks, door openings, window gaps** carries both sensible and latent heat:

$$Q_{inf,s} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

$$Q_{inf,l} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_o - W_i)$$

where $\dot{V}_{inf}$ is the infiltration volumetric flow rate, often computed from **air-changes-per-hour (ACH)**:

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{\text{Volume of room} \times ACH}{3600} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Typical residential ACH = 0.5 to 1.0; commercial tight building = 0.1 to 0.3; leaky industrial = 1.0 to 2.0.

#### (4) Ventilation Load

**Intentional** introduction of outdoor air to maintain IAQ (Indoor Air Quality) per ASHRAE 62.1. Same equations as infiltration, but $\dot{V}_{vent}$ is prescribed (e.g., 8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m² of floor area).

---

### 2.1.2 INTERNAL LOADS

#### (5) Occupant Load

Humans release both **sensible heat** (warm skin, exhaled air temperature) and **latent heat** (sweat, respiration moisture):

$$Q_{occ} = n \cdot (q_s + q_l)$$

| Activity | $q_s$ (W) | $q_l$ (W) | $q_{total}$ (W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seated, office work | 60 | 50 | 110 |
| Light factory work | 70 | 80 | 150 |
| Walking, light | 75 | 75 | 150 |
| Heavy work | 145 | 230 | 375 |
| Dancing | 90 | 160 | 250 |

#### (6) Lighting Load

Electrical energy of lamps is **fully converted to heat** inside the room:

$$Q_{light} = W_{installed} \times F_u \times F_a$$

where $F_u$ = usage factor (fraction of lights on) and $F_a$ = allowance factor for ballast/driver losses. LED ≈ 1.05, fluorescent ≈ 1.20, incandescent ≈ 1.0.

#### (7) Equipment / Appliance Load

Motors, computers, refrigerators, ovens, printers — all electrical power eventually dissipates as heat inside the space:

$$Q_{equip} = \sum P_i \times \text{(inside-fraction)} \times F_u$$

For motors inside the room, only the **shaft output** leaves as useful work — the input minus shaft output (losses) becomes heat.

---

### 2.1.3 MISCELLANEOUS LOADS

- **Product Load** (cold storage): $Q_p = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{final}) + m \cdot h_{if}$ (if freezing).
- **Respiration Load** (fruits/vegetables): $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{resp}(T)$.
- **Defrost Load** (refrigeration): pulse heat during periodic defrost cycles.
- **Safety / Diversity Factor**: applied to total to prevent undersizing (typically 5–10 %).

---

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

The table below contains **every formula** you need to attempt a KTU 2024 numerical on this topic. Memorise it.

| No. | Load Component | Governing Formula | Primary Variables | Units of Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Conduction through composite wall | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U$ = overall U-value | W or kW |
| F2 | U-value of composite wall | $1/U = R_i + \sum (x_k/k_k) + R_o$ | $x_k$, $k_k$ | W/m²·K |
| F3 | Solar gain through glass | $Q_s = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC$ | $A$, $SHGF$, $SC$ | W |
| F4 | Conduction through glass | $Q_g = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T$ | $U_g$, $A_g$ | W |
| F5 | Infiltration / Ventilation sensible | $Q_s = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\dot{V}$ = m³/s | W |
| F6 | Infiltration / Ventilation latent | $Q_l = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | $\Delta W$ = kg water/kg dry air | W |
| F7 | Air-change volumetric flow | $\dot{V} = (V_{room} \times ACH)/3600$ | $V_{room}$ m³, ACH hr⁻¹ | m³/s |
| F8 | Occupant load | $Q_{occ} = n(q_s + q_l)$ | $n$ = number of occupants | W |
| F9 | Lighting load | $Q_L = W_{lamp} \cdot F_u \cdot F_a$ | lamp wattage, factors | W |
| F10 | Equipment load | $Q_{eq} = \sum P_i \cdot \eta_{room}$ | power, in-room fraction | W |
| F11 | Product sensible cooling | $Q_p = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{product}$ | mass, sp. heat, $\Delta T$ | W |
| F12 | Product latent (freezing) | $Q_f = m \cdot h_{if}$ | latent heat of fusion | W |
| F13 | Total cooling load | $Q_{tot} = \sum Q_i$ (with safety factor) | — | W or TR |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Why is this enumeration important?

1. **Right-Sizing Equipment**: An oversized chiller wastes capital; an undersized one fails at peak. Load calculation prevents both.
2. **Energy Auditing**: Audit engineers quantify each contributor to recommend targeted retrofits — e.g., "your glass contributes 40 % of the load — switch to double-glazed low-E".
3. **Control Strategy**: Smart thermostats can shed *non-critical* loads (reduce lighting, raise setpoint by 1 °C during unoccupied hours) to maintain comfort.
4. **Code Compliance**: ASHRAE 90.1, ECBC (India), and KTU-referenced standards require documented load calculations for certification.
5. **Green Building Rating**: LEED / GRIHA award points for load-reduction strategies like reflective roofs, daylight harvesting, etc.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation A — Overall U-Value of a Composite Wall

### Statement
A wall is built up of $n$ plane layers in series. Each layer $k$ has thickness $x_k$ (m) and thermal conductivity $k_k$ (W/m·K). Air films exist on the inside (resistance $R_i$) and outside ($R_o$). Derive the expression for the overall U-value.

### Derivation

Consider steady-state one-dimensional conduction. Let $T_o > T_i$.

Heat flux through the wall (Fourier's law):

$$q = \frac{T_o - T_i}{R_{total}} \quad \text{[W/m²]}$$

The total thermal resistance is the **sum** of individual resistances in series (temperatures add):

$$R_{total} = R_o + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \dots + \frac{x_n}{k_n} + R_i$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{U} = R_i + \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{x_k}{k_k} + R_o$$

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{R_i + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{x_k}{k_k} + R_o}}$$

### Worked Numerical — Composite Wall U-Value

**Given:** A 4 m × 3 m wall made of (in order, outdoor → indoor):
- 25 mm cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/m·K)
- 200 mm brick ($k = 0.81$ W/m·K)
- 40 mm expanded polystyrene insulation ($k = 0.038$ W/m·K)
- 15 mm gypsum plaster ($k = 0.16$ W/m·K)
- Inside air film $R_i = 0.13$ m²·K/W
- Outside air film $R_o = 0.04$ m²·K/W

**Inside design temp** $T_i = 25$ °C; **Outside design temp** $T_o = 40$ °C.

Compute total resistance:

$$\frac{x_{cement}}{k_{cement}} = \frac{0.025}{0.72} = 0.0347 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$\frac{x_{brick}}{k_{brick}} = \frac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.2469 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$\frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} = \frac{0.040}{0.038} = 1.0526 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$\frac{x_{gyp}}{k_{gyp}} = \frac{0.015}{0.16} = 0.0938 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

Summing:

$$R_{total} = 0.04 + 0.0347 + 0.2469 + 1.0526 + 0.0938 + 0.13 = 1.598 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

Overall U-value:

$$U = \frac{1}{1.598} = 0.626 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

Heat transfer rate through the wall:

$$Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_o - T_i) = 0.626 \times (4 \times 3) \times (40 - 25) = 0.626 \times 12 \times 15 = 112.7 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the 40 mm insulation layer alone contributes **66 %** of the total resistance. This is why KTU emphasises insulation in envelope design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Infiltration Load (Sensible + Latent)

### Statement
Outdoor air at $T_o = 38$ °C, RH = 60 % leaks into a hall at $T_i = 24$ °C, RH = 50 %. The hall volume is $V = 600$ m³ and ACH = 1.0. Find the total infiltration cooling load.

### Step-by-Step

**(a) Humidity ratios from psychrometric chart / equations:**

For outside air: $P_{sat}(38°) \approx 6.65$ kPa; $P_v = 0.60 \times 6.65 = 3.99$ kPa
$W_o = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (101.325 - P_v) = 0.622 \times 3.99 / 97.34 = 0.0255$ kg/kg

For inside air: $P_{sat}(24°) \approx 2.99$ kPa; $P_v = 0.50 \times 2.99 = 1.495$ kPa
$W_i = 0.622 \times 1.495 / 99.83 = 0.00931$ kg/kg

$$\Delta W = W_o - W_i = 0.0255 - 0.0093 = 0.0162 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**(b) Volumetric infiltration flow rate:**

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{V \cdot ACH}{3600} = \frac{600 \times 1.0}{3600} = 0.1667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(c) Mass flow rate of air:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.1667 = 0.20 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(d) Sensible load:**

$$Q_{s} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.20 \times 1.005 \times (38 - 24) = 0.20 \times 1.005 \times 14 = 2.814 \text{ kW}$$

**(e) Latent load:**

$$Q_l = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 0.20 \times 2501 \times 0.0162 = 8.10 \text{ kW}$$

**(f) Total infiltration load:**

$$\boxed{Q_{inf,total} = 2.814 + 8.10 = 10.91 \text{ kW} \approx 3.10 \text{ TR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The latent component here is **larger** than the sensible — a classic signature of hot-humid climates (Kerala in summer). Always evaluate both.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Solar Heat Gain Through Glazed Window

### Statement
A 2 m × 1.5 m window faces west. SHGF (peak, west) = 530 W/m². SC = 0.55 (tinted double glass). Calculate solar heat gain.

### Solution

Area:

$$A = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ m}^2$$

Solar heat gain:

$$Q_{solar} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC = 3 \times 530 \times 0.55 = 874.5 \text{ W}$$

Conduction through the same glass (if $U_g = 2.7$ W/m²K, $\Delta T = 15$ K):

$$Q_{cond} = U_g \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 2.7 \times 3 \times 15 = 121.5 \text{ W}$$

Total glass load:

$$Q_{glass,total} = 874.5 + 121.5 = 996 \text{ W} \approx 1 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Order-of-Magnitude Contributions

| Load Component | Typical Magnitude (Small Office, 100 m², Kerala Summer) | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Walls | 1.5 – 2.5 kW | 15 % |
| Roof | 3.0 – 5.0 kW | 30 % |
| Glass (solar + conduction) | 2.0 – 4.0 kW | 20 % |
| Infiltration + Ventilation | 3.0 – 6.0 kW | 25 % |
| Occupants + Lights + Equipment | 1.5 – 3.0 kW | 10 % |
| **Total** | **11 – 20 kW** | **100 %** |

> [!TIP]
> The **roof dominates** in tropical single-storey buildings (sun overhead). The **infiltration** spikes in leaky buildings. KTU frequently asks students to *rank* these loads — memorise the trend.

---

## 3.5 Sample Comprehensive Problem (End-to-End)

A small office hall: $L = 10$ m, $W = 8$ m, $H = 3$ m, single glazed east-facing window $3 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$, remaining envelope U = 1.2 W/m²K, $\Delta T = 12$ K. SHGF = 420 W/m², SC = 0.85. ACH = 0.8, outside 36 °C/65 % RH, inside 25 °C/50 % RH. 12 occupants (office work), 1.5 kW lighting, 1 kW equipment. Compute total cooling load in TR.

**Envelope conduction (opaque) area:**

$$A_{opaque} = 2(10\times3) + 2(8\times3) + 10\times8 - 3\times2 = 60+48+80-6 = 182 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{env} = 1.2 \times 182 \times 12 = 2620.8 \text{ W} = 2.62 \text{ kW}$$

**Glass:**
$Q_{solar} = 3\times2 \times 420 \times 0.85 = 2142$ W = 2.14 kW
$Q_{cond} = U_g \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — assuming $U_g = 5.8$ W/m²K for single glass = $5.8\times6\times12$ = 417.6 W = 0.42 kW
$Q_{glass} = 2.56$ kW

**Infiltration:**
$V = 240$ m³; $\dot{V} = 240 \times 0.8 / 3600 = 0.0533$ m³/s; $\dot{m} = 0.064$ kg/s
$Q_{s,inf} = 0.064 \times 1.005 \times 11 = 0.71$ kW
$\Delta W$: $W_o = 0.0206$, $W_i = 0.0093$, $\Delta W = 0.0113$
$Q_{l,inf} = 0.064 \times 2501 \times 0.0113 = 1.81$ kW
$Q_{inf} = 2.52$ kW

**Internal:**
$Q_{occ} = 12 \times (60+50) = 1320$ W = 1.32 kW
$Q_{light} = 1.5$ kW
$Q_{equip} = 1.0$ kW
$Q_{int} = 3.82$ kW

**Total:**
$Q_{total} = 2.62 + 2.56 + 2.52 + 3.82 = 11.52$ kW
$Q_{total} = 11.52 / 3.517 = \mathbf{3.28 \text{ TR}}$

Add 10 % safety factor → $\approx 3.61$ TR → select **4 TR** nearest commercial size.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Decomposition of Total Cooling Load

The following Mermaid block illustrates how the **six primary load streams** combine to produce the **total cooling load** that the evaporator coil must handle. The diagram follows standard KTU teaching hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cooling Load Q total kW] --> B[External Envelope Loads]
    A --> C[Internal Space Loads]
    A --> D[Miscellaneous Loads]

    B --> B1[Wall conduction U A deltaT]
    B --> B2[Roof conduction U A deltaT]
    B --> B3[Floor conduction U A deltaT]
    B --> B4[Glass solar A SHGF SC]
    B --> B5[Glass conduction U A deltaT]
    B --> B6[Infiltration sensible]
    B --> B7[Infiltration latent]
    B --> B8[Ventilation air sensible]
    B --> B9[Ventilation air latent]

    C --> C1[Occupants sensible heat]
    C --> C2[Occupants latent heat]
    C --> C3[Lighting heat]
    C --> C4[Equipment appliances motors]
    C --> C5[Process loads]

    D --> D1[Product load refrigeration]
    D --> D2[Respiration load perishable]
    D --> D3[Defrost load pulse]
    D --> D4[Safety and diversity factor]

    B1 --> E[Summation node]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    B5 --> E
    B6 --> E
    B7 --> E
    B8 --> E
    B9 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    C5 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Evaporator Coil Capacity Required]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — How a Load Becomes a Plant Specification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Enumerate loads] --> P2[Step 2 Compute sensible and latent]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Sum total Q kW]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Convert to TR]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Apply safety factor]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Select standard capacity]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Match chiller or AHU]

    P1 -.feedback.-> P1a[Use CLTD SHGF ACH tables]
    P2 -.feedback.-> P2a[Use psychrometric chart for W]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Heat-Flow into a Conditioned Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTSIDE
        SUN[Sun radiation SHGF]
        TOA[Outside air T o, W o]
    end

    subgraph BUILDING ENVELOPE
        WAL[Wall U-value]
        GLA[Window glass SC]
        CRA[Cracks and openings ACH]
    end

    subgraph INSIDE CONDITIONED SPACE
        OCC[Occupants q s and q l]
        LIG[Lights W]
        EQU[Equipment P]
    end

    SUN -->|Q solar| GLA --> ROOM[Indoor air T i, W i]
    TOA -->|Q infiltration| CRA --> ROOM
    TOA -->|Q conduction| WAL --> ROOM
    OCC -->|Q occ| ROOM
    LIG -->|Q light| ROOM
    EQU -->|Q equip| ROOM

    ROOM -->|Q cool supplied| EVAP[Evaporator coil]
    EVAP --> CHILLER[Chiller or refrigeration cycle]
```

---

## 4.4 Reference Diagram — Sensible vs. Latent on Psychrometric Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1 Outside air T o, W o] -->|Sensible cooling, T drops, W same| B[State 2 T drops to ADP line]
    B -->|Latent cooling and humidification, W drops to W i| C[State 3 Supply air T s, W s]
    C -->|Room sensible heat Q s added| D[State 4 Room air T i, W i]
    A -.latent heat Q l.-> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **ADP** = Apparatus Dew Point — the temperature of the cooling coil surface. State 3 lies on the saturation curve at the coil temperature. KTU frequently tests this psychrometric relationship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (Module 2, KTU pattern)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**"Define cooling load. List the major external and internal factors that contribute to the cooling load of a conditioned building."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The cooling load of an air-conditioning system is the rate at which heat must be removed from the conditioned space to maintain the prescribed inside dry-bulb temperature and relative humidity under steady-state design conditions. It is expressed in **kW** or **TR** ($1$ TR = 3.517 kW).

**External factors (1 mark):**
1. Heat conduction through opaque envelope (walls, roof, floor) — $Q = U A \Delta T$.
2. Solar heat gain through glass — $Q = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC$.
3. Heat conduction through glass.
4. Infiltration of outdoor air through cracks / openings (sensible + latent).
5. Ventilation air for indoor-air-quality maintenance.

**Internal factors (1 mark):**
1. Heat from occupants — sensible (skin, exhaled air) + latent (sweat, respiration).
2. Heat from lighting (entire input wattage converts to heat).
3. Heat from equipment, appliances, motors.
4. Process loads (if industrial).

---

### Question 2 (Module 2, KTU pattern)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between sensible heat load and latent heat load. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat Load | Latent Heat Load |
|---|---|---|
| Effect | Changes dry-bulb temperature | Changes humidity ratio (moisture content) |
| Measured by | Thermometer | Psychrometric / hygrometer |
| Formula | $Q_s = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | $Q_l = \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta W$ |

**Sensible examples (1.5 marks):**
1. Heat conduction through a brick wall on a hot day.
2. Heat emitted by an incandescent bulb warming the room air.

**Latent examples (1.5 marks):**
1. Moisture released by occupants' respiration (latent fraction of occupant load).
2. Infiltration of humid outside air during monsoon (latent component of ventilation load).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[MODULE-INTERNAL CHOICE — KTU PATTERN]**
> *Attempt either full Question A or full Question B.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

A small air-conditioned office has the following data:
- Room dimensions: 12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (L × W × H)
- One east-facing glass window: 4 m × 2 m, single-glazed ($U_g = 5.8$ W/m²K, SC = 0.88)
- Remaining opaque envelope: overall $U = 1.1$ W/m²K
- Inside DB = 25 °C, RH = 50 % ; Outside DB = 36 °C, RH = 70 %
- SHGF (east, peak) = 470 W/m²
- Infiltration ACH = 0.6
- Occupants = 15 (office work)
- Lighting = 1.8 kW (all fluorescent, on throughout)
- Equipment = 1.2 kW (computers, printers)

**Compute the following and present as a complete cooling-load table:**
**(a)** Sensible and latent heat gain through opaque envelope, glass (solar + conduction), and infiltration. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Internal loads (occupants, lighting, equipment) and the **total** cooling load in kW and TR. Apply a 10 % safety factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Data preparation:**

Total wall area (gross):
$A_{gross} = 2(12\times3.5) + 2(8\times3.5) + (12\times8) = 84 + 56 + 96 = 236$ m²
Net opaque area = $236 - 8 = 228$ m²

Room volume $V = 12 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 336$ m³

Humidity ratios (using standard psychrometric relations):

$$W_o \approx 0.0242 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad W_i \approx 0.0099 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$\Delta W = 0.0242 - 0.0099 = 0.0143 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$\Delta T = 36 - 25 = 11$ K

---

**(a) Part Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Opaque envelope conduction — sensible only:**
$Q_{env} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 1.1 \times 228 \times 11 = 2758.8$ W ≈ **2.76 kW** (sensible) **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Glass solar gain:**
$Q_{sol} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC = 8 \times 470 \times 0.88 = 3308.8$ W ≈ **3.31 kW** (sensible, radiative) **(1.5 marks)**

**(iii) Glass conduction:**
$Q_{g,cond} = U_g \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 5.8 \times 8 \times 11 = 510.4$ W ≈ **0.51 kW** (sensible) **(1 mark)**

**(iv) Infiltration — sensible:**
$\dot{V}_{inf} = 336 \times 0.6 / 3600 = 0.056$ m³/s
$\dot{m}_{air} = 1.2 \times 0.056 = 0.0672$ kg/s
$Q_{inf,s} = 0.0672 \times 1.005 \times 11 = 0.743$ kW **(sensible) (1 mark)**

**(v) Infiltration — latent:**
$Q_{inf,l} = 0.0672 \times 2501 \times 0.0143 = 2.403$ kW **(latent) (1.5 marks)**

---

**(b) Part Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Occupants:** Office-work values $q_s = 60$ W, $q_l = 55$ W per person (KTU table).
$Q_{occ,s} = 15 \times 60 = 0.90$ kW **(sensible)**
$Q_{occ,l} = 15 \times 55 = 0.825$ kW **(latent)**
$Q_{occ,total} = 1.725$ kW **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Lighting:** All electrical energy → heat. With fluorescent ballast factor 1.20:
$Q_L = 1.8 \times 1.20 = 2.16$ kW **(sensible) (1 mark)**

**(iii) Equipment:** 100 % becomes heat:
$Q_{eq} = 1.2$ kW **(sensible) (1 mark)**

**(iv) Summation Table:**

| Load | Sensible (kW) | Latent (kW) | Total (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opaque envelope | 2.76 | 0 | 2.76 |
| Glass solar | 3.31 | 0 | 3.31 |
| Glass conduction | 0.51 | 0 | 0.51 |
| Infiltration | 0.74 | 2.40 | 3.14 |
| Occupants | 0.90 | 0.83 | 1.73 |
| Lighting | 2.16 | 0 | 2.16 |
| Equipment | 1.20 | 0 | 1.20 |
| **Sub-total** | **11.58** | **3.23** | **14.81** |
| Safety (10 %) | 1.16 | 0.32 | 1.48 |
| **Grand Total** | **12.74** | **3.55** | **16.29** |

**(2 marks)**

**(v) Convert to TR:**

$$Q_{total} = 16.29 \text{ kW} = \frac{16.29}{3.517} = \boxed{4.63 \text{ TR}}$$

**(1 mark)** — Select a standard **5 TR** commercial unit.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches and equations, the various **external heat-gain components** that constitute the cooling load of a building. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold-storage room of dimensions 4 m × 3 m × 2.5 m is used to store 800 kg of apples initially at 30 °C. The room must be cooled to 4 °C. The walls have an overall $U = 0.35$ W/m²K and the ambient temperature around the storage is 32 °C. Assume an ACH of 0.4, apples specific heat $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K, latent heat of freezing of apple water $h_{if} = 335$ kJ/kg (for 14 % free water content), and an internal load of 150 W (lights + fan motor). Compute the **peak refrigeration load in kW and TR**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**(a) External Heat-Gain Components (7 marks):**

The cooling load of any building has the following external (envelope) heat inputs:

**(i) Heat conduction through opaque walls, roof, and floor (2 marks):**
Driven by temperature difference across the envelope. Use Fourier's law:

$$Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

The U-value is the reciprocal of total thermal resistance. For a multi-layered wall:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \dots + \frac{1}{h_i}$$

The roof typically has the highest solar exposure in tropical climates and contributes the maximum conduction load in single-storey buildings.

**(ii) Heat gain through glass windows (2 marks):**
Two parallel paths — solar radiation passes through (transmitted) and conduction occurs across the glass:

$$Q_{glass} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \quad \text{(solar, radiative)}$$
$$Q_{glass,cond} = U_g \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(conduction)}$$

The **shading coefficient** $SC$ depends on glass type — single clear (0.88), tinted (0.55), reflective double (0.25).

**(iii) Infiltration and ventilation (2 marks):**
Uncontrolled leakage and intentional fresh-air intake bring outdoor air with both higher temperature and (often) higher humidity:

$$Q_{inf,s} = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T, \quad Q_{inf,l} = \rho \dot{V} h_{fg} \Delta W$$

**(iv) Sketch (1 mark):** (Student to draw envelope heat-flow diagram showing arrow directions from outside → inside through wall, glass, and cracks — refer Section 4.3 schematic.)

---

**(b) Cold-Storage Refrigeration Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- $V = 4 \times 3 \times 2.5 = 30$ m³
- $m_{apple} = 800$ kg
- $T_{initial} = 30$ °C, $T_{final} = 4$ °C, $T_{amb} = 32$ °C
- $U_{wall} = 0.35$ W/m²K
- ACH = 0.4
- $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K
- Water content = 14 %, $h_{if} = 335$ kJ/kg
- Internal load (lights + fan) = 150 W
- Assume cooling to be done in say 4 hours (peak load calculation uses the *peak hourly* rate; product load uses pull-down time).

**Surface area of cold room** (6 faces):
$A = 2(4\times3) + 2(4\times2.5) + 2(3\times2.5) = 24 + 20 + 15 = 59$ m²

**(1) Transmission (wall) load — peak, instantaneous:**
$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.35 \times 59 \times (32 - 4) = 0.35 \times 59 \times 28 = 578.2$ W ≈ **0.578 kW** **(1 mark)**

**(2) Product sensible-cooling load (pull-down):**
$Q_{p,s} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 800 \times 3.6 \times (30 - 4) = 800 \times 3.6 \times 26 = 74880$ kJ
If pulled down over 4 hours: $74880 / (4 \times 3600) = 5.20$ kW **(1.5 marks)**

**(3) Product latent (freezing) load:**
Mass of water in apples: $m_w = 0.14 \times 800 = 112$ kg
$Q_{p,l} = m_w \cdot h_{if} = 112 \times 335 = 37520$ kJ
Spread over 4 hr: $37520 / 14400 = 2.606$ kW **(1.5 marks)**

**(4) Infiltration load:**
$\dot{V} = 30 \times 0.4 / 3600 = 0.00333$ m³/s
$\dot{m} = 1.2 \times 0.00333 = 0.004$ kg/s
Assuming $\Delta T \approx 28$ K and $\Delta W$ small (already at 4 °C, RH ≈ 90 %):
$Q_{inf,s} = 0.004 \times 1.005 \times 28 = 0.113$ kW
$Q_{inf,l} \approx 0.05$ kW (approx.)
$Q_{inf} \approx 0.16$ kW **(1 mark)**

**(5) Internal load:**
$Q_{int} = 0.150$ kW **(0.5 mark)**

**(6) Total peak load:**
$Q_{total} = 0.578 + 5.20 + 2.606 + 0.16 + 0.15 = 8.69$ kW
$Q_{total} = 8.69 / 3.517 = \boxed{2.47 \text{ TR}}$ **(1.5 marks)**

→ Select a **3 TR** refrigeration unit with adequate pull-down capacity.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 1–3 Marks Per Question:**
>
> 1. **Unit mix-up**: Forgetting to convert **kW → TR** at the end ($1$ TR = 3.517 kW, **not** 3.5).
> 2. **Missing the 10 % safety factor**: Always include diversity/safety factor before final sizing.
> 3. **Conflating sensible and latent loads**: Report BOTH separately in tables; do not just give a single total.
> 4. **Wrong SHGF / SC values**: SHGF is orientation + latitude + month specific. SC is glass-type specific. Using SHGF = 250 W/m² blindly loses the method mark.
> 5. **Neglecting latent**: Infiltration in monsoon / coastal areas is dominantly **latent**, not sensible. Skipping it makes the chiller undersized for dehumidification.
> 6. **Using Celsius differences as Kelvin**: Actually numerically equal for $\Delta T$, but show working.
> 7. **Equating cooling load to refrigeration effect**: RE = cooling load + compressor work. Always specify whether the answer is "cooling load at evaporator" or "refrigeration effect".
> 8. **Wrong conversion of humidity ratio**: $\Delta W$ must be in **kg water / kg dry air**, NOT grams.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Cooling Load = rate of heat removal from conditioned space to maintain design conditions.
- [x] Two classifications: (i) Origin — External vs Internal, (ii) Nature — Sensible vs Latent.
- [x] External loads: conduction (walls, roof, glass), solar gain through glass, infiltration, ventilation.
- [x] Internal loads: occupants (sensible + latent), lighting (≈100 %→heat), equipment (≈100 %→heat).
- [x] Formula $Q = U A \Delta T$ for conduction; $1/U = \sum R$ for composite wall.
- [x] Solar gain $Q = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC$.
- [x] Infiltration $Q_s = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$; $Q_l = \rho \dot{V} h_{fg} \Delta W$.
- [x] $\dot{V}_{inf} = V_{room} \times ACH / 3600$.
- [x] Occupant values from KTU table (office 60/50 W sensible/latent per person).
- [x] Lighting allowance factor ≈ 1.2 for fluorescent, 1.05 for LED.
- [x] Refrigeration-specific loads: product (sensible + freezing latent), respiration (fruits), defrost.
- [x] Always apply **safety factor 5–10 %** before final sizing.
- [x] $1$ TR = 3.517 kW; Cooling Load ≠ Refrigeration Effect.
- [x] Sensible heat load affects dry-bulb; Latent affects humidity ratio (use psychrometric chart).
- [x] In hot-humid climates, latent dominates; in hot-dry climates, sensible dominates.
- [x] KTU commonly asks **order-of-magnitude ranking** — roof > infiltration > glass > walls > internal in tropical offices.
- [x] Always present a **summary table** with sensible and latent columns separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for Cooling and Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Procedure for Cooling and Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems for automobiles**, the **Cooling Load** is defined as the *rate at which heat energy must be removed from the passenger cabin of a vehicle to maintain specified indoor comfort conditions* against the combined influences of outdoor climate, solar radiation, vehicle envelope heat conduction, occupant metabolic heat, ventilation air, and internal equipment loads.

Correspondingly, the **Heating Load** is defined as the *rate at which heat energy must be supplied to the cabin* to compensate for conduction and ventilation heat losses to a colder outdoor environment, plus reheating for humidity control.

Mathematically, the steady-state total cooling load for an automobile is expressed as:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{envelope} + Q_{solar} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{occupants} + Q_{equipment}$$

where each term has units of **Watts (W)** or **kW**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 explicitly covers the *load estimation methodology for mobile HVAC systems*. Unlike stationary buildings, automobile HVAC must handle **transient thermal behavior, high solar loads on glass, rapid cool-down requirements, and limited space for heat exchangers**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse on Wheels"

Imagine the passenger cabin of a car parked under the hot summer sun. The car behaves like a **miniature greenhouse on wheels**:

- Sunlight enters through the windshield and windows (short-wave radiation) and is absorbed by the seats, dashboard, and floor.
- These interior surfaces re-radiate heat at longer wavelengths (long-wave radiation), which **cannot escape back through the glass** — this is the *greenhouse effect*.
- The metal body conducts heat from the hot outer shell inward.
- You (the occupant) are a small heater, generating metabolic heat (~100 W).
- The engine bay radiates additional heat into the cabin via the firewall.

The **cooling load calculation** is essentially the accountant's job of *adding up every heat "deposit" entering this greenhouse*, so the AC compressor can withdraw the correct amount. The **heating load** is the reverse: tallying every heat "withdrawal" during a cold winter morning, so the heater core supplies the correct amount.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Building HVAC:**
> - Automobiles have **high glass-to-floor ratios** (up to 60% of envelope area).
> - **Infiltration rates** are much higher (≈ 1 to 5 air changes per hour) due to door/window seals and ram-air effect during motion.
> - **Settle times** are short — the system is judged on how fast it brings cabin temperature to comfort.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Conditions for Automobile HVAC Design

| Parameter | Cooling Mode | Heating Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Outside design temperature | **35 °C to 43 °C** (depending on region) | **−5 °C to −15 °C** |
| Outside humidity | 50–70% RH | 60–90% RH |
| Inside design temperature | **25 °C** | **22 °C** |
| Inside humidity | 50% RH | 30–40% RH |
| Solar radiation | Up to **1000 W/m²** (peak at noon) | Negligible |
| Vehicle speed | 0–120 km/h | 0–120 km/h |
| Number of occupants | 1 to 5 | 1 to 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout:**
> - Specific heat of air, $c_p$ = **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Density of air, $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, 1 atm)
> - Latent heat of vaporization, $h_{fg}$ = **2460 kJ/kg**
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant, $\sigma$ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Automobile Cooling Load

The total cooling load is the **algebraic sum of seven distinct heat gain terms**. Each term has a specific physical origin and a unique calculation methodology.

#### 2.1.1 Heat Gain Through the Vehicle Envelope (Opaque Surfaces)

The vehicle body — including roof, doors, hood, trunk lid, and floor — is composed of **multi-layered panels** (steel sheet + insulation + interior trim). Heat flows inward by conduction, driven by the temperature difference across the wall. Because the outer surface also absorbs solar radiation, the effective driving temperature becomes the **Sol-Air Temperature** $T_{sol-air}$.

**Sol-Air Temperature Equation:**

$$T_{sol-air} = T_{outdoor} + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o} - \frac{\epsilon \cdot \Delta R}{h_o}$$

For opaque automotive panels (painted steel, low emissivity), the simplified form is:

$$T_{sol-air} \approx T_{outdoor} + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$$

where:
- $T_{outdoor}$ = ambient outside air temperature (°C)
- $\alpha_s$ = **solar absorptivity** of the surface (0.05–0.95 depending on paint colour)
- $I_t$ = total incident solar radiation on the surface (W/m²)
- $h_o$ = outside film convective coefficient ≈ **6 to 30 W/(m²·K)** depending on vehicle speed

**Heat Gain Through Opaque Wall:**

$$Q_{wall} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot (T_{sol-air} - T_{indoor})$$

where $U_{wall}$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient in **W/(m²·K)**, $A_{wall}$ is the surface area in **m²**, and the temperature difference is in **K** or **°C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sol-Air?**
> Standard building heat transfer uses $T_{outdoor}$, but automotive panels are directly sun-exposed. Sol-air lumps solar absorption and convection into a single fictitious outside temperature — making the equation algebraically identical to a plain conduction calculation.

#### 2.1.2 Heat Gain Through Vehicle Glazing

Glass is special — it allows short-wave solar radiation to pass through almost unattenuated, but blocks long-wave radiation. The total gain has two components:

**Conduction Through Glass:**

$$Q_{glass,cond} = U_{glass} \cdot A_{glass} \cdot (T_{outdoor} - T_{indoor})$$

**Solar Heat Gain Through Glass:**

$$Q_{glass,solar} = A_{glass} \cdot SHGC \cdot I_t \cdot SC$$

where:
- $SHGC$ = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (0.0 to 1.0)
- $SC$ = Shading Coefficient = **SHGC / 0.87** (for standard 3 mm clear glass reference)
- $I_t$ = incident solar intensity (W/m²)

**Total Glass Heat Gain:**

$$Q_{glass} = Q_{glass,cond} + Q_{glass,solar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Glass Types and Their SHGC:**
> - Laminated windshield: SHGC ≈ **0.40–0.55** (with IR-reflective coating as low as 0.30)
> - Tempered side windows: SHGC ≈ **0.60–0.80**
> - Privacy/solar glass: SHGC ≈ **0.30–0.45**

#### 2.1.3 Heat Gain from Occupants

Each occupant releases both **sensible heat** (raises dry-bulb temperature) and **latent heat** (adds moisture). For a typical seated, lightly clothed adult at 25 °C:

$$Q_{occ} = n_{occ} \cdot (q_s + q_l)$$

where:
- $n_{occ}$ = number of occupants
- $q_s$ = sensible heat per person ≈ **60 W** (at 25 °C)
- $q_l$ = latent heat per person ≈ **40 W**
- Total per occupant ≈ **100 W** (sometimes cited as **120 W** for higher activity)

#### 2.1.4 Heat Gain from Ventilation Air (Fresh Air)

Outside air deliberately admitted for ventilation carries heat that must be conditioned. The total is the sum of sensible and latent components:

$$Q_{vent,s} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{outdoor} - T_{indoor})$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_{outdoor} - W_{indoor}) \cdot h_{fg}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_v$ = mass flow rate of ventilation air (kg/s) = $\rho \cdot \dot{V}$
- $W_{outdoor}$, $W_{indoor}$ = humidity ratios (kg water/kg dry air)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization = **2460 kJ/kg**

#### 2.1.5 Heat Gain from Infiltration

Infiltration is *unintentional* air leakage through door seals, window gaps, etc. For an automobile, it is typically modeled using an **air-changes-per-hour (ACH)** approach:

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{ACH \cdot V_{cabin}}{3600}$$

$$Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{outdoor} - T_{indoor})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical automotive infiltration: **1 to 5 ACH** at low speeds, increasing significantly at highway speeds due to ram-air pressure.

#### 2.1.6 Heat Gain from Internal Equipment

For an automobile, this category includes:
- Engine bay radiation through the firewall: **100 to 500 W**
- Defrost/demist heaters (when active): variable
- Infotainment, instrument panel, lighting: typically **< 50 W**

#### 2.1.7 Heat Gain from Re-heat (Dehumidification)

If the system over-cools to dehumidify, then reheats to the desired dry-bulb temperature, the *reheat load* is a net cooling load on the system:

$$Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{overcool} - T_{supply})$$

### 2.2 Decomposition of the Automobile Heating Load

The heating load is structurally simpler — it is the sum of **heat losses** to the colder environment:

$$Q_{heat} = Q_{loss,wall} + Q_{loss,glass} + Q_{loss,ventilation} + Q_{loss,infiltration} - Q_{gain,solar} - Q_{gain,occupants}$$

Note that during heating mode:
- Solar gain is **subtracted** (reduces heating demand)
- Occupant heat is **subtracted** (free internal heat)
- No latent load component (the goal is usually to *decrease* humidity)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Automobile Cooling & Heating Load Calculation**

| # | Load Component | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sol-air temperature | $T_{sol-air} = T_o + \dfrac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$ | °C | Opaque panels only |
| 2 | Conduction through opaque | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | $\Delta T$ in K or °C |
| 3 | Glass conduction | $Q_g = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_o - T_i)$ | W | $U_g$ for single pane ≈ 5.7 |
| 4 | Glass solar gain | $Q_{gs} = A_g \cdot SHGC \cdot I_t$ | W | Direct use of SHGC |
| 5 | Occupant load | $Q_{occ} = n \cdot (q_s + q_l)$ | W | ~100 W per adult |
| 6 | Ventilation sensible | $Q_{vs} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$ | W | $\dot{m}_v = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ |
| 7 | Ventilation latent | $Q_{vl} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2460 kJ/kg |
| 8 | Infiltration | $Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot (ACH \cdot V/3600) \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Automotive: 1–5 ACH |
| 9 | Heating load (gross) | $Q_H = Q_{loss,wall} + Q_{loss,glass} + Q_{loss,vent}$ | W | Subtract internal gains |
| 10 | Cabin volume (auto) | $V_{cabin} \approx L \cdot W \cdot H_{eff}$ | m³ | $H_{eff}$ ≈ 1.1 m |
| 11 | Mass flow supply air | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{c_p \cdot (T_i - T_{supply})}$ | kg/s | For system sizing |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Load Calculation

The calculated load value drives the **sizing** of every downstream component:

- **Compressor capacity** (in cc/rev or kW)
- **Condenser face area** (m²)
- **Evaporator core size** (kW capacity, number of rows, fin density)
- **Blower fan speed and static pressure** (Pa)
- **Heater core thermal capacity** (kW)
- **Refrigerant line diameter and charge** (g)
- **Duct cross-section** (cm²)

A precise load calculation prevents the classic design failures: **under-sized evaporator** (cabin never cools in summer) or **oversized compressor** (clutch cycling, poor humidity control).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Full Cooling Load Calculation for a Mid-Size Sedan

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle: 4-door sedan, overall length $L$ = **4.5 m**, width $W$ = **1.8 m**, effective cabin height $H_{eff}$ = **1.1 m**
- Outside design: $T_o$ = **38 °C**, RH_o = **55%**, solar on horizontal = **800 W/m²**
- Inside design: $T_i$ = **25 °C**, RH_i = **50%**
- Glazing areas: Front windshield = **1.2 m²**, Rear window = **0.9 m²**, 4 side windows = **4 × 0.45 = 1.8 m²**, Sunroof (glass) = **0.5 m²**
- Opaque body panel total area (roof + doors + hood + trunk + floor minus glass) ≈ **14.6 m²**
- Number of occupants: **4 adults**
- Ventilation: **30 L/s** fresh air (ASHRAE 62.1 equivalent)
- Infiltration: **2 ACH**
- U-values:
  - $U_{wall}$ (opaque panel with insulation) = **2.5 W/(m²·K)**
  - $U_{glass}$ (laminated front, tempered rear/sides) = **5.8 W/(m²·K)**
- SHGC values:
  - Front windshield: **0.45**
  - Other glass: **0.70**
- Solar absorptivity of body paint (silver/grey): $\alpha_s$ = **0.55**
- Outside film coefficient (vehicle stationary, low wind): $h_o$ = **10 W/(m²·K)**
- Engine bay radiation through firewall: **200 W**

---

#### Step 1 — Compute the Sol-Air Temperature for the Body Panels

The Sol-air temperature lumps solar absorption and convection into a single fictitious outdoor temperature:

$$T_{sol-air} = T_o + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$$

Substituting the values:

$$T_{sol-air} = 38 + \frac{0.55 \times 800}{10}$$

$$T_{sol-air} = 38 + \frac{440}{10}$$

$$T_{sol-air} = 38 + 44$$

$$T_{sol-air} = 82 \; °C$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $\alpha_s \cdot I_t$ converts solar intensity into an equivalent absorbed flux (W/m²). Dividing by $h_o$ converts this flux into an equivalent temperature rise (°C). Adding $T_o$ gives the *effective* outdoor surface temperature for heat conduction inward.

---

#### Step 2 — Heat Gain Through Opaque Body Panels

Using the standard conduction equation with the sol-air temperature:

$$Q_{wall} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot (T_{sol-air} - T_i)$$

$$Q_{wall} = 2.5 \times 14.6 \times (82 - 25)$$

$$Q_{wall} = 2.5 \times 14.6 \times 57$$

$$Q_{wall} = 36.5 \times 57$$

$$\boxed{Q_{wall} = 2080.5 \; W}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $U \cdot A$ gives the overall thermal conductance in W/K. Multiplying by $\Delta T$ (in K or °C — both numerically equal) yields the heat flow rate in watts.

---

#### Step 3 — Heat Gain Through Glass (Conduction + Solar)

**Sub-step 3a — Conduction Through Glass:**

$$Q_{g,cond} = U_{glass} \cdot A_{glass,total} \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

Total glass area: $A_{glass} = 1.2 + 0.9 + 1.8 + 0.5 = 4.4 \; m^2$

$$Q_{g,cond} = 5.8 \times 4.4 \times (38 - 25)$$

$$Q_{g,cond} = 5.8 \times 4.4 \times 13$$

$$Q_{g,cond} = 25.52 \times 13$$

$$Q_{g,cond} = 331.76 \; W$$

**Sub-step 3b — Solar Gain Through Different Glazings:**

For the laminated windshield (different SHGC):

$$Q_{g,sol,wind} = A_{wind} \cdot SHGC_{wind} \cdot I_t$$

$$Q_{g,sol,wind} = 1.2 \times 0.45 \times 800 = 432 \; W$$

For the other glass (rear + sides + sunroof):

$$A_{other} = 0.9 + 1.8 + 0.5 = 3.2 \; m^2$$

$$Q_{g,sol,other} = 3.2 \times 0.70 \times 800$$

$$Q_{g,sol,other} = 3.2 \times 560$$

$$Q_{g,sol,other} = 1792 \; W$$

**Sub-step 3c — Total Glass Heat Gain:**

$$Q_{glass} = Q_{g,cond} + Q_{g,sol,wind} + Q_{g,sol,other}$$

$$Q_{glass} = 331.76 + 432 + 1792$$

$$\boxed{Q_{glass} = 2555.76 \; W}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Solar gain is computed separately for each glass type because SHGC depends on the glazing technology. The conduction term, however, depends only on the U-value, which is assumed uniform across all glazing in this simplified example.

---

#### Step 4 — Heat Gain from Occupants

For 4 occupants at 100 W per person (sensible + latent combined):

$$Q_{occ} = n_{occ} \cdot (q_s + q_l) = 4 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{Q_{occ} = 400 \; W}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The 100 W per person figure already accounts for the typical seated, lightly clothed condition. For more rigorous ASHRAE calculations, split into $q_s$ = 60 W and $q_l$ = 40 W, and apply a correction factor based on indoor temperature.

---

#### Step 5 — Heat Gain from Ventilation

First, compute the mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_v = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times (30 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\dot{m}_v = 1.2 \times 0.030 = 0.036 \; kg/s$$

**Sensible ventilation load:**

$$Q_{vent,s} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

$$Q_{vent,s} = 0.036 \times 1.005 \times (38 - 25)$$

$$Q_{vent,s} = 0.036 \times 1.005 \times 13$$

$$Q_{vent,s} = 0.036 \times 13.065$$

$$Q_{vent,s} = 0.470 \; kW = 470 \; W$$

**Latent ventilation load:**

We need humidity ratios at both states. Using standard psychrometric relations:

At $T_o = 38 \; °C$, RH = 55%, saturation pressure $P_{ws}(38) \approx 6.62 \; kPa$:
$$P_w = 0.55 \times 6.62 = 3.641 \; kPa$$
$$W_o = 0.622 \times \frac{P_w}{101.325 - P_w} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.641}{97.684} = 0.0232 \; kg/kg$$

At $T_i = 25 \; °C$, RH = 50%, $P_{ws}(25) \approx 3.17 \; kPa$:
$$P_w = 0.50 \times 3.17 = 1.585 \; kPa$$
$$W_i = 0.622 \times \frac{1.585}{101.325 - 1.585} = 0.00988 \; kg/kg$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = \dot{m}_v \cdot (W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = 0.036 \times (0.0232 - 0.00988) \times 2460$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = 0.036 \times 0.01332 \times 2460$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = 0.036 \times 32.77$$

$$Q_{vent,l} = 1.180 \; kW = 1180 \; W$$

**Total ventilation load:**

$$Q_{vent} = Q_{vent,s} + Q_{vent,l} = 470 + 1180$$

$$\boxed{Q_{vent} = 1650 \; W}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Ventilation air at outside conditions must be cooled *and* dehumidified. The sensible term handles temperature reduction; the latent term handles moisture removal. Both use the same mass flow rate $\dot{m}_v$ but different physical properties ($c_p$ vs $h_{fg}$).

---

#### Step 6 — Heat Gain from Infiltration

Cabin volume:

$$V_{cabin} = L \cdot W \cdot H_{eff} = 4.5 \times 1.8 \times 1.1 = 8.91 \; m^3$$

Infiltration volumetric flow:

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{ACH \cdot V_{cabin}}{3600} = \frac{2 \times 8.91}{3600}$$

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{17.82}{3600} = 0.00495 \; m^3/s = 4.95 \; L/s$$

Infiltration heat (sensible only — assume latent already covered by infiltration):

$$Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.00495 \times 1.005 \times (38 - 25)$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.00495 \times 1.005 \times 13$$

$$Q_{inf} = 0.00594 \times 1.005 \times 13$$

$$Q_{inf} = 0.00597 \times 13$$

$$Q_{inf} = 0.0776 \; kW \approx 78 \; W$$

(Including latent at 0.008 kg/kg humidity ratio difference, an additional ~96 W is added.)

$$\boxed{Q_{inf} \approx 174 \; W \; \text{(total, sensible + latent)}}$$

---

#### Step 7 — Heat Gain from Equipment (Firewall Radiation)

Given directly:

$$\boxed{Q_{equip} = 200 \; W}$$

---

#### Step 8 — Total Cooling Load — Summation

$$Q_{total,cooling} = Q_{wall} + Q_{glass} + Q_{occ} + Q_{vent} + Q_{inf} + Q_{equip}$$

$$Q_{total,cooling} = 2080.5 + 2555.76 + 400 + 1650 + 174 + 200$$

$$Q_{total,cooling} = 7060.26 \; W$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total,cooling} \approx 7.06 \; kW}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Heating Load for the Same Sedan in Winter

**Given Winter Conditions:**
- $T_o$ = **−5 °C**, RH = **80%**
- $T_i$ = **22 °C**
- No solar (night operation, conservative)
- Same vehicle, same areas, same U-values
- 4 occupants (subtracted as internal gain)
- Same ventilation and infiltration rates

---

#### Step 1 — Heat Loss Through Opaque Body

For heating mode, no sol-air correction (dark night, no radiation):

$$Q_{wall,H} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$

$$Q_{wall,H} = 2.5 \times 14.6 \times (22 - (-5))$$

$$Q_{wall,H} = 2.5 \times 14.6 \times 27$$

$$Q_{wall,H} = 36.5 \times 27 = 985.5 \; W$$

#### Step 2 — Heat Loss Through Glass (Conduction Only)

$$Q_{glass,H} = U_{glass} \cdot A_{glass} \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$

$$Q_{glass,H} = 5.8 \times 4.4 \times 27 = 689.04 \; W$$

#### Step 3 — Ventilation Heat Loss

$$\dot{m}_v = 0.036 \; kg/s \; (\text{same as before})$$

$$Q_{vent,H} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$

$$Q_{vent,H} = 0.036 \times 1.005 \times 27 = 0.977 \; kW = 977 \; W$$

#### Step 4 — Infiltration Heat Loss

$$Q_{inf,H} = 1.2 \times 0.00495 \times 1.005 \times 27 = 161 \; W$$

#### Step 5 — Subtract Internal Heat Gains (Occupants)

$$Q_{int,subtract} = 4 \times 100 = 400 \; W$$

#### Step 6 — Total Heating Load

$$Q_{heat} = Q_{wall,H} + Q_{glass,H} + Q_{vent,H} + Q_{inf,H} - Q_{int,subtract}$$

$$Q_{heat} = 985.5 + 689.04 + 977 + 161 - 400$$

$$Q_{heat} = 2412.54 \; W$$

$$\boxed{Q_{heat} \approx 2.41 \; kW}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling vs Heating Load — Side-by-Side Analysis for the Same Vehicle**

| Parameter | Cooling (Summer) | Heating (Winter) |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant load | **Solar gain through glass** (≈ 36%) | **Ventilation loss** (≈ 40%) |
| $Q_{envelope}$ | 2080 W (with sol-air) | 985 W (no solar) |
| $Q_{glass}$ | 2556 W (solar-dominated) | 689 W (conduction only) |
| $Q_{ventilation}$ | 1650 W (sensible + latent) | 977 W (sensible only) |
| $Q_{occupants}$ | +400 W (added) | −400 W (subtracted) |
| **Total** | **≈ 7.06 kW** | **≈ 2.41 kW** |
| System response time | Critical (minutes) | Moderate |
| Equipment sized for | Compressor, condenser, evaporator | Heater core, blower |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The summer cooling load is **~3× the winter heating load** for the same vehicle. This is because solar gain (which is absent in winter) and latent dehumidification (which adds ~70% to ventilation load in humid climates) inflate the cooling load dramatically.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Heat Flow Pathways into Automobile Cabin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun["Solar Radiation 800 W per m2"]:::source
    Tout["Outside Air 38 C"]:::source
    Wind["Ram Air Effect"]:::source
    Engine["Engine Bay 200 W"]:::source
    Occup["Occupants 4 x 100 W"]:::source

    Sun -->|"Solar Through Glass"| Glass["Glazing Area 4.4 m2 SHGC 0.45 to 0.70"]
    Sun -->|"Solar on Body"| Wall["Body Panels 14.6 m2 alpha s 0.55"]
    Tout -->|"Conduction"| Glass
    Tout -->|"Conduction"| Wall
    Wind -->|"Infiltration"| Cabin["CABIN INTERIOR 25 C 50 RH"]
    Engine -->|"Firewall Radiation"| Cabin
    Occup -->|"Metabolic Heat"| Cabin

    Glass --> Cabin
    Wall -->|"Sol Air 82 C"| Cabin

    Cabin --> AC["HVAC SYSTEM"]
    AC -->|"Refrigerant Cycle"| Cool["Cooling Coil Evaporator"]
    AC -->|"Heater Core"| Heat["Heating Mode"]

    classDef source fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sink fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Cabin Cross-Section Showing Load Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CABIN["AUTOMOBILE PASSENGER CABIN"]
        direction TB
        O1["Occupant 1"]:::occ
        O2["Occupant 2"]:::occ
        O3["Occupant 3"]:::occ
        O4["Occupant 4"]:::occ

        FW["Firewall Engine Side"]:::struct
        ROOF["Roof Panel U 2.5"]:::struct
        FLOOR["Floor Pan U 2.5"]:::struct
        WS["Windshield Laminated SHGC 0.45"]:::glass
        SW["Side Windows Tempered SHGC 0.70"]:::glass
        RW["Rear Window SHGC 0.70"]:::glass

        O1 -. "100 W" .-> CABIN
        O2 -. "100 W" .-> CABIN
        O3 -. "100 W" .-> CABIN
        O4 -. "100 W" .-> CABIN
        FW -. "200 W Radiation" .-> CABIN
    end

    SUN["Solar I 800 W per m2 alpha 0.55"]:::external
    AIR["Outside Air 38 C"]:::external

    SUN -->|"S"| WS
    SUN -->|"S"| SW
    SUN -->|"S"| RW
    SUN -->|"S Absorbed"| ROOF
    AIR -->|"Conduction"| ROOF
    AIR -->|"Conduction"| FLOOR
    AIR -->|"Conduction"| WS
    AIR -->|"Conduction"| SW
    AIR -->|"Conduction"| RW

    classDef occ fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef struct fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px
    classDef glass fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef external fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Block Diagrams:**
> - **Input blocks** (blue) feed into **calculation blocks** (orange).
> - All six calculation blocks converge at the **SUMMATION** stage.
