> - The summation branches based on mode (summer/winter) and feeds **equipment sizing** outputs (green).
> - The cabin cross-section shows geometrically which envelope elements contribute to the load.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Sol-Air Temperature" and explain its role in the cooling load calculation of an automobile body panel.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Sol-Air Temperature** is a fictitious outdoor surface temperature that combines the actual outdoor air temperature with the effect of incident solar radiation absorbed by the surface. It is defined mathematically as:

$$T_{sol-air} = T_{outdoor} + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o} - \frac{\epsilon \cdot \Delta R}{h_o}$$

For an automobile, it serves two critical purposes:
1. **Simplifies the conduction equation** by replacing solar flux + convection into a single equivalent temperature, allowing use of the standard $Q = U A \Delta T$ formula.
2. **Represents realistic peak conditions** — for a silver-painted car in tropical sun, $T_{sol-air}$ can reach **70 °C to 90 °C** even when ambient is only 38 °C, dramatically increasing the cooling load.

The term is essential for opaque panels (roof, doors, hood, trunk); it does not apply to glass, where solar radiation passes through rather than being absorbed at the surface. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six heat gain components that contribute to the cooling load of an automobile passenger cabin.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six major heat gain components are:
1. **Heat conduction through opaque body panels** (roof, doors, floor)
2. **Heat conduction and solar gain through glazing** (windshield, windows)
3. **Heat from occupants** (sensible + latent metabolic heat)
4. **Heat from ventilation air** (sensible + latent)
5. **Heat from infiltration** (leakage through seals)
6. **Heat from internal equipment** (engine bay radiation, electronics)

A complete cooling load calculation sums all six components to determine the required refrigeration capacity. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

### 5.2 Part B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** (full internal choice as per KTU 2024 scheme).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for calculating the **cooling load of an automobile**, listing all heat gain components with their respective formulas. State clearly the design assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a compact car with cabin volume 6.5 m³, glazing area 3.2 m² (SHGC = 0.65), opaque body area 11 m² (U = 2.8 W/(m²·K), $\alpha_s$ = 0.6), outside design conditions 40 °C / 60% RH with solar radiation 900 W/m², inside conditions 25 °C / 50% RH, 3 occupants, and ventilation of 25 L/s, compute the **total cooling load**. Use $h_o$ = 12 W/(m²·K) and $h_{fg}$ = 2460 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Formulas (7 Marks):**

The cooling load of an automobile is computed by summing six independent heat gain components. The standard procedure is:

**Step 1 — Identify the envelope areas:** Catalog total opaque area $A_w$, total glazing area $A_g$, broken into subcategories (windshield, sides, rear, sunroof), each with its own SHGC.

**Step 2 — Determine design conditions:** Specify $T_o$, RH_o, $T_i$, RH_i, and solar intensity $I_t$ on each surface orientation.

**Step 3 — Compute sol-air temperature** for opaque body panels:
$$T_{sol-air} = T_o + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat gain components:**
- Opaque conduction: $Q_w = U_w A_w (T_{sol-air} - T_i)$
- Glass conduction: $Q_{gc} = U_g A_g (T_o - T_i)$
- Glass solar gain: $Q_{gs} = A_g \cdot SHGC \cdot I_t$ (summed per glazing type)
- Occupants: $Q_{occ} = n \cdot 100 \; W$
- Ventilation: $Q_v = \dot{m}_v [c_p (T_o - T_i) + (W_o - W_i) h_{fg}]$
- Infiltration: $Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot (ACH \cdot V/3600) \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- Equipment: $Q_{eq}$ (given, e.g., firewall radiation)

**Step 5 — Total cooling load:**
$$Q_{total} = Q_w + Q_{gc} + Q_{gs} + Q_{occ} + Q_v + Q_{inf} + Q_{eq}$$

**Design assumptions** (typical): $h_o$ = 6 to 30 W/(m²·K), $\alpha_s$ depends on paint colour, $T_i$ = 25 °C, ACH = 2, occupant metabolic rate = 100 W per person. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sol-air temperature:**
$$T_{sol-air} = 40 + \frac{0.6 \times 900}{12} = 40 + 45 = 85 \; °C$$

**Step 2 — Opaque conduction:**
$$Q_w = 2.8 \times 11 \times (85 - 25) = 2.8 \times 11 \times 60 = 1848 \; W$$

**Step 3 — Glass conduction:**
$$Q_{gc} = 5.8 \times 3.2 \times (40 - 25) = 5.8 \times 3.2 \times 15 = 278.4 \; W$$

**Step 4 — Glass solar gain:**
$$Q_{gs} = 3.2 \times 0.65 \times 900 = 1872 \; W$$

**Step 5 — Occupants:**
$$Q_{occ} = 3 \times 100 = 300 \; W$$

**Step 6 — Ventilation:**
$$\dot{m}_v = 1.2 \times 0.025 = 0.030 \; kg/s$$

At 40 °C, 60% RH: $W_o \approx 0.0273 \; kg/kg$
At 25 °C, 50% RH: $W_i \approx 0.00988 \; kg/kg$

$$Q_{v,s} = 0.030 \times 1.005 \times (40 - 25) = 0.452 \; kW = 452 \; W$$

$$Q_{v,l} = 0.030 \times (0.0273 - 0.00988) \times 2460 = 0.030 \times 0.01742 \times 2460 = 1286 \; W$$

$$Q_v = 452 + 1286 = 1738 \; W$$

**Step 7 — Infiltration:**
$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{2 \times 6.5}{3600} = 0.00361 \; m^3/s$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.00361 \times 1.005 \times 15 = 65 \; W \; \text{(sensible)}$$

Adding latent (≈ 80 W): $Q_{inf} \approx 145 \; W$

**Step 8 — Total cooling load:**
$$Q_{total} = 1848 + 278.4 + 1872 + 300 + 1738 + 145$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 6.18 \; kW}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for calculating the **heating load of an automobile** for winter conditions. Compare and contrast it with the cooling load procedure, highlighting the differences in dominant load components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedan operates in winter with outside temperature −7 °C, inside setpoint 22 °C, cabin volume 9 m³, glass area 4 m² (U = 5.8 W/(m²·K)), opaque area 14 m² (U = 2.5 W/(m²·K)), 4 occupants, and ventilation 30 L/s. Compute the **total heating load** including a safety margin of 10%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Comparison (7 Marks):**

The heating load procedure is structurally similar to cooling load but with **opposite signs** and certain load components removed or reduced.

**Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Determine outside/inside temperatures:** $T_o$ is low (often negative in cold climates), $T_i$ = 20–22 °C.

**Step 2 — Compute envelope heat losses:**
- Opaque walls: $Q_w = U_w A_w (T_i - T_o)$
- Glass conduction: $Q_g = U_g A_g (T_i - T_o)$ — no solar term at night

**Step 3 — Compute ventilation loss:**
$$Q_v = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$
(No latent term — cold outside air is usually dry.)

**Step 4 — Compute infiltration loss:**
$$Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot (ACH \cdot V/3600) \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$

**Step 5 — Subtract internal gains:**
- Occupants: 100 W per person (subtracted)
- Solar gain (if daytime): subtracted
- Engine waste heat (if running): partially subtracted

**Step 6 — Total heating load:**
$$Q_{heat} = Q_w + Q_g + Q_v + Q_{inf} - Q_{int,gains}$$

**Differences from Cooling Load Procedure:**

| Aspect | Cooling | Heating |
|---|---|---|
| Solar gain | **Major contributor** (often 30–40%) | Subtracts if present |
| Latent load | **Significant** (dehumidification) | Usually absent |
| $T_{sol-air}$ | Required | Not used (night-time) |
| Occupant load | Added | Subtracted |
| Equipment load | Engine bay radiation added | Engine heat subtracted |
| Magnitude | **3–4× higher** | Lower |

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Opaque heat loss:**
$$Q_w = 2.5 \times 14 \times (22 - (-7)) = 2.5 \times 14 \times 29 = 1015 \; W$$

**Step 2 — Glass heat loss:**
$$Q_g = 5.8 \times 4 \times 29 = 672.8 \; W$$

**Step 3 — Ventilation heat loss:**
$$\dot{m}_v = 1.2 \times 0.030 = 0.036 \; kg/s$$

$$Q_v = 0.036 \times 1.005 \times 29 = 1.049 \; kW = 1049 \; W$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration heat loss (assume 2 ACH):**
$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{2 \times 9}{3600} = 0.005 \; m^3/s$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.005 \times 1.005 \times 29 = 175 \; W$$

**Step 5 — Subtract occupant gains:**
$$Q_{int} = 4 \times 100 = 400 \; W$$

**Step 6 — Gross heating load:**
$$Q_{heat,gross} = 1015 + 672.8 + 1049 + 175 - 400 = 2511.8 \; W$$

**Step 7 — Apply 10% safety margin:**
$$Q_{heat,final} = 2511.8 \times 1.10$$

$$\boxed{Q_{heat,final} \approx 2763 \; W \approx 2.76 \; kW}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting humidity ratio conversion** in ventilation latent load — students often use $(RH_o - RH_i) \cdot h_{fg}$ instead of $(W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}$. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU paper valuations.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using W/m²·°C and K interchangeably is correct numerically, but writing U in BTU/hr·ft²·°F causes arithmetic errors.
> 3. **Ignoring sol-air temperature** for dark-coloured vehicles — examiners specifically check if you apply $\alpha_s \cdot I_t / h_o$ for body panels.
> 4. **Double-counting occupants** — once in sensible and again in latent is correct; but adding them with the wrong sign for heating mode loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping infiltration** in "approximate" problems — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if this term is missing.
> 6. **Not splitting glass SHGC** for windshield vs side windows — modern laminated windshields have lower SHGC than tempered side glass.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cooling & Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles**

- ✅ **Total Cooling Load** = $Q_{wall} + Q_{glass} + Q_{occ} + Q_{vent} + Q_{inf} + Q_{eq}$
- ✅ **Total Heating Load** = $Q_{losses} - Q_{internal\;gains}$
- ✅ **Sol-air temperature** is required **only for opaque panels**, not glass.
- ✅ **SHGC** quantifies the fraction of solar radiation that passes through glass and becomes heat inside the cabin.
- ✅ **Automotive glass is a major load** because it has high SHGC and large area — up to 60% of envelope.
- ✅ **Ventilation has two parts**: sensible ($c_p \Delta T$) and latent ($h_{fg} \Delta W$) — both must be included for cooling.
- ✅ **Heating load excludes latent term** — cold outside air is typically dry.
- ✅ **Infiltration** modeled via ACH: $\dot{V}_{inf} = ACH \cdot V_{cabin} / 3600$
- ✅ **Physical constants to memorize:** $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³, $h_{fg}$ = 2460 kJ/kg
- ✅ **Typical design conditions:** $T_i$ = 25 °C (cool), 22 °C (heat), 50% RH (cool)
- ✅ **Cooling load ≈ 3 to 4× heating load** for the same vehicle due to solar gain and latent dehumidification.
- ✅ **Occupant metabolic heat**: 60 W sensible + 40 W latent = 100 W per person (seated, 25 °C).
- ✅ **Firewall radiation** from engine bay adds **100 to 500 W** — often the largest "equipment" term.
- ✅ **The cooling load drives compressor sizing**; the heating load drives **heater core sizing** and **blower airflow**.
- ✅ **A safety margin of 5–10%** is recommended on the final design value for both heating and cooling.
- ✅ **U-values to remember**: opaque body ≈ 2.5 W/(m²·K), laminated glass ≈ 5.8 W/(m²·K), single-pane glass ≈ 5.7 W/(m²·K).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

The **Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance** refers to the measurable degradation in an internal combustion engine's output characteristics caused by the parasitic mechanical demand imposed by the automotive air conditioning (A/C) compressor. When the A/C system is engaged, the compressor — typically belt-driven off the crankshaft — extracts a portion of the engine's indicated work, which manifests as a reduction in **brake power (kW)**, **brake torque (N·m)**, and an increase in **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC, g/kWh)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this phenomenon is quantified under three primary performance parameters:

$$
\Delta P_{engine} = P_{AC,\,compressor} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_{BSFC,\,AC} > \eta_{BSFC,\,AC\,OFF}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT742 – Module 2):**
> The effect of A/C load must be analyzed under steady-state and transient engine operating conditions, including idle, part-throttle, and wide-open throttle (WOT) modes. The compressor is treated as a **parasitic auxiliary load** coupled to the crankshaft.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stolen Horsepower" Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle uphill. Your legs produce a fixed amount of energy per pedal stroke. Now, suppose you tie a small child to a sidecar that constantly pedals a hand-cranked fan. The fan is blowing cool air on you (comfort), but part of your leg-power is now diverted to spin the fan — you reach the top of the hill slower and tire faster.

In this analogy:
- **Your legs** = Engine crankshaft
- **The fan (sidecar)** = A/C compressor
- **Reaching the hill top** = Vehicle propulsion (acceleration, top speed)
- **Tiring faster** = Increased fuel consumption
- **Cool air on your face** = Cabin cooling comfort

The fan doesn't add energy to the system; it *steals* a slice of the energy you already produce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used (per SAE J1349 & ISO 1585):**
> - **Power Loss**: typically **2 – 5 kW** for a passenger car A/C system.
> - **Fuel Penalty**: **+5 % to +20 %** in city driving cycles (UDDS, FTP-72).
> - **CO₂ Increment**: **+10 to +30 g/km** per vehicle.
> - **Idle RPM Compensation**: ECU raises idle by **+50 to +150 RPM**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Load Transfer — Bulleted Logic Flow

1. **Driver initiates A/C demand** → Cabin thermostat (or automatic climate ECU) calls for cooling.
2. **Compressor clutch engages** → Electromagnetic clutch couples the compressor pulley to the compressor input shaft, drawing torque via the serpentine belt.
3. **Refrigerant compression begins** → Low-pressure vapor (≈ 2 bar, 0 °C saturation) is compressed to high-pressure vapor (≈ 15–20 bar, 60–70 °C condensation).
4. **Torque demand on crankshaft rises** → Compressor absorbs shaft torque $T_{AC}$, computed via:

$$
T_{AC} = \frac{P_{AC}}{\omega_{engine}}
$$

5. **ECU compensates** → To maintain idle stability and prevent stalling, the Engine Control Unit (ECU):
   - Advances ignition timing slightly (where knock limits allow).
   - Increases throttle opening (idle air control valve).
   - Enriches the air-fuel mixture marginally.
6. **Net result at the wheels** → Reduced net propulsion torque; the vehicle accelerates slower, climbs grades slower, and consumes more fuel per kilometre.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Power Demand of the Compressor

The **theoretical isentropic compression work** (per kg of refrigerant) is:

$$
w_{isen} = h_{2s} - h_{1}
$$

The **actual compression work**, accounting for compressor isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen,\,comp}$, is:

$$
w_{actual} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{\eta_{isen,\,comp}}
$$

The **mechanical (shaft) power** required to drive the compressor is therefore:

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times (h_2 - h_1) \times \frac{1}{\eta_{mech,\,comp}}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{ref}$ = refrigerant mass flow rate (kg/s), typically **0.02 – 0.08 kg/s** for R-134a in passenger cars.
- $h_1, h_2$ = specific enthalpies at compressor inlet and outlet (kJ/kg).
- $\eta_{mech,\,comp}$ = mechanical efficiency of compressor (≈ **0.85 – 0.95**).

## 2.3 Effect on Engine Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

$$
BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{brake}} \quad \left[ \frac{g}{kW \cdot h} \right]
$$

When A/C engages, $P_{brake}$ falls while $\dot{m}_{f}$ rises → **BSFC worsens**.

$$
\frac{BSFC_{AC}}{BSFC_{no\,AC}} \approx 1.05 \text{ to } 1.20
$$

## 2.4 Additional Fuel Burned to Compensate A/C Load

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,AC} = \dot{m}_{f,\,base} + \frac{P_{comp}}{\eta_{th,\,engine} \times LHV_{fuel}}
$$

For **gasoline**: $LHV \approx$ **44 MJ/kg**.
For **diesel**: $LHV \approx$ **43 MJ/kg**.

## 2.5 Engine Performance Loss Equation

$$
P_{available,\,wheels} = P_{engine,\,brake} - P_{comp} - P_{alt} - P_{water\,pump} - P_{other\,aux}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The A/C compressor is the **single largest parasitic load** on a passenger-car engine after the alternator.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Typical Magnitude / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor shaft power | $P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} (h_2 - h_1) / \eta_{mech}$ | **2 – 5 kW** |
| 2 | Compressor torque demand | $T_{AC} = P_{comp} / \omega_{engine}$ | **15 – 35 N·m** |
| 3 | Engine power reduction | $\Delta P = P_{comp} \times \eta_{transmission}$ | **2 – 4.5 kW at wheels** |
| 4 | BSFC penalty factor | $f_{BSFC} = BSFC_{AC} / BSFC_{no\,AC}$ | **1.05 – 1.20 (unitless)** |
| 5 | Extra fuel burned | $\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = P_{comp} / (\eta_{th} \times LHV)$ | **0.4 – 1.0 kg/h** |
| 6 | CO₂ increase | $\Delta CO_2 = \dot{m}_{f,\,extra} \times 3.2$ (gasoline stoich) | **+10 to +30 g/km** |
| 7 | 0–100 km/h time penalty | $\Delta t \approx 5\%-15\%$ | **+0.4 to +1.5 s** |
| 8 | Idle RPM increment | $\Delta N_{idle} = N_{idle,AC} - N_{idle,noAC}$ | **+50 to +150 RPM** |
| 9 | Carnot COP reference | $COP_{Carnot} = T_{evap} / (T_{cond} - T_{evap})$ | **4 – 6 (unitless)** |
| 10 | Real COP (R-134a) | $COP_{real} = Q_{evap} / W_{comp}$ | **2.0 – 3.0 (unitless)** |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai, Toyota) use this analysis to size compressors against engine displacement.
- **Hybrid/EV designers** replace the belt-driven compressor with a **high-voltage electric compressor** (HV-EC) to decouple A/C load from the ICE.
- **Emission certification engineers** must quantify A/C-induced CO₂ for **WLTP** and **EPA FTP** test cycles.
- **After-treatment designers** ensure catalyst light-off timing accounts for A/C-induced exhaust perturbations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Compressor Power from First-Principle Thermodynamics

### Step 1 — Define Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate

For a positive-displacement (swash-plate or scroll) compressor, the displaced volume per unit time is:

$$
\dot{V}_{disp} = V_{disp} \times n_{comp}
$$

Where $n_{comp}$ is compressor rotational speed. The actual refrigerant mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \eta_{vol} \times \rho_{1} \times \dot{V}_{disp}
$$

Here $\rho_1$ is the density of refrigerant vapor at the compressor inlet (suction).

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to Compressor Control Volume

Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes:

$$
w_{shaft,in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

For a real (non-isentropic) compressor with isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen}$:

$$
w_{shaft,in} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen,\,comp}}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by Mass Flow Rate to Obtain Shaft Power

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen,\,comp} \times \eta_{mech,\,comp}}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Practical Form Using Pressure Ratio

For an ideal gas approximation and using the polytropic relation $pV^k = const$:

$$
w_{isen} = \frac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1 \right]
$$

Substituting into the power expression:

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times \frac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1 \right] \times \frac{1}{\eta_{overall}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **R-134a**, due to its non-ideal behaviour near saturation, the **polytropic exponent $k$** is replaced by a refrigerant-specific compressibility-corrected value, typically yielding $k_{eff} \approx 1.15$ to $1.20$ (vs $1.30$ for an ideal diatomic gas).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Model Problem)

> **Given:** A 1.5 L petrol engine delivers $P_{brake} = 75 \, kW$ at 5500 RPM. The A/C compressor draws $\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.04 \, kg/s$ of R-134a. The enthalpy rise across the compressor is $h_2 - h_1 = 60 \, kJ/kg$. Compressor mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 0.90$. Engine thermal efficiency at this load $\eta_{th} = 0.32$. LHV of gasoline = **44 MJ/kg**.
>
> **Find:** (a) Compressor power demand, (b) Percentage engine power loss, (c) Additional fuel consumption rate, (d) New BSFC vs original.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**(a) Compressor shaft power:**

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times \frac{(h_2 - h_1)}{\eta_{mech}}
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 0.04 \times \frac{60}{0.90}
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 0.04 \times 66.667
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 2.667 \, kW
$$

**(b) Percentage engine power loss:**

$$
\% \, P_{loss} = \frac{P_{comp}}{P_{brake}} \times 100 = \frac{2.667}{75} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \, P_{loss} = 3.56 \, \%
$$

**(c) Additional fuel consumption rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\eta_{th} \times LHV}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = \frac{2.667 \, kJ/s}{0.32 \times 44000 \, kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = \frac{2.667}{14080}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = 1.894 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/s
$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = 1.894 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 0.682 \, kg/h
$$

**(d) BSFC penalty:**

Original BSFC (assume base fuel flow = 6 kg/h for 75 kW):

$$
BSFC_{no\,AC} = \frac{6000}{75} = 80 \, g/kWh
$$

New BSFC:

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \frac{6000 + 682}{75 - 2.667} = \frac{6682}{72.333}
$$

$$
BSFC_{AC} = 92.38 \, g/kWh
$$

Penalty:

$$
f_{BSFC} = \frac{92.38}{80} = 1.155 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{+15.5\% BSFC}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Even a modest 2.67 kW compressor load causes a **15.5% jump in BSFC** because the denominator (effective brake power) shrinks faster than the numerator (fuel flow) rises. This is the core numerical fingerprint examiners expect KTU students to demonstrate.

## 3.3 Effect on Engine Torque and Acceleration

$$
F_{tractive} = \frac{T_{wheels}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{(T_{engine} - T_{AC}) \times \eta_{drivetrain}}{r_{wheel}}
$$

For a 0–100 km/h run, the additional time penalty is approximated as:

$$
\frac{\Delta t}{t_0} \approx \frac{P_{comp}}{P_{net,\,propulsion}} \times \frac{1}{1 - \left(\frac{V_{final}}{V_{final,\,no\,AC}}\right)^2}
$$

For a typical mid-size sedan, this evaluates to **5% – 12%** longer 0–100 km/h time.

## 3.4 Transient Behaviour During A/C Engagement (Idle Stability)

When the clutch engages at idle, an instantaneous torque step $\Delta T \approx 15$–$25 \, N \cdot m$ is applied. Without ECU compensation, this would cause RPM dip of:

$$
\Delta N \approx \frac{\Delta T \times 60}{2\pi \times J_{engine}}
$$

For $J_{engine} \approx 0.15 \, kg \cdot m^2$, the dip without compensation can be **200–400 RPM** — enough to stall a small engine. Hence, the **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** opens within 50–100 ms to restore equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Engine ↔ A/C Compressor Load Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demands Cabin Cooling] --> B[Cabin Thermostat ECU Call]
    B --> C[A/C Clutch Energized]
    C --> D[Compressor Pulley Coupled to Crankshaft]
    D --> E[Refrigerant Compression R134a]
    E --> F[Shaft Torque Demand TAC on Engine]
    F --> G[Engine Brake Torque Reduced]
    G --> H[ECU Increases Throttle Opening]
    H --> I[Extra Fuel Injected]
    I --> J[Fuel Consumption Rises]
    G --> K[Wheel Power Reduced]
    K --> L[Acceleration Top Speed Climb Reduced]
    J --> M[BSFC Worsens by 5 to 20 percent]
    L --> N[Vehicle Net Performance Penalty]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Parasitic Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG[Engine Subsystem]
        A1[Combustion Energy Source]
        A2[Crankshaft Output]
        A3[Brake Power Measured]
    end
    subgraph AUX[Auxiliary Loads]
        B1[A/C Compressor]
        B2[Alternator]
        B3[Water Pump]
        B4[Power Steering]
    end
    subgraph DRV[Drivetrain]
        C1[Transmission]
        C2[Differential]
        C3[Wheels]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A2 -. Parasitic Take-off .-> B1
    A2 -. Parasitic Take-off .-> B2
    A2 -. Parasitic Take-off .-> B3
    A2 -. Parasitic Take-off .-> B4
    A3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Effect Cascade on Performance Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[A/C Switch ON] --> P2[Compressor Engaged]
    P2 --> P3[Compressor Power Demand Pcomp]
    P3 --> P4[Engine Power Reduced deltaP]
    P3 --> P5[Fuel Flow Increased delta m f]
    P4 --> P6[BSFC Worsens]
    P5 --> P6
    P4 --> P7[Acceleration Slower]
    P4 --> P8[Climb Performance Reduced]
    P4 --> P9[Idle RPM Increase]
    P6 --> P10[CO2 Emissions Rise]
    P7 --> P10
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Net Vehicle Performance Penalty]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Notes

- **Solid arrows** = energy / information flow.
- **Dotted arrows** = parasitic mechanical take-off from the crankshaft.
- The **A/C compressor** is shown as the *largest* parasitic node, reflecting its typical **2–5 kW** demand.
- In **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, the belt path is broken — an **electric scroll compressor** is fed from the **HV battery**, completely decoupling A/C from propulsion torque.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "parasitic load" in the context of an automotive air-conditioning system. List two auxiliary loads that compete with the A/C compressor for engine power.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *parasitic load* is any auxiliary device driven mechanically by the engine crankshaft that consumes a portion of the engine's brake power without contributing to vehicle propulsion. The A/C compressor is the largest such load in a passenger car (≈ 2–5 kW). Two other parasitic loads are: **(i)** the **alternator** (≈ 0.5–1.5 kW), and **(ii)** the **power steering pump** (≈ 0.3–0.8 kW). The water pump, oil pump, and radiator fan are additional parasitic loads. (3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why does engaging the A/C system cause the idle RPM of an engine to increase? Mention the role of the ECU.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When the A/C compressor engages, it draws an additional torque of 15–25 N·m from the crankshaft. Without compensation, this would cause the idle speed to drop by 200–400 RPM and potentially stall the engine. The **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** detects the load via the A/C request signal and **increases the idle air flow** through the Idle Air Control Valve (IACV) and adjusts the fuel injection to raise the idle speed by **50–150 RPM**, thereby maintaining engine stability and torque equilibrium. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. A(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain how the air-conditioning compressor is coupled to the engine and discuss the energy flow path. **(7 marks)**

**Q. A(b)** A 1.2 L petrol engine develops 60 kW at 5500 RPM. The A/C compressor draws 0.035 kg/s of R-134a with an enthalpy rise of 55 kJ/kg. Compressor mechanical efficiency is 0.88. Compute (i) compressor shaft power, (ii) percentage engine power loss, and (iii) BSFC penalty if base BSFC = 85 g/kWh and engine thermal efficiency = 30%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

The compressor is mounted on the engine block and driven by a **serpentine belt** (multi-ribbed V-belt) looped over the crankshaft pulley. The belt also drives the alternator, power-steering pump, and idler pulleys. Between the compressor pulley and its input shaft sits an **electromagnetic clutch** controlled by the ECU. When the cabin thermostat calls for cooling, the clutch plate magnetizes and locks, transmitting torque to the compressor scroll/swash-plate mechanism. **Energy flow path:**

$$
E_{fuel} \rightarrow E_{combustion} \rightarrow E_{crankshaft} \rightarrow E_{belt} \rightarrow E_{compressor\,shaft} \rightarrow E_{refrigerant\,compression}
$$

The remaining crankshaft energy proceeds to the transmission, differential, and wheels. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**(i) Compressor shaft power:**

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times \frac{(h_2 - h_1)}{\eta_{mech}} = 0.035 \times \frac{55}{0.88}
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 0.035 \times 62.5 = 2.1875 \, kW
$$

**(ii) Percentage power loss:**

$$
\% \, P_{loss} = \frac{2.1875}{60} \times 100 = 3.65 \, \%
$$

**(iii) BSFC penalty:**

Extra fuel rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\eta_{th} \times LHV} = \frac{2.1875}{0.30 \times 44000} = 1.657 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/s
$$

Converting to g/h:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = 1.657 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 596.6 \, g/h
$$

Net brake power with A/C:

$$
P_{brake,\,AC} = 60 - 2.1875 = 57.8125 \, kW
$$

Original fuel rate (using BSFC = 85 g/kWh):

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,base} = 85 \times 60 = 5100 \, g/h
$$

New fuel rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,new} = 5100 + 596.6 = 5696.6 \, g/h
$$

New BSFC:

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \frac{5696.6}{57.8125} = 98.54 \, g/kWh
$$

Penalty:

$$
f_{BSFC} = \frac{98.54}{85} = 1.159 \quad \Rightarrow \quad +15.9\% \text{ BSFC}
$$

(7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. B(a)** Explain, with suitable equations, how engaging the A/C system affects (i) engine brake torque, (ii) brake specific fuel consumption, and (iii) CO₂ emissions. **(7 marks)**

**Q. B(b)** A vehicle fitted with a 2.0 L diesel engine (LHV = 43 MJ/kg) shows an A/C-induced fuel consumption increase of 0.55 kg/h. The engine thermal efficiency is 35%. Estimate the compressor shaft power demand and the percentage of total fuel energy diverted to the A/C system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**(i) Effect on brake torque:** The compressor absorbs a torque $T_{AC}$ that subtracts from the engine's indicated torque.

$$
T_{AC} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\omega_{engine}}
$$

Hence, net brake torque becomes:

$$
T_{brake,\,AC} = T_{brake,\,no\,AC} - T_{AC} = T_{brake,\,no\,AC} - \frac{P_{comp}}{\omega_{engine}}
$$

Typical reduction is **3–8%** at rated speed.

**(ii) Effect on BSFC:**

$$
BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}
$$

Since $\dot{m}_f$ rises and $P_{brake}$ falls, BSFC worsens by 5–20%.

**(iii) Effect on CO₂ emissions:** For gasoline (stoichiometric combustion), each kg of fuel produces ≈ **3.2 kg of CO₂**. Therefore:

$$
\Delta CO_2 = \dot{m}_{f,\,extra} \times 3.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{typical} \, +10 \text{ to } +30 \, g/km
$$

(7 marks)

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**(i) Compressor shaft power:**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\,extra} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\eta_{th} \times LHV}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{f,\,extra} \times \eta_{th} \times LHV
$$

$$
P_{comp} = \frac{0.55}{3600} \, kg/s \times 0.35 \times 43000 \, kJ/kg
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 1.528 \times 10^{-4} \times 15050
$$

$$
P_{comp} = 2.30 \, kW
$$

**(ii) Percentage of total fuel energy diverted to A/C:**

Total fuel energy rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{f,\,extra} \times LHV = \frac{0.55}{3600} \times 43000 = 6.57 \, kW
$$

Percentage of fuel energy reaching compressor (after engine efficiency):

$$
\% \, \text{diverted} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} \times 100 = \frac{2.30}{6.57} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \, \text{diverted} = 35.0 \, \%
$$

Alternatively, the rest of the input fuel energy (65%) goes to other parasitic loads, drivetrain losses, or is recovered as wheel work.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Forgetting to convert kg/h → kg/s when computing compressor power from fuel rate. *Always check units first.*
> 2. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency**: Using $\eta_{mech} = 1.0$ in the compressor power formula leads to a 10–15% under-prediction.
> 3. **Sign confusion in torque equation**: $T_{brake,AC} = T_{brake,noAC} - T_{AC}$, *not* the reverse. Writing the wrong sign loses 2 marks instantly.
> 4. **Mixing up BSFC definition**: Writing BSFC as $\dot{m}_f / P_{indicated}$ instead of $P_{brake}$ is a common error — KTU strictly uses brake values.
> 5. **Forgetting LHV of diesel ≠ gasoline**: Diesel ≈ 43 MJ/kg, Gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg. Numerical answers differ by 2–3%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **A/C load is a parasitic mechanical load** driven off the crankshaft via a serpentine belt and electromagnetic clutch.
- **Compressor power demand** ranges from **2 to 5 kW** in a typical passenger car; this is **3–6% of rated engine power**.
- **Key formula:** $P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref} \times (h_2 - h_1) / \eta_{mech}$.
- **BSFC worsens by 5–20%** when A/C is ON because both numerator (fuel) rises and denominator (brake power) falls.
- **Fuel penalty** is computed via $\dot{m}_{f,extra} = P_{comp} / (\eta_{th} \times LHV)$.
- **Idle RPM rises by 50–150 RPM** via ECU intervention through the IACV to prevent stalling.
- **Acceleration (0–100 km/h) suffers by 5–15%** in typical mid-size sedans.
- **CO₂ emissions rise by 10–30 g/km** depending on driving cycle.
- **EVs use electric scroll compressors** to fully decouple A/C from propulsion — a critical design distinction from ICE vehicles.
- **Typical LHV values to memorise:** Gasoline = **44 MJ/kg**, Diesel = **43 MJ/kg**.
- **Always convert** kg/h → kg/s (÷3600) before using SI power formulas.
- **Refrigerant R-134a** is the legacy standard; **R-1234yf** is the modern low-GWP replacement mandated in the EU since 2017.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerants: Classification, Properties, and Selection Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refrigerants: Classification, Properties, and Selection Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — REFRIGERANTS: CLASSIFICATION, PROPERTIES, AND SELECTION CRITERIA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigerant (KTU Definition):**
> A *refrigerant* is a working fluid that undergoes a reversible thermodynamic phase-change cycle within a refrigeration system. It absorbs heat from a low-temperature reservoir (evaporator) at low pressure and rejects heat to a high-temperature reservoir (condenser) at high pressure, thereby transporting thermal energy against its natural gradient.

The fluid must possess a saturation curve that lies *between* the evaporator and condenser temperatures at practical operating pressures, allowing it to **boil** in the evaporator and **condense** in the condenser.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salaried Worker of the Refrigeration Cycle"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — The Refrigerant as a Heat Mover**
> Imagine a **salaried delivery person** who picks up a heavy parcel (heat) at the *cold shop* (evaporator) using **minimum effort** (low-pressure evaporation), carries it across town, and unloads it at the *hot warehouse* (condenser) by working slightly harder (high-pressure condensation). After unloading, the cycle repeats. The **refrigerant is that delivery worker**, and the *parcel* is **heat (Q)**.
>
> - If the worker is too *weak* (low latent heat $h_{fg}$), they must make many trips → low **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.
> - If the worker is *too heavy* (high molecular weight), they consume extra fuel → high compressor work.
> - If the worker is *toxic* or *flammable*, the delivery van (system) becomes unsafe.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** |
| Ozone Depletion Potential | ODP | Reference: R-11 = **1.0** |
| Global Warming Potential | GWP | Reference: CO₂ = **1.0** (100-yr horizon) |
| Normal Boiling Point | NBP | At **101.325 kPa** |
| Critical Temperature | $T_{cr}$ | Above which liquefaction impossible |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Refrigerants are characterized by their *ASHRAE 34* designation (e.g., R-134a, R-410A). The number encodes chemical composition — useful for board-exam numerical identification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Refrigerants

### 2.1.1 Primary (Primary Refrigerants vs. Secondary Refrigerants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Refrigerant** — Undergoes the actual phase-change cycle (R-134a, NH₃, CO₂).
> **Secondary Refrigerant** — Remains in single phase; acts as a *heat carrier* (brine, glycol-water, chilled water).

### 2.1.2 Detailed Chemical Classification

| Family | Chemical Root | Examples | KTU Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CFC** (Chlorofluorocarbon) | Chloro + Fluoro + Carbon | R-11, R-12, R-502 | **Banned** (Montreal 1987) |
| **HCFC** (Hydrochlorofluorocarbon) | Hydrogen + Chloro + Fluoro + Carbon | R-22, R-123 | **Phased out** (developing nations 2030, 2040) |
| **HFC** (Hydrofluorocarbon) | Hydrogen + Fluoro + Carbon | R-134a, R-410A, R-407C | **Current** — High GWP, transitional |
| **HFO** (Hydrofluoroolefin) | Unsaturated HFC | R-1234yf, R-1234ze | **Future** — Low GWP |
| **Natural Refrigerants** | Inorganic / Hydrocarbons | NH₃ (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), Propane (R-290) | **Revival** |
| **Zeotropic Blends** | Glide during phase change | R-407C, R-404A | Non-zero *temperature glide* |
| **Azeotropic Blends** | Behaves like pure fluid | R-502, R-507A | Negligible glide |

### 2.1.3 ASHRAE Nomenclature Logic (KTU Favorite Question)

For a saturated hydrocarbon derivative (Halogen numbering):
- Compound: $\text{C}_m \text{H}_n \text{F}_p \text{Cl}_q \text{Br}_r$
- **Last digit of code** = number of Fluorine atoms = $p$
- **Second-to-last digit** = number of Hydrogen atoms + 1 = $n + 1$
- **Third digit** = number of Carbon atoms − 1 = $m - 1$
- Chlorine is calculated by **valence balance**: $q = 2m + 2 - n - p$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Trick:** For **methane-based (1 carbon)** refrigerants, the code is **three digits**. For **ethane-based (2 carbons)**, a leading **"1"** is added.

### 2.2 Required Thermophysical & Environmental Properties

A *good* refrigerant must satisfy a multi-objective property matrix. KTU examiners love framing this as a *Selection Problem*.

> [!NOTE]
> **A. Thermodynamic Properties**
> 1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (e.g., R-134a = $-26.3 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$)
> 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** $h_{fg}$ → less mass flow $\dot{m}$ per kW cooling
> 3. **Low specific volume of vapor** → small compressor displacement
> 4. **Moderate condensing pressure** → safe, thin-walled equipment
> 5. **High critical temperature** → large pressure-temperature slope above $T_{cr}$ avoided
> 6. **Low freezing point** → solidification in evaporator tubes prevented

> [!NOTE]
> **B. Chemical & Safety Properties**
> 7. **Chemical stability** — no decomposition in cycle
> 8. **Non-corrosive** to copper, steel, aluminum
> 9. **Non-flammable** (ASHRAE A1 = no flame propagation, low toxicity)
> 10. **Low toxicity** (ASHRAE Class B = low toxicity; A = no toxicity)

> [!NOTE]
> **C. Environmental & Practical Properties**
> 11. **Zero / Low ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential)
> 12. **Zero / Low GWP** (Global Warming Potential)
> 13. **Good miscibility with lubricating oil**
> 14. **High thermal conductivity** in both phases
> 15. **Reasonable cost & easy availability**
> 16. **Easy leak detection** (low leakage rate, detectable by methods like electronic sniffers)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_e = \dot{m} \, (h_g - h_f)$ | Evaporator heat absorption rate | **kW** |
| 2 | $Q_c = \dot{m} \, (h_g - h_f)$ | Condenser heat rejection rate | **kW** |
| 3 | $W_{comp} = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work input | **kW** |
| 4 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | **dimensionless** |
| 5 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | **kJ/kg** |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_{fg}}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | **kg/s** |
| 7 | $V_s = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, v_1}{\eta_{vol}}$ | Theoretical compressor displacement | **m³/s** |
| 8 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{R \, T_{cr}}{v_{cr}}$ | Critical pressure (idealized) | **kPa** |
| 9 | $w_{net} = c_p (T_c - T_e) - \text{(losses)}$ | Work of compression (ideal gas) | **kJ/kg** |
| 10 | $\eta_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$ | Carnot COP reference | **dimensionless** |
| 11 | $\text{GWP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Forcing}_x}{\text{Forcing}_{CO_2}}$ | Global Warming Potential ratio | **kg CO₂-eq** |
| 12 | $\text{TEWI} = \text{GWP} \cdot L \cdot n + \text{Indirect CO}_2$ | Total Equivalent Warming Impact | **kg CO₂-eq** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** The numerator of COP, $Q_e$, is the **refrigeration effect**, NOT $h_g$. It is *cooling effect per unit mass* $= h_g - h_f$ at evaporator pressure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air-Conditioning (Residential & Commercial):** R-410A, R-32 are dominant; transition to R-454B underway due to AIM Act (USA) and F-Gas Regulation (EU).
- **Industrial Refrigeration:** NH₃ (R-717) for cold storage due to high $h_{fg}$ and zero GWP.
- **Automotive AC:** R-1234yf replaces R-134a (GWP = 1430 → <1).
- **Cascade Systems:** CO₂ (R-744) in low-temperature cascade stages.
- **Cryogenics:** Helium, Hydrogen, Neon for ultra-low temperatures ($< -150^{\circ}\text{C}$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate & Compressor Displacement for a Given Refrigeration Load

> This is a frequently-asked **numerical in Part B (14 Marks)**. We derive the complete analytical chain.

### Problem Setup

A vapor-compression refrigeration system uses **R-134a** as the working fluid. The system delivers a refrigeration load of $Q_e = 10 \, \text{kW}$. At evaporator conditions ($T_e = -10^{\circ}\text{C}$), the enthalpy of saturated vapor is $h_g = 392.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, and the enthalpy of saturated liquid at condenser pressure is $h_f = 255.6 \, \text{kJ/kg}$. The specific volume at compressor inlet is $v_1 = 0.0993 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, and the volumetric efficiency of the compressor is $\eta_v = 0.85$.

**Required:** Mass flow rate, theoretical compressor displacement, and COP if the compressor work equals $30 \, \text{kJ/kg}$.

### Step 1 — Refrigeration Effect per kg of refrigerant

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} &= h_g - h_f \\
&= 392.3 - 255.6 \\
&= 136.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The refrigerant enters the evaporator as a saturated vapor (after the expansion) and leaves as saturated vapor. The useful *cooling effect* delivered per kg of circulating fluid is the enthalpy difference across the evaporator, namely $h_g - h_f$.

### Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{Q_e}{\text{Re}} \\
&= \frac{10 \, \text{kW}}{136.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}} \\
&= 0.0732 \, \text{kg/s} \quad \left[ \approx 263.4 \, \text{kg/hr} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Total cooling duty $Q_e$ divided by specific cooling effect per kg gives total mass flow. **Power units (kW = kJ/s)** must be consistent.

### Step 3 — Theoretical Compressor Displacement Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_v} \\
&= \frac{0.0732 \cdot 0.0993}{0.85} \\
&= \frac{0.00727}{0.85} \\
&= 0.00855 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 8.55 \, \text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The *ideal* suction volume equals $\dot{m} \cdot v_1$, but real compressors experience re-expansion and clearance losses. We divide by volumetric efficiency to obtain the *geometric* displacement the compressor must possess.

### Step 4 — Compressor Power Input

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} &= \dot{m} \cdot w_{comp} \\
&= 0.0732 \cdot 30 \\
&= 2.196 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Specific work input × mass flow rate = total shaft power. For Part B, often an *isentropic* or *actual* $w_{comp}$ is given; the procedure is identical.

### Step 5 — Coefficient of Performance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{Q_e}{W_{comp}} \\
&= \frac{10}{2.196} \\
&= 4.55
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* COP is dimensionless. For air-conditioning, typical COP is 2.5–4.5; the higher the better.

### Step 6 — Carnot COP Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \, \text{K} \\
T_c &= 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \, \text{K} \\
\text{COP}_{carnot} &= \frac{T_e}{T_c - T_e} \\
&= \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} \\
&= \frac{263.15}{50.0} \\
&= 5.263
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Second-Law Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{II} &= \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{carnot}} \\
&= \frac{4.55}{5.263} \\
&= 0.8646 \quad \text{or} \quad 86.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary Table**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Refrigeration Effect | $\text{Re}$ | **136.7** | kJ/kg |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | **0.0732** | kg/s |
| Compressor Displacement | $V_s$ | **8.55** | L/s |
| Compressor Power | $W_{comp}$ | **2.196** | kW |
| COP | $\text{COP}$ | **4.55** | — |
| Carnot COP | $\text{COP}_{C}$ | **5.263** | — |
| 2nd Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II}$ | **86.46** | % |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Refrigerants (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix for any 14-mark "Compare refrigerants" question.**

| Property | R-12 | R-22 | R-134a | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) | R-290 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Boiling Point (°C) | $-29.8$ | $-40.8$ | $-26.3$ | $-33.3$ | $-78.4$ | $-42.1$ |
| Cond. Press at 40°C (bar) | 9.6 | 15.3 | 10.2 | 15.5 | 105 (transcritical) | 13.0 |
| Latent Heat $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | 152 | 206 | 217 | 1377 | 156 (sublimation) | 426 |
| ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| Toxicity (ASHRAE) | A | A | A | B (mild) | A | — |
| Flammability | Non | Non | Non | Mild | Non | High |
| Oil Miscibility | Good | Good | Good | Poor | Poor | Limited |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ASHRAE Nomenclature for a Specific Compound

**Example:** Identify the refrigerant having formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{F}_4$ (1,1,1,2-Tetrafluoroethane).

**Step A:** Identify number of Carbon atoms:
- $m = 2 \Rightarrow$ First digit contribution = $m - 1 = 1$
- Ethane-based → ASHRAE code begins with **1**

**Step B:** Number of Hydrogen atoms:
- $n = 2 \Rightarrow$ Second digit = $n + 1 = 3$

**Step C:** Number of Fluorine atoms:
- $p = 4 \Rightarrow$ Last digit = $4$

**Result:** Code = **134a** (suffix 'a' indicates isomer — the asymmetric H placement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** $1 + 3 + 4 = 8$. For saturated compound: $2m + 2 = 6$. Number of Cl needed = $6 - n - p = 6 - 2 - 4 = 0$. Hence the formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{F}_4$ contains *no chlorine*, confirming **R-134a is an HFC** — zero ODP. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[REFRIGERANTS] --> B[PRIMARY]
    A --> C[SECONDARY]
    B --> D[SYNTHETIC]
    B --> E[NATURAL]
    D --> D1[CFC]
    D --> D2[HCFC]
    D --> D3[HFC]
    D --> D4[HFO]
    D --> D5[BLENDS]
    D5 --> D5A[ZEOTROPIC]
    D5 --> D5B[AZEOTROPIC]
    E --> E1[INORGANIC]
    E --> E2[HYDROCARBON]
    E1 --> E1A[NH3 R717]
    E1 --> E1B[CO2 R744]
    E1 --> E1C[H2O R718]
    E2 --> E2A[PROPANE R290]
    E2 --> E2B[ISOBUTANE R600a]
    C --> C1[BRINE]
    C --> C2[GLYCOL WATER]
    C --> C3[CHILLED WATER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Synthetic refrigerants (CFC/HCFC/HFC/HFO) dominate modern cycles, while natural refrigerants are the *next generation* due to zero ODP and negligible GWP. Secondary refrigerants never change phase — they only transport heat.

## 4.2 Refrigerant Selection Logic — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[REFRIGERANT SELECTION REQUIREMENT] --> q1[APPLICATION TEMPERATURE RANGE]
    q1 --> q2[REQUIRED COOLING LOAD Qe]
    q2 --> q3[ENVIRONMENTAL CONSTRAINTS ODP GWP]
    q3 --> q4[SAFETY TOXICITY FLAMMABILITY]
    q4 --> q5[THERMODYNAMIC CHECK Tcr NBP hfg]
    q5 --> q6[ECONOMIC FACTORS COST AVAILABILITY]
    q6 --> dec1{ACCEPTABLE}
    dec1 -- YES --> end[FINAL REFRIGERANT CHOICE]
    dec1 -- NO --> q4
    q5 --> err[REJECT REFRIGERANT]
```

## 4.3 Phase-Change Pressure-Temperature Operating Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    evapLow[LOW PRESSURE EVAPORATOR Pe Te] --> comp[COMPRESSOR Wcomp 1 to 2]
    comp --> condHigh[HIGH PRESSURE CONDENSER Pc Tc]
    condHigh --> expValve[EXPANSION VALVE 3 to 4]
    expValve --> evapLow
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle States Mapping:**
> - **1 → 2** : Isentropic compression (low P, low T → high P, high T)
> - **2 → 3** : Condensation (high P, superheat removed + latent heat rejected)
> - **3 → 4** : Isenthalpic expansion (high P → low P; temperature drops)
> - **4 → 1** : Evaporation (low P; absorbs latent heat $h_{fg}$)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Classify refrigerants into primary and secondary with two examples each.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Answer]**
>
> **Primary refrigerants** undergo phase change (boiling/condensation) within the refrigeration cycle and act as the actual working fluid.
> - *Examples:* R-134a, R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂).
>
> **Secondary refrigerants** remain in a single phase (usually liquid) and only transport heat from a remote load to the primary refrigerant at the evaporator.
> - *Examples:* Brine (CaCl₂ solution), chilled water, ethylene glycol-water mixture.

### Question 2. Define ODP and GWP. State the ODP and GWP of R-22 and R-134a.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Answer]**
>
> **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Relative measure of a substance's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone compared to R-11 (ODP = 1.0).
>
> **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Cumulative radiative forcing effect over 100 years compared to CO₂ (GWP = 1.0).
>
> | Refrigerant | ODP | GWP |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | R-22 (HCFC) | 0.055 | 1810 |
> | R-134a (HFC) | 0 | 1430 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare the properties of R-12, R-22, R-134a, and R-717 with respect to environmental impact, safety, and thermodynamic suitability. *(7 Marks)*

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> A *good* refrigerant must satisfy a set of thermodynamic, chemical, safety, environmental, and economic criteria:
>
> 1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure — ensures evaporation at the desired low temperature.
> 2. **High latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg}$** — minimizes refrigerant circulation rate and hence compressor size.
> 3. **Low specific volume of vapor** — reduces compressor swept volume.
> 4. **Moderate condensing pressure** — keeps equipment thin-walled, lightweight, and safe.
> 5. **High critical temperature $T_{cr}$** and **high critical pressure $P_{cr}$** — allows condensation at moderate pressures.
> 6. **Low freezing point** — prevents solidification in evaporator.
> 7. **Chemical stability** — no degradation over thousands of cycles.
> 8. **Non-corrosive** to common metals (Cu, Al, mild steel).
> 9. **Non-toxic and non-flammable** — safe for residential/commercial use (ASHRAE Class A1 ideal).
> 10. **Zero / Low ODP and GWP** — Montreal & Kigali protocol compliance.
> 11. **Good miscibility with lubricating oil** — ensures oil return to compressor.
> 12. **Easy leak detection** and **reasonable cost/availability**.

> **Model Solution (b):** (Tabulated Comparison)
>
> | Property | R-12 | R-22 | R-134a | R-717 (NH₃) |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | Type | CFC | HCFC | HFC | Inorganic Natural |
> | ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 |
> | GWP | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 0 |
> | Toxicity | A (low) | A | A | B (mild) |
> | Flammability | Non | Non | Non | Mild |
> | $h_{fg}$ (kJ/kg) | 152 | 206 | 217 | 1377 |
> | Cond. Pressure (bar) | 9.6 | 15.3 | 10.2 | 15.5 |
> | Oil Miscibility | Good | Good | Good | Poor |
> | Status | **Banned** | **Phasing out** | **Transitional** | **Industrial revival** |
>
> **Inferences:**
> - R-12: Highest ODP & GWP → banned.
> - R-22: Transitional, low ODP but high GWP.
> - R-134a: Zero ODP, but GWP still high → being replaced by R-1234yf.
> - R-717: Zero ODP/GWP, very high $h_{fg}$, but toxic and mildly flammable — restricted to industrial plants with strict safety codes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A refrigeration system uses R-134a and produces $5 \, \text{kW}$ cooling. Evaporator conditions: $-15^{\circ}\text{C}$, where $h_f = 31.95 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ and $h_g = 392.30 \, \text{kJ/kg}$. Condenser saturation temperature is $35^{\circ}\text{C}$ where $h_f = 249.70 \, \text{kJ/kg}$. Assuming isentropic compression to condenser pressure gives $h_2 = 425.50 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ and expansion is isenthalpic, compute:
1. Refrigeration effect per kg
2. Mass flow rate
3. Compressor work and COP
4. Carnot COP and second-law efficiency. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the ASHRAE nomenclature for refrigerants. Identify the refrigerant having the formula $\text{CCl}_2\text{F}_2$. *(7 Marks)*

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **Step 1: Refrigeration Effect (RE)**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{RE} &= h_g - h_f \\
> &= 392.30 - 31.95 \\
> &= 360.35 \, \text{kJ/kg}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *Note:* $h_f$ at evaporator is *very low*; the practical cooling effect equals $h_1 - h_4$ where $h_4 = h_3 = 249.70 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ (after isenthalpic expansion from condenser saturation).
>
> **Step 2: Practical RE using actual cycle states**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{RE}_{actual} &= h_1 - h_4 \\
> &= 392.30 - 249.70 \\
> &= 142.60 \, \text{kJ/kg}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3: Mass Flow Rate**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \dot{m} &= \frac{Q_e}{\text{RE}_{actual}} \\
> &= \frac{5 \, \text{kW}}{142.60 \, \text{kJ/kg}} \\
> &= 0.03506 \, \text{kg/s}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4: Compressor Work**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> W_{comp} &= \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \\
> &= 0.03506 \cdot (425.50 - 392.30) \\
> &= 0.03506 \cdot 33.20 \\
> &= 1.164 \, \text{kW}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 5: COP**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{COP} &= \frac{Q_e}{W_{comp}} \\
> &= \frac{5.0}{1.164} \\
> &= 4.295
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 6: Carnot COP**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> T_e &= -15 + 273.15 = 258.15 \, \text{K} \\
> T_c &= 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \, \text{K} \\
> \text{COP}_{C} &= \frac{T_e}{T_c - T_e} \\
> &= \frac{258.15}{308.15 - 258.15} \\
> &= \frac{258.15}{50.0} \\
> &= 5.163
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 7: Second-Law Efficiency**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \eta_{II} &= \frac{4.295}{5.163} \\
> &= 83.19\% \approx 83.2\%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **ASHRAE Nomenclature Rule** for saturated halogenated hydrocarbon derivatives:
>
> - Let compound be $\text{C}_m \text{H}_n \text{F}_p \text{Cl}_q$ where $m + n + p + q$ corresponds to ASHRAE digits.
> - **First digit:** $(m - 1)$ for ethane-based, otherwise the number of C atoms − 1.
> - **Second digit:** $(n + 1)$ = number of H atoms + 1.
> - **Third digit:** $p$ = number of F atoms.
> - **Chlorine balance:** $q = 2m + 2 - n - p$.
> - Suffix 'a', 'b', 'c' indicates isomers with different H positions.
>
> **For $\text{CCl}_2\text{F}_2$** (Dichlorodifluoromethane):
> - $m = 1 \Rightarrow m - 1 = 0$ (leading zero is omitted)
> - $n = 0 \Rightarrow n + 1 = 1$
> - $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ last digit = 2
> - Verification: $q = 2(1) + 2 - 0 - 2 = 2$ ✓
> - Code = **R-12** (CFC family — Dichlorodifluoromethane)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Mixing $h_f$ at evaporator with $h_f$ at condenser** in RE formula → use $h_1 - h_4$, not $h_g^{evap} - h_f^{evap}$ for *actual* cycles.
> 2. **Confusing ODP and GWP units** — ODP/GWP are *dimensionless ratios*, not pressures.
> 3. **Forgetting the "−1" rule** in ASHRAE nomenclature — major 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Writing 'R134a' instead of 'R-134a'** — full ASHRAE form is mandatory in board exams.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention** — $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ (energy balance), not $Q_c - Q_e = W_{comp}$ reversed.
> 6. **Missing unit conversion** — kW = kJ/s; ensure consistency before dividing.
> 7. **Forgetting the +273.15 in Carnot COP** — temperatures must be in **Kelvin**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Card**
>
> - A **refrigerant** is a *phase-changing working fluid* — evaporation absorbs heat, condensation rejects heat.
> - **ASHRAE Rule:** Code digits = $(m-1) \vert (n+1) \vert p$; chlorine from $q = 2m+2-n-p$.
> - **CFC** has high ODP (banned); **HCFC** transitional; **HFC** zero ODP but high GWP; **HFO** & **Natural (NH₃, CO₂)** are future.
> - **Refrigeration Effect** $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ where $h_4 = h_3$ (isenthalpic expansion).
> - **Mass flow rate** $\dot{m} = Q_e / \text{RE}$.
> - **Compressor displacement** $V_s = \dot{m} \, v_1 / \eta_v$.
> - **COP** $= Q_e / W_{comp}$ is *dimensionless*; higher = better.
> - **Carnot COP** $= T_e / (T_c - T_e)$, temperatures in **Kelvin**.
> - **Second-law efficiency** $\eta_{II} = \text{COP}_{actual} / \text{COP}_{carnot}$.
> - **Ideal refrigerant:** high $h_{fg}$, low $T_{b}$, low ODP/GWP, non-toxic, non-flammable, miscible with oil, cheap.
> - **R-134a:** zero ODP, GWP = 1430 → being replaced by **R-1234yf** (GWP < 1).
> - **R-717 (NH₃):** zero ODP/GWP, very high $h_{fg}$, but **toxic** (Class B) and mildly flammable — industrial only.
> - **R-744 (CO₂):** transcritical cycle, used in cascade low-stage & automotive, GWP = 1.
> - **Refrigeration effect per kg** uses *enthalpy across evaporator*, NOT across condenser.
> - Always state $Q_c = Q_e + W_{comp}$ for energy balance on the cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commonly Used Refrigerants in Automobile Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Commonly Used Refrigerants in Automobile Applications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Automobile Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a working fluid used in the vapor-compression refrigeration cycle of an automotive air-conditioning (MAC) system to absorb heat from the vehicle cabin (evaporator) at low temperature/pressure and reject it to the ambient (condenser) at high temperature/pressure. In the context of KTU 2024 automobile HVAC studies, it is a specially formulated fluid with defined thermodynamic, environmental, toxicological, and material-compatibility characteristics that suit the compact, leak-prone, and safety-critical environment of a moving vehicle. For an automobile application, the refrigerant must additionally satisfy: 1. Operate efficiently across a wide range of compressor speeds (idle ~700 rpm to highway ~4000 rpm). 2. Be compatible with the elastomers, hose materials, and lubricants (PAG/POE oils) used in MAC systems. 3. Have a low **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and increasingly, a low **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**. 4. Exhibit chemical stability over years of service inside metal/aluminum heat exchangers. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Blood of the AC System — Plain English Intuition** > Think of an automobile AC system as the *circulatory system* of a living organism. The **compressor** is the *heart*, the **condenser** is the *lungs exhaling heat*, the **expansion device** is a *valve/narrowing*, and the **evaporator** is the *skin absorbing coolness*. The **refrigerant** is the **blood** that flows through all of these, carrying heat from the cabin and dumping it outside. Just as blood must be the right type (A, B, AB, O) for the body, a refrigerant must be the right "type" so it boils at the right temperature, doesn't poison the environment, and doesn't corrode the metal pipes. Using the wrong refrigerant is like putting diesel in a petrol engine — it technically may run, but the system is poisoned. ## 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics (Bold Constants) - **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** — Reference: R-11 = **1.0** - **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** — Reference: CO₂ = **1.0** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR4 basis) - **Atmospheric Lifetime (years)** — Time a molecule persists before breakdown - **ASHRAE Safety Classification** — Letter (A=lower toxicity, B=higher toxicity) + Number (1=no flame propagation, 2=lower flammability, 3=higher flammability) - **Normal Boiling Point (NBP)** — Saturation temperature at **101.325 kPa** ## 1.4 Historical Evolution of Automobile Refrigerants > [!NOTE] > The trajectory of MAC refrigerants mirrors global environmental policy: > > **1930s – 1990s:** **R-12 (Dichlorodifluoromethane, CCl₂F₂)** — A CFC, dominant for ~50 years. > **1990s – 2010s:** **R-134a (1,1,1,2-Tetrafluoroethane, CH₂FCF₃)** — An HFC, became the global standard after the Montreal Protocol. > **2010s – Present:** **R-1234yf (2,3,3,3-Tetrafluoropropene, CF₃CF=CH₂)** — An HFO, introduced under EU MAC Directive **2006/40/EC** for new vehicle types from **2011** and all new registrations from **2017**. > **Emerging:** **R-744 (CO₂)** — Transcritical, used in EVs and high-efficiency systems (e.g., BMW, Daimler). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties Required of an Automobile Refrigerant A refrigerant used in a moving vehicle must satisfy a multi-objective optimization across the following domains: ### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties - **Moderate Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg):** Higher h_fg means smaller refrigerant mass flow for the same cooling capacity → smaller compressor displacement. - **Low Specific Volume at Compressor Inlet:** Reduces compressor swept volume. - **Suitable Saturation Pressures:** Suction pressure must be > atmospheric (to prevent air ingress through leaks); discharge pressure must be manageable (typically < **2.5 MPa** for automotive systems). - **Low Compressor Discharge Temperature:** To prevent lubricant breakdown (PAG oil degrades above ~150 °C). ### 2.1.2 Environmental Properties - **ODP = 0** (mandatory since Montreal Protocol, 1987). - **Low GWP:** EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC mandates GWP < **150** for new type approvals from 2011 onwards. This is what triggered the R-1234yf transition. ### 2.1.3 Chemical & Material Properties - **Chemical Stability:** Inert to copper, aluminum, steel, and elastomers (HNBR, EPDM). - **Lubricant Miscibility:** Must dissolve in PAG (Polyalkylene Glycol) or POE (Polyolester) oil used with the compressor. - **Non-Corrosive:** Should not form acids in presence of moisture. ### 2.1.4 Safety Properties - **ASHRAE A1 preferred:** Low toxicity, no flame propagation. R-1234yf is **A2L** (mildly flammable), accepted because the charge size in vehicles is small (< 1.5 kg). ## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of Major Automobile Refrigerants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Refrigerant Properties** > (All values are typical/normal-condition; consult NIST REFPROP for precision.) | Property | R-12 | R-134a | R-1234yf | R-152a | R-744 (CO₂) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Chemical Family | CFC | HFC | HFO | HFC | Natural | | Molecular Formula | CCl₂F₂ | CH₂FCF₃ | CF₃CF=CH₂ | CH₃CHF₂ | CO₂ | | Molecular Mass (g/mol) | 120.91 | 102.03 | 114.04 | 66.05 | 44.01 | | NBP (°C) | −29.8 | −26.1 | −29.5 | −24.0 | −78.5 (sublimes) | | Critical Temp. (°C) | 112.0 | 101.1 | 94.7 | 113.3 | 31.0 | | Critical Pressure (MPa) | 4.11 | 4.06 | 3.38 | 4.50 | 7.38 | | ODP | 0.82 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,430 | 4 | 124 | 1 | | Atmospheric Life (yrs) | 100 | 14 | 10.5 | 1.4 | Variable | | ASHRAE Class | A1 | A1 | A2L | A2 | A1 | | Lubricant | Mineral | PAG/POE | POE/PAG | POE | POE (special) | | LFL (kg/m³) | None | None | 0.058 | 0.103 | None | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Refrigerant Performance Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following equations form the analytical core for any numerical problem on refrigerants. ### 2.3.1 Volumetric Cooling Capacity (q_v) The refrigeration effect per unit volume of refrigerant sucked by the compressor: $$q_v = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{v_1}$$ where **h₁** = enthalpy at compressor inlet (kJ/kg), **h₄** = enthalpy at evaporator outlet (= h₁ ideally for dry compression), **v₁** = specific volume at compressor inlet (m³/kg). Units: **kJ/m³** ### 2.3.2 Coefficient of Performance (COP) $$COP_{ref} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ where **Q_e** = evaporator heat absorption rate, **W_c** = compressor work. ### 2.3.3 Compressor Work (per unit mass) $$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$ ### 2.3.4 Pressure Ratio (rp) $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$ Automotive systems typically operate at **rp ≈ 8 to 12**. ### 2.3.5 Discharge Temperature Approximation (Isentropic) $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$$ where **k** = specific heat ratio (Cp/Cv). For R-134a ≈ 1.13, for R-1234yf ≈ 1.10, for R-12 ≈ 1.14. ### 2.3.6 GWP Calculation (Reference-Based) $$GWP_x = \frac{\text{Radiative forcing per kg of } x \text{ over 100 yrs}}{\text{Radiative forcing per kg of } CO_2 \text{ over 100 yrs}}$$ ### 2.3.7 Environmental Equivalence If a vehicle has **m** kg of refrigerant with GWP = G: $$CO_2\text{-equivalent leakage} = m \times G \;\; \text{(kg CO}_2\text{eq)}$$ This is the basis of the **EU F-Gas Regulation** and **Kigali Amendment** compliance. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Service Workshop Identification:** Knowing the GWP allows technicians to compute carbon-equivalent leakage for **F-Gas Regulation** reporting. - **Retrofit Decisions:** R-12 to R-134a retrofit required lubricant change (mineral → PAG) and condenser upgrades. - **EV/Hybrid Systems:** Lower GWP refrigerants and natural refrigerants (R-744) reduce total life-cycle carbon footprint — critical for **LEED** and **ISO 14001** certified automotive plants. - **Fire Safety in Tunnels:** A2L refrigerants (R-1234yf, R-152a) require engineering controls (PRD — Pressure Relief Devices venting refrigerant safely) to prevent accumulation in enclosed parking structures. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why R-12 Was Phased Out — GWP-Based Comparative Analysis > [!NOTE] > This is a standard KTU-style numerical problem. We'll work through it methodically. ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A 1990 vintage automobile originally uses **2.5 kg of R-12**. In a year, due to a leak, **150 g** of refrigerant escapes into the atmosphere. Compute: (a) The CO₂-equivalent emission from this single vehicle. (b) How much R-134a would have the same environmental impact? (c) How much R-1234yf would have the same environmental impact? Given: GWP of R-12 = 10,900; R-134a = 1,430; R-1234yf = 4. ### 3.1.2 Solution **Step 1 — Identify given data.** Let mass leaked, **m = 150 g = 0.150 kg**. **Step 2 — Apply the GWP equivalence formula.** $$CO_2\text{-eq} = m_{leaked} \times GWP$$ **Step 3 — Part (a): R-12 leakage.** $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R12} = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R12} = 1{,}635 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq}$$ **Step 4 — Part (b): R-134a equivalent mass.** For equivalent impact, we set: $$m_{134a} \times 1430 = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ Solving for **m₁₃₄ₐ**: $$m_{134a} = \frac{0.150 \times 10{,}900}{1430} = \frac{1{,}635}{1{,}430}$$ $$m_{134a} = 1.143 \;\; \text{kg}$$ **Step 5 — Part (c): R-1234yf equivalent mass.** For equivalent impact: $$m_{1234yf} \times 4 = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ $$m_{1234yf} = \frac{1{,}635}{4}$$ $$m_{1234yf} = 408.75 \;\; \text{kg}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** The same 150 g leak has the same climate impact as: - **1.143 kg of R-134a** leaking, or - **408.75 kg of R-1234yf** leaking. This shows the **enormous reduction in environmental burden** achieved by transitioning to R-1234yf — a single 150 g leak of R-12 is equivalent to leaking nearly half a ton of R-1234yf. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Discharge Temperature Comparison > [!NOTE] > This derivation uses the isentropic relation, frequently tested in KTU ESE. ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement A vehicle AC compressor compresses R-134a from **P₁ = 0.25 MPa** (suction) to **P₂ = 2.0 MPa** (discharge). The suction temperature is **T₁ = 5 °C = 278.15 K**. Compute the isentropic discharge temperature. Given: For R-134a, **k = 1.13** (approximate ideal-gas value at ~300 K). ### 3.2.2 Solution **Step 1 — Pressure ratio computation.** $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2.0}{0.25} = 8.0$$ **Step 2 — Apply the isentropic temperature relation.** $$T_2 = T_1 \left(r_p\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the exponent.** $$\frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{1.13 - 1}{1.13} = \frac{0.13}{1.13} = 0.1150$$ **Step 4 — Compute the pressure-ratio power term.** $$r_p^{0.1150} = 8.0^{0.1150}$$ Using logarithms: $$\ln(8.0) = 2.0794$$ $$0.1150 \times 2.0794 = 0.2391$$ $$e^{0.2391} = 1.270$$ **Step 5 — Compute T₂.** $$T_2 = 278.15 \times 1.270 = 353.25 \;\; \text{K}$$ $$T_2 = 353.25 - 273.15 = 80.1 \;\; °C$$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** The isentropic discharge temperature of **80.1 °C** is well within the lubricant stability limit. For comparison, computing the same for R-1234yf (k ≈ 1.10) yields a slightly higher exponent (0.0909) but similar magnitude discharge temperature. **R-12 (k ≈ 1.14)** yields marginally higher discharge temperature than R-134a for the same pressure ratio. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Refrigerant > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Decision Logic** | Application Scenario | Recommended Refrigerant | Justification | |---|---|---| | Vintage pre-1995 car, original system | R-12 (if available) | Compatibility, but illegal to vent | | 1995–2014 production car | R-134a | OEM design, well-supported | | New car (post-2017 EU / global) | R-1234yf | GWP < 150 compliance | | Electric Vehicle (high-heat-load) | R-744 or R-1234yf | High COP, low GWP, high critical temp not needed | | Tropical climate vehicle (India) | R-134a / R-1234yf | Both suitable; condensation pressure acceptable | | Hybrid retrofit (R-12 → R-134a) | R-134a + PAG oil | Requires flushing, new dryer, label change | --- ## 3.4 Verification: Volumetric Cooling Capacity Comparison ### 3.4.1 Quick Numerical Example A MAC system uses R-134a with evaporator at **0.244 MPa**, h₁ ≈ **402 kJ/kg**, h₄ ≈ **252 kJ/kg**, v₁ ≈ **0.084 m³/kg**. Compute q_v. **Step 1 — Net refrigeration effect.** $$q_e = h_1 - h_4 = 402 - 252 = 150 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric cooling capacity.** $$q_v = \frac{q_e}{v_1} = \frac{150}{0.084}$$ $$q_v = 1{,}785.7 \;\; \text{kJ/m}^3$$ This value is used to size the compressor displacement for a given cooling duty. A higher q_v means a smaller, lighter compressor — desirable for fuel efficiency. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Refrigerant Selection Logic for Automobile AC ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select MAC Refrigerant] --> B{Is vehicle pre-1995?} B -- Yes --> C[Check legacy R-12 system
and F-Gas compliance] C --> D[Option 1: Recycle R-12
Option 2: Retrofit to R-134a] B -- No --> E{Is vehicle post-2017
EU or GWP regulated market?} E -- No --> F[Use R-134a
OEM Standard] E -- Yes --> G{Is system designed
for high pressure transcritical?} G -- Yes --> H[Use R-744
CO2 Transcritical] G -- No --> I[Use R-1234yf
HFO Low GWP] D --> J[Service and Recycle] F --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[End: Refrigerant Charged
and System Operational] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Refrigerant Property Trade-off Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Refrigerant Property
Trade-off] --> B[Thermodynamic
Performance] A --> C[Environmental
Impact] A --> D[Material
Compatibility] A --> E[Safety
Classification] B --> B1[Latent Heat h_fg] B --> B2[Saturation Pressures] B --> B3[Discharge Temp] C --> C1[ODP must be 0] C --> C2[GWP lt 150 target] C --> C3[Atmospheric Life] D --> D1[Elastomer Seal] D --> D2[Lubricant PAG POE] D --> D3[Metal Corrosion] E --> E1[Toxicity A or B] E --> E2[Flammability 1 2L 2 3] E --> E3[Charge Size Limit] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Functional Flow of Refrigerant in Automobile AC System ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Compressor Suction
Low Pressure Vapor] --> S2[Compressor
Compression] S2 --> S3[Discharge
High Pressure Vapor] S3 --> S4[Condenser
Heat Rejection to Ambient] S4 --> S5[High Pressure Liquid] S5 --> S6[Receiver Dryer
Moisture Removal] S6 --> S7[Expansion Valve
Pressure Drop] S7 --> S8[Low Pressure Mixture] S8 --> S9[Evaporator
Heat Absorption from Cabin] S9 --> S1 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Refrigerant Generations ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Refrigerant Evolution
in MAC Systems] --> B[Generation 1
CFC Era 1930 to 1990] A --> C[Generation 2
HFC Era 1990 to 2015] A --> D[Generation 3
HFO Era 2015 to Present] A --> E[Generation 4
Natural Refrigerants Future] B --> B1[R-12 CCl2F2
ODP 0.82 GWP 10900] C --> C1[R-134a CH2FCF3
ODP 0 GWP 1430] D --> D1[R-1234yf CF3CFCH2
ODP 0 GWP 4 A2L] E --> E1[R-744 CO2
ODP 0 GWP 1 Transcritical] E --> E2[R-152a CH3CHF2
ODP 0 GWP 124 A2] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]` **Q: List any two desirable properties of a refrigerant used in automobile air-conditioning systems.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — to comply with the Montreal Protocol and protect the stratospheric ozone layer. 2. **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP)** — preferably less than 150, as mandated by the EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC, to limit climate impact in case of accidental leakage. *(Any two from: high latent heat, chemical stability, non-flammability or low flammability, lubricant miscibility with PAG oil, low toxicity, suitable discharge temperature, non-corrosive to metals.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set A]` **Q: Why is R-12 (CFC) no longer used as a refrigerant in modern automobile AC systems?** **Model Answer:** R-12 is a chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) with an **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) of 0.82** and a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 10,900**. Its chlorine atoms catalytically destroy stratospheric ozone, and its high GWP causes severe greenhouse forcing. Under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and subsequent amendments, CFCs were phased out globally. Modern vehicles use R-134a, R-1234yf, or R-744 instead. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B for this module offers an internal choice. Both options are provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a comparison table, explain the properties of commonly used refrigerants in automobile applications: R-12, R-134a, R-1234yf, and R-744. Discuss their ODP, GWP, ASHRAE classification, and lubricant compatibility. **(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle AC system uses R-134a. The compressor compresses the refrigerant from **0.20 MPa, 0 °C** to **1.5 MPa**. Assuming isentropic compression with **k = 1.13** for R-134a, calculate the discharge temperature and comment on the lubricant stability. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison Table:** *(See SECTION 2.2 — Table reproduced below for full marks.)* | Property | R-12 | R-134a | R-1234yf | R-744 | |---|---|---|---|---| | ODP | 0.82 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,430 | 4 | 1 | | ASHRAE Class | A1 | A1 | A2L | A1 | | Lubricant | Mineral oil | PAG / POE | POE / PAG | POE (special grade) | **Discussion:** - **R-12** dominated the industry from the 1930s but was banned under the Montreal Protocol due to its ODP. - **R-134a** became the universal replacement in the 1990s because of zero ODP and acceptable thermodynamic performance, but its GWP of 1,430 is high. - **R-1234yf** is a fourth-generation HFO with GWP = 4, designed specifically to meet the EU MAC Directive limit of GWP < 150. Slight flammability (A2L) is managed by limiting charge size and using PRD valves. - **R-744 (CO₂)** is a natural refrigerant with GWP = 1, used in transcritical cycles for EVs (e.g., BMW i3) due to its excellent heat transfer and high critical temperature suitability. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - P₁ = 0.20 MPa = 200 kPa - T₁ = 0 °C = 273.15 K - P₂ = 1.5 MPa = 1500 kPa - k = 1.13 **Step 1: Pressure ratio** $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5$$ **Step 2: Compute exponent** $$\frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{1.13 - 1}{1.13} = 0.1150$$ **Step 3: Pressure ratio power** $$r_p^{0.1150} = 7.5^{0.1150}$$ Using logarithms: $$\ln(7.5) = 2.0149$$ $$0.1150 \times 2.0149 = 0.2317$$ $$e^{0.2317} = 1.2608$$ **Step 4: Discharge temperature** $$T_2 = 273.15 \times 1.2608 = 344.37 \;\; K$$ $$T_2 = 344.37 - 273.15 = 71.22 \;\; °C$$ **Step 5: Comment on lubricant stability** The isentropic discharge temperature of approximately **71 °C** is well below the PAG oil degradation threshold of ~150 °C. Therefore, the lubricant remains stable and the compressor is safe under these conditions. In real systems, the actual discharge temperature is higher (typically 80–110 °C) due to non-ideal effects such as superheat, friction, and non-isentropic losses, but it still remains within safe operating limits for automotive duty cycles. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set C]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Montreal Protocol and the Kigali Amendment in the context of automobile refrigerants. Discuss how they have shaped the transition from R-12 to R-1234yf. **(b) [7 Marks]** A MAC system originally charged with **2.0 kg of R-12** is retrofitted to use **R-134a**. If **20 g of refrigerant leaks annually** from each system, compare the annual CO₂-equivalent emissions before and after retrofit. Given: GWP(R-12) = 10,900, GWP(R-134a) = 1,430. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment:** **Montreal Protocol (1987):** An international treaty signed under the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) to protect the stratospheric ozone layer. It mandated the phase-out of **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** like R-12 and **Halons**.
