# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air Control Testing Workflow

The following **Mermaid flow diagram** illustrates the end-to-end testing procedure from instrument selection to final commissioning report.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Design Documents] --> A1[Pre-Commissioning Review]
    A1 --> A2{Ductwork Class Verified?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Rectify Joints and Sealing]
    A3 --> A2
    A2 -- Yes --> B1[Leakage Test at Test Pressure]
    B1 --> B2{Leakage within Class A Limit 1 percent?}
    B2 -- No --> B3[Re-Seal and Re-Test]
    B3 --> B1
    B2 -- Yes --> C1[AHU Fan Rotation Check]
    C1 --> C2[Filter and Coil Inspection]
    C2 --> C3[Insert Pitot Tube at Traverse Plane]
    C3 --> C4[Measure Static and Velocity Pressure]
    C4 --> C5[Compute Average Velocity and Flow Q]
    C5 --> D1{Design Q Match within 10 percent?}
    D1 -- No --> D2[Adjust VFD or Damper]
    D2 --> C3
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Proportional Balancing of Branches]
    E1 --> E2[Measure ESP at AHU Fan Inlet and Outlet]
    E2 --> E3{ESP within Design Range?}
    E3 -- No --> F1[Trim Impeller or Adjust Pulley]
    F1 --> C3
    E3 -- Yes --> G1[Terminal Device Flow Verification]
    G1 --> G2[Capture Hood at Each Diffuser]
    G2 --> H1[Control Loop Response Test]
    H1 --> H2[PID and Damper Stroke Check]
    H2 --> I1[Compile TAB Report]
    I1 --> I2[Client Handover and Sign Off]

    style A0 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Instrument Interconnection at AHU Test Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AHU[Air Handling Unit] -- Supply Duct --> TP1[Test Plane 1 Pitot Manometer]
    AHU -- Return Duct --> TP2[Test Plane 2 Pitot Manometer]
    AHU -- Outside Air --> TP3[Test Plane 3 Pitot Manometer]
    TP1 --> CALC[Flow Calculation Q equals A times V avg]
    TP2 --> CALC
    TP3 --> CALC
    CALC --> ESP[ESP equals PS supply plus PS return]
    ESP --> FAN[Fan Performance Verification]
    FAN --> VFD[VFD Speed Adjustment]
    VFD --> AHU
    DIFF[Supply Diffusers] --> HOOD[Capture Hood Anemometer]
    HOOD --> BAL[Branch Balancing Module]
    BAL --> VFD

    style AHU fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CALC fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VFD fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Duct Traverse Point Layout (Log-Tchebycheff)

The schematic below shows the **6-point traverse** across a circular duct of diameter $D$. Each point is at a specific non-dimensional radial distance from the wall, following the **log-Tchebycheff** rule that minimises sampling error for turbulent flow.

```
Circular Duct Cross-Section (Diameter = D)

        +----------------------+
        |                      |
        |    (P1)        (P6)  |   P1 = 0.043 D from wall
        |                      |   P2 = 0.293 D
        |  (P2)     (P5)       |   P3 = 0.500 D (center)
        |                      |   P4 = 0.707 D
        |     (P3, P4)         |   P5 = 0.866 D
        |                      |   P6 = 0.957 D
        |    (P4)        (P3)  |
        |                      |
        |  (P5)     (P2)       |
        |                      |
        |    (P6)        (P1)  |
        |                      |
        +----------------------+

  - Two perpendicular diameters (90 deg apart) are used.
  - 6 points on each diameter = 12 points total (per ASHRAE 111).
  - Or 6 points per single diameter (simplified KTU exam version).
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Air Control Test Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Isolate AHU and Verify Safety Lockout] --> step2[Step 2: Inspect Ductwork for Visible Defects]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Perform Duct Leakage Test]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Energise Fan and Verify Rotation]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Traverse Each Main Duct with Pitot Tube]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Record P_s and P_v at AHU Inlet and Outlet]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Calculate ESP and Compare with Design]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Measure Flow at Each Terminal with Capture Hood]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Adjust Balancing Dampers Iteratively]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Verify Damper Control Response to Thermostat]
    step10 --> step11[Step 11: Final Tabulation and Sign Off]

    style step1 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333
    style step11 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define 'External Static Pressure' (ESP) of an AHU fan and explain its significance in air control testing.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **External Static Pressure (ESP)** of an AHU fan is the total static pressure the fan must overcome to deliver the design air volume through the connected ductwork. It is computed as:
>
> $$\text{ESP} = P_{s,\text{supply duct}} + P_{s,\text{return duct}}$$
>
> (excluding the AHU internal components such as coils, filters, dampers, which are accounted for internally by the manufacturer).
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** ESP is the **primary selection criterion** for the fan. During testing, the measured ESP is compared against the design ESP. A higher-than-design ESP indicates **duct under-sizing, dirty filters, or closed dampers**, while a lower ESP indicates **over-sized ductwork or open bypass**. **A deviation beyond $\pm 10\%$** necessitates fan re-selection or system modification.
>
> **Unit (1 mark):** SI unit is **$\text{Pa}$**; in Indian practice, **mm of water gauge (mm.wg)** is also common ($1 \text{ mm.wg} \approx 9.81 \text{ Pa}$).

### **Q2. List any three instruments used for measuring air flow in HVAC ductwork and state the principle of one of them.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Three instruments are:
> 1. **Pitot-static tube with inclined manometer** (1 mark)
> 2. **Vane anemometer** (1 mark)
> 3. **Capture hood (flow hood)** (1 mark)
>
> **Principle of Pitot Tube:** It measures the **velocity pressure** $P_v$ of the air stream, which is the difference between the total pressure $P_t$ (measured at the impact opening facing the flow) and the static pressure $P_s$ (measured at the side openings perpendicular to the flow). The velocity is then computed as $V = \sqrt{2 P_v / \rho}$, and the flow rate is $Q = A \times V$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — With Sub-Parts

**Q.A.** A centrifugal AHU fan is rated to deliver **$12\,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$** at an **ESP of $750 \text{ Pa}$** when running at **$1450 \text{ RPM}$**. During a field air control test, the measured values are:
- Duct dimensions: **$800 \text{ mm} \times 500 \text{ mm}$**
- Traverse readings (Pa) at 6 points: **$18, 24, 28, 30, 26, 20$**
- Static pressure at supply side: **$+380 \text{ Pa}$**
- Static pressure at return side: **$-340 \text{ Pa}$**
- Pitot calibration constant $C = 0.98$, air density $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the actual volumetric flow rate delivered, compare it with the design value, and compute the percentage deviation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the actual ESP and check whether the fan is operating within the design range. If the flow is to be reduced to the design value using a VFD, calculate the new RPM and new ESP using fan affinity laws.

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Actual Flow Rate Calculation

**Step 1 — Duct cross-sectional area:**
$$
A = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at each traverse point:**
$$
V_i = C \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{v,i}}{\rho}} = 0.98 \times 1.291 \times \sqrt{P_{v,i}} = 1.265 \sqrt{P_{v,i}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= 1.265 \sqrt{18} = 1.265 \times 4.243 = 5.367 \text{ m/s} \\
V_2 &= 1.265 \sqrt{24} = 1.265 \times 4.899 = 6.197 \text{ m/s} \\
V_3 &= 1.265 \sqrt{28} = 1.265 \times 5.292 = 6.694 \text{ m/s} \\
V_4 &= 1.265 \sqrt{30} = 1.265 \times 5.477 = 6.929 \text{ m/s} \\
V_5 &= 1.265 \sqrt{26} = 1.265 \times 5.099 = 6.450 \text{ m/s} \\
V_6 &= 1.265 \sqrt{20} = 1.265 \times 4.472 = 5.657 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean velocity:**
$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{5.367 + 6.197 + 6.694 + 6.929 + 6.450 + 5.657}{6} = \dfrac{37.294}{6} = 6.216 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual volumetric flow:**
$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = A \times V_{\text{avg}} = 0.40 \times 6.216 = 2.486 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$:**
$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = 2.486 \times 3600 = 8951 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 6 — Deviation from design:**
$$
\% \text{Deviation} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{design}}}{Q_{\text{design}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{8951 - 12000}{12000} \times 100 = -25.4\%
$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** The fan is delivering only $74.6\%$ of the design flow — a serious shortfall that demands corrective action via VFD, damper adjustment, or pulley change.

### Part (b) — ESP and Fan Affinity Calculations

**Step 1 — Measured ESP:**
$$
\text{ESP}_{\text{actual}} = P_{s,\text{supply}} - P_{s,\text{return}} = 380 - (-340) = 380 + 340 = 720 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with design:**
$$
\text{Design ESP} = 750 \text{ Pa}, \quad \text{Actual} = 720 \text{ Pa} \Rightarrow \text{Deviation} = \dfrac{720 - 750}{750} \times 100 = -4.0\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** ESP is within $\pm 10\%$ — **acceptable**. The problem is **low flow at near-design pressure**, indicating the fan is operating **away from its design point** (likely the system resistance is higher than expected, perhaps due to closed balancing dampers).

**Step 3 — VFD Speed Trim:**

To deliver design flow $Q_{\text{design}} = 12\,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ using Fan Law 1:
$$
\dfrac{Q_{\text{new}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{new}}}{N_{\text{actual}}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 1450 \times \dfrac{12\,000}{8951} = 1450 \times 1.3405 = 1943.7 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — New ESP at the new operating point** (Note: when changing speed, the system curve remains the same, so we must compute the *new pressure required to push design flow through the same system resistance*). The system resistance $R$ is constant, and $P = R Q^2$:

$$
\dfrac{P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{actual}}} = \left(\dfrac{Q_{\text{new}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{12\,000}{8951}\right)^2 = 1.797
$$

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 1.797 \times 720 = 1294 \text{ Pa}
$$

> **Final Comment (1 mark):** The new ESP ($1294 \text{ Pa}$) significantly exceeds the design ESP ($750 \text{ Pa}$) because the duct system has higher resistance than the original design. This implies the **fan is undersized for the system resistance**, and a VFD speed-up to $1944 \text{ RPM}$ will likely overload the motor. The proper engineering recommendation is to **reduce system resistance** (open balancing dampers, clean filters, enlarge ducts) rather than increase fan speed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** Describe the procedure for **Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing (TAB)** the air side of an Air Handling System. Include a note on duct leakage testing and the role of the **ASHRAE Standard 111** in field measurement.

#### **Model Answer Outline (14 marks):**

**(a) TAB Procedure [7 marks]**

1. **Pre-TAB Documentation Review** (1 mark): Verify drawings, equipment schedules, control diagrams.
2. **Pre-Inspection** (1 mark): Visual check of ductwork, insulation, supports, damper access, and clean filters.
3. **Leakage Test** (1 mark): Pressurise representative duct section to design test pressure and quantify leakage.
4. **Fan Performance Verification** (1 mark): Measure $P_{s,\text{supply}}, P_{s,\text{return}}$, RPM, motor current, and plot on the fan curve.
5. **Main Duct Traverse** (1 mark): Pitot traverse of all main supply, return, and outside-air ducts.
6. **Branch Balancing** (1 mark): Iterative adjustment of balancing dampers starting from the most restrictive (index) branch.
7. **Terminal Air Measurement and Final Report** (1 mark): Capture hood readings at every grille/diffuser; compile a TAB report with $\pm 10\%$ tolerance check.

**(b) Duct Leakage Testing and ASHRAE 111 [7 marks]**

- **Duct Leakage Test Procedure** (3 marks): Seal all openings, attach a calibrated test fan (e.g., Duct Blaster), pressurise to test pressure ($250 \text{ Pa}$ for low-pressure, $1000 \text{ Pa}$ for medium-pressure), record injected air volume over time. Compute leakage class per SMACNA ($L = Q_L \cdot P^{0.65}/A_{\text{duct}}$).
- **Acceptance Criteria** (2 marks): SMACNA Seal Class A (sealed): $\le 1\%$; Class B: $\le 3\%$; Class C: $\le 5\%$. For healthcare and cleanroom: $\le 1\%$.
- **ASHRAE Standard 111** (2 marks): Defines **"Practices for Measurement, Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing of Building HVAC Systems."** Specifies the **equal-area log-Tchebycheff traverse** method, the use of calibrated instruments, the $\pm 10\%$ tolerance band, and the format of the final TAB report. It is the **standard reference** in the Indian consulting industry for AHU acceptance testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating **velocity pressure** with **static pressure** — they are *not* the same. $P_v$ is *dynamic*, $P_s$ is *potential*, and $P_t = P_s + P_v$. Many students incorrectly use $P_s$ in the formula $V = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **pitot calibration constant $C$** — it is *not* 1.00 for a non-ideal probe; the answer is penalised if the $C$ factor is omitted.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting units consistently — mixing **mm.wg** and **Pa** in the same equation. Use $1 \text{ mm.wg} = 9.81 \text{ Pa}$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In **fan affinity law** problems, students often wrongly assume the *system curve is constant* when speed changes. Correct interpretation: **system resistance is constant**, so $P \propto Q^2$ at the new speed.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Quoting a $\pm 10\%$ tolerance without specifying that this is per **ASHRAE / SMACNA**, not an arbitrary KTU rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems**

- [x] **Air Control Testing = TAB** (Testing, Adjusting, Balancing) of AHU and ductwork.
- [x] **Primary Goal:** Verify that **$Q$, $P_s$, $P_v$, $P_t$, and ESP** match design within **$\pm 10\%$**.
- [x] **Fundamental equation:** $Q = A \cdot V$, where $V = \sqrt{2 P_v / \rho}$.
- [x] **Pitot traverse** uses **log-Tchebycheff** or **equal-area** points (typically 6 or 12) for accurate mean velocity.
- [x] **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$, sea level.
- [x] **Conversion constants:** $V = 4005 \sqrt{P_v}$ (fpm, in.wg); $1 \text{ in.wg} = 249 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ CFM} = 1.699 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$.
- [x] **External Static Pressure (ESP):** $\text{ESP} = P_{s,\text{supply}} + \vert P_{s,\text{return}} \vert$.
- [x] **Fan Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$; $P \propto N^2$; $W \propto N^3$. System resistance $\Rightarrow P \propto Q^2$.
- [x] **Duct Leakage Class** (SMACNA): $L = Q_L \cdot P^{0.65}/A_{\text{duct}}$ in $\text{cfm/100 ft}^2$.
- [x] **ASHRAE Standard 111** governs field measurement; **SMACNA** governs leakage classification; **ECBC 2017** (India) governs energy compliance.
- [x] **Instruments to remember:** Pitot tube + inclined manometer, capture hood, vane/hot-wire anemometer, tachometer, U-tube manometer.
- [x] **Balancing philosophy:** Start from the **index run** (longest/restrictive), work progressively toward the AHU.
- [x] **Acceptance Tolerance:** **$\pm 10\%$** of design for flow; **$\pm 5\%$** for pressure; **$\le 5\%$** leakage for Class B/C ducts.
- [x] **Energy Hook:** A well-balanced system can save **20–40 %** of fan energy — directly relevant to **ECBC-compliant green buildings**.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Candidates:** Always cite the **standard reference** (ASHRAE 111, SMACNA, ISHRAE, ECBC) in long answers, draw a **clearly labelled schematic** in TAB questions, and present numerical solutions in a **tabular format** with units. This signals professional competence to the examiner and routinely gains **1–2 grace marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls (Thermostats, Humidistat, Control Dampers, Pressure Cut Outs, Relays)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls (Thermostats, Humidistat, Control Dampers, Pressure Cut Outs, Relays) # Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Air Conditioning Control** refers to the integrated network of sensing, decision-making, and actuating devices that regulate the thermodynamic and psychrometric state of air within a conditioned space to maintain prescribed parameters of **temperature**, **humidity**, **cleanliness**, and **air motion**. The five foundational control elements mandated under KTU Module 3 are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus-Mandated Common Controls (Module 3)** > 1. **Thermostats** – Temperature-sensing control devices > 2. **Humidistats** – Humidity-sensing control devices > 3. **Control Dampers** – Airflow modulation devices > 4. **Pressure Cut-Outs** – Safety pressure-sensing switches > 5. **Relays** – Electromagnetic switching/amplifying devices ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The HVAC "Nervous System" Imagine your body as a building, and your **brain** as the central controller. The five common controls function exactly like the body's nervous system: | Human Body Analog | HVAC Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Skin (temperature) | **Thermostat** | Senses deviation from setpoint | | Skin (moisture) | **Humidistat** | Senses humidity deviation | | Muscles (blood flow) | **Control Dampers** | Modulates the working fluid (air) | | Pain reflex | **Pressure Cut-Out** | Safety shutdown on extremes | | Spinal cord neurons | **Relays** | Low-power signal → high-power action | > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Control Loop Structure:** Every HVAC control operates on the closed-loop principle: > **SENSOR** → **CONTROLLER** → **ACTUATOR** → **PROCESS** → (back to SENSOR) ### 1.3 Why These Controls Are Indispensable A modern HVAC system without these controls is a **runaway thermodynamic system** — energy-inefficient, unsafe, and unable to deliver occupant comfort. The American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard **55** and Indian Standard **IS 1391** both require precise control of indoor thermal conditions for **human comfort and process integrity**. Standard Performance Metrics (bolded for emphasis): - **Operative Temperature: 20°C – 26°C** (for comfort) - **Relative Humidity: 30% – 60%** (for comfort) - **CO₂ Level: < 1000 ppm** (for IAQ) - **Duct Static Pressure: 25 mm – 50 mm w.g.** (for ducted systems) # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermostats — The Temperature Sentinel A thermostat is a **temperature-actuated switch** that opens/closes an electrical circuit in response to a change in temperature, comparing it to a predetermined setpoint. ### 2.1.1 Types of Thermostats (KTU Board-Relevant) **A) Bimetallic Strip Thermostat (Most common in residential split ACs)** Two metals with **different coefficients of linear expansion** ($\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$) are bonded. Temperature change causes differential expansion, producing mechanical deflection. **Deflection Equation of a Bimetallic Strip:** The free-end deflection $\delta$ of a bimetallic strip of length $L$, thickness $t$, for a temperature change $\Delta T$ is: $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ Where $K$ is a geometry constant (typically **0.5 to 0.75** depending on end conditions). **B) Gas-Filled Thermostat (Industrial grade)** Uses the **Ideal Gas Law**: $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ When temperature rises at constant volume, pressure rises proportionally → moves a diaphragm → opens/closes contacts. **C) Electronic Thermostat (Modern DDC systems)** Uses **thermistors** (resistance varies with temperature) or **RTDs** (Resistance Temperature Detectors). The governing equation is the **Steinhart-Hart equation** for an NTC thermistor: $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^3$$ Where $T$ is in Kelvin, $R$ is resistance in ohms, and $A, B, C$ are calibration constants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** For part-load modulation, modern thermostats use **Proportional (P)**, **Proportional-Integral (PI)**, or **PID control algorithms** rather than simple on/off hysteresis. --- ## 2.2 Humidistats — The Moisture Sentinel A humidistat is a **humidity-actuated control device** that maintains relative humidity by switching humidification/dehumidification equipment. ### 2.2.1 Working Principle Most humidistats use **human hair, nylon, or animal membrane** as the sensing element. These materials **elongate when humidity increases** because they absorb moisture. The elongation is mechanically amplified to operate a switch. **Empirical Elongation Equation:** $$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta \phi$$ Where $\beta$ is the moisture expansion coefficient, $\Delta \phi$ is the change in RH, $L_0$ is the original length. ### 2.2.2 Dew-Point vs. Relative-Humidity Sensing > [!NOTE] > **RH = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapour / Saturation Pressure at Same Temperature) × 100%** Electronic humidistats use **capacitive polymer sensors** or **chilled-mirror dew-point sensors**. The chilled-mirror operates on the principle that a mirror cooled to the **dew-point temperature** $T_{dp}$ will form condensation, detected optically. $$\phi = \frac{p_v(T_{dp})}{p_{vs}(T_{room})} \times 100\%$$ --- ## 2.3 Control Dampers — The Airflow Regulators **Control dampers** are louvered devices installed in ductwork to **modulate the volume flow rate** of air by varying the flow cross-sectional area. ### 2.3.1 Types (KTU Focus) | Type | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **Opposed Blade** | Adjacent blades rotate in opposite directions | Good mixing downstream | | **Parallel Blade** | All blades rotate in same direction | Linear flow characteristic | | **Butterfly Damper** | Single disc rotating about a shaft | On/off service | | **Inlet Vane Damper** | Pre-rotation of air entering fan | Centrifugal fan control | | **Variable Volume (VAV) Box** | Pressure-independent damper | Variable Air Volume systems | ### 2.3.2 Governing Flow Equation The volumetric flow rate through a damper is governed by the orifice-like equation: $$Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_{air}}}$$ Where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient (typically **0.6 – 0.65** for sharp-edged dampers), $A_{eff}$ is the effective open area, $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the damper, $\rho_{air}$ is air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C). --- ## 2.4 Pressure Cut-Outs — The Safety Sentinel A **pressure cut-out** is a safety switch that **opens the control circuit** when system pressure exceeds (high-pressure cut-out) or falls below (low-pressure cut-out) a preset value. ### 2.4.1 Operating Principle Uses a **bellows or diaphragm** sensing element connected to the refrigerant line. When pressure force exceeds the spring pre-load: $$F_{pressure} = P \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$ $$F_{pressure} \geq F_{spring} \rightarrow \text{Switch Trips}$$ ### 2.4.2 Critical KTU Application Points > [!WARNING] > **Mandatory Placement (Refrigerant Circuit):** > - **High-Pressure Cut-Out (HPCO):** Compressor discharge line (cuts off at typically **24 – 28 bar** for R-410A) > - **Low-Pressure Cut-Out (LPCO):** Compressor suction line (cuts off at typically **2 – 3 bar** for R-410A) > - **Differential Setting:** Cut-out and cut-in must differ by a fixed value to prevent short-cycling (typically **3 – 5 bar** differential) ### 2.4.3 Duct Static Pressure Sensor For VAV systems, a **differential pressure transducer** measures $\Delta P$ across a pitot tube or across a filter bank to maintain duct pressure at the design value of approximately **25 mm w.g.** (≈ **245 Pa**). --- ## 2.5 Relays — The Electrical Muscle A **relay** is an **electromagnetic switch** that uses a low-power control signal to switch a higher-power load. In HVAC, relays are essential because thermostats and humidistats carry currents of only **milliamps**, while compressors and contactors require **amperes**. ### 2.5.1 Operating Equation The electromagnetic force pulling the armature is: $$F_{mag} = \frac{(N \cdot I)^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$ Where $N$ is turns, $I$ is coil current, $\mu_0$ is permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**, $A$ is pole-face area, $g$ is air-gap length. The relay operates when: $$F_{mag} \geq F_{spring}$$ ### 2.5.2 KTU-Relevant Relay Types | Type | Construction | Application | |---|---|---| | **Electromagnetic Relay (EMR)** | Coil + armature + contacts | General switching | | **Solid State Relay (SSR)** | Triac/SCR + optocoupler | Silent, fast switching for heaters | | **Contactor** | Heavy-duty EMR | Motors, compressors | | **Time-Delay Relay** | Pneumatic/electronic timer | Sequential start/stop of chillers | | **Step Relay** | Latching, multi-position | Multi-stage heating/cooling | --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | Bimetallic Strip Deflection | $\delta = K \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2 / t$ | m | Few mm | | Ideal Gas Pressure-Temp | $P_2/P_1 = T_2/T_1$ (at const V) | Pa/K | — | | Relative Humidity | $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}) \times 100\%$ | % | 30–60 % | | Damper Flow | $Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | m³/s | — | | Pressure Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | — | | Relay Magnetic Force | $F = (NI)^2 \mu_0 A / (2g^2)$ | N | — | | Wet-Bulb Approx. | $T_{dp} \approx T_{wb} - (100 - \phi)/5$ | °C | — | | Heat Transfer Through Wall | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | — | | Sensible Heat | $Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | — | | Latent Heat | $Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | — | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility These five controls together form the **automation backbone** of: - **Smart Buildings** (IoT-integrated BMS using BACnet/Modbus protocols) - **Cleanrooms** (Semiconductor/pharma — temperature ±0.1°C, RH ±1%) - **Hospital OTs** (Positive pressure, HEPA-filtered air with backup pressure sensors) - **Data Centers** (ASHRAE TC 9.9 — precision cooling with multi-sensor redundancy) # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bimetallic Thermostat Deflection ### Given: - Bimetallic strip length $L$ = **100 mm = 0.1 m** - Thickness $t$ = **1 mm = 0.001 m** - High-expansion metal: Brass, $\alpha_1$ = **19 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** - Low-expansion metal: Invar, $\alpha_2$ = **1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** - Temperature change $\Delta T$ = **30°C** - Geometry constant $K$ = **0.75** (cantilever, end-free) ### Find: Free-end deflection $\delta$ in mm. ### Step 1 — Write the governing equation $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute differential expansion coefficient $$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} = 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute all values $$\delta = 0.75 \cdot \frac{(17.8 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (30) \cdot (0.1)^2}{0.001}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerator evaluation $$\text{Numerator} = 0.75 \cdot 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 30 \cdot 0.01$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.3$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 5.34 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 4.005 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ ### Step 5 — Divide by $t$ $$\delta = \frac{4.005 \times 10^{-6}}{0.001} = 4.005 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ ### Step 6 — Convert to mm $$\boxed{\delta = 4.005 \text{ mm}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 4 mm deflection is sufficient to actuate a microswitch with a typical trip travel of 1–2 mm, confirming the design is viable. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Humidity from Wet-Bulb/Dry-Bulb Readings ### Given: - Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$ = **30°C** - Wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$ = **22°C** - Atmospheric pressure $P$ = **101.325 kPa** ### Find: Relative humidity $\phi$ and dew-point temperature $T_{dp}$. ### Step 1 — Saturation pressure at $T_{db}$ = 30°C (from steam tables) $$p_{vs}(30°C) = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 2 — Saturation pressure at $T_{wb}$ = 22°C $$p_{vs}(22°C) = 2.645 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Carrier's equation (approximate) for psychrometric calculation The actual vapour pressure $p_v$ is approximately: $$p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (P - p_{vs}(T_{wb})) \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \cdot 0.000367$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $$p_v = 2.645 - (101.325 - 2.645) \cdot (30 - 22) \cdot 0.000367$$ $$p_v = 2.645 - (98.68) \cdot (8) \cdot 0.000367$$ $$p_v = 2.645 - 0.2897$$ $$p_v = 2.355 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Relative Humidity $$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\% = \frac{2.355}{4.246} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 55.5\%}$$ ### Step 6 — Dew-Point Temperature Since $p_v = p_{vs}(T_{dp})$, look up which temperature gives $p_{vs} = 2.355$ kPa. From steam tables, this corresponds to approximately: $$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 19.7°C}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Damper Flow Rate & Fan Power Saving ### Given: - Circular duct diameter $D$ = **0.4 m** - Damper fully open area $A_{max} = \pi D^2/4$ = **0.1257 m²** - Discharge coefficient $C_d$ = **0.62** - Pressure drop $\Delta P$ = **250 Pa** (full open) - Air density $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³** ### Find: (a) Maximum volumetric flow rate. (b) When damper closes to 50% area, how does $\Delta P$ change for same flow? (c) Compare fan power. ### Part (a): Maximum Flow $$Q_{max} = C_d \cdot A_{max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ $$Q_{max} = 0.62 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 250}{1.2}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{500}{1.2}} = \sqrt{416.67} = 20.41 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q_{max} = 0.62 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot 20.41 = 1.591 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ ### Part (b): Partial Closing at Same Flow If effective area becomes $A_{eff} = 0.5 \cdot A_{max}$, then for the **same $Q$**, the new pressure drop $\Delta P_2$ must satisfy: $$Q = C_d \cdot (0.5 \cdot A_{max}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho}} = \frac{Q}{0.5 \cdot C_d \cdot A_{max}} = 2 \cdot \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot A_{max}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho} = 4 \cdot \frac{2 \Delta P_1}{\rho}$$ $$\Delta P_2 = 4 \cdot \Delta P_1 = 4 \cdot 250 = 1000 \text{ Pa}$$ ### Part (c): Fan Power Comparison Assuming fan affinity law: $P_{fan} \propto \Delta P$ for the same flow rate: $$\frac{P_{50\%}}{P_{100\%}} = \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = 4$$ **Important Note:** Although pressure drop rises 4×, real fan systems show that **part-load fan power actually decreases** because the system curve flattens. Damper control is inefficient compared to **VFD control** — a classic KTU comparison point. > [!NOTE] > **Fan Affinity Laws (Quick Reference):** > - $\dot{V}_2/\dot{V}_1 = N_2/N_1$ (flow ∝ speed) > - $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ > - $P_{fan,2} / P_{fan,1} = (N_2/N_1)^3$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Cut-Out Trip Threshold ### Given: - Refrigerant: R-410A - Diaphragm effective area $A$ = **5 cm² = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m²** - Spring constant $k$ = **500 N/m** - Spring pre-compression $x_0$ = **2 cm = 0.02 m** - Safety factor $n$ = **1.2** (over-pressure trip margin) ### Find: Cut-out trip pressure. ### Step 1 — Spring pre-load force $$F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0 = 500 \cdot 0.02 = 10 \text{ N}$$ ### Step 2 — Required pressure force (with safety factor) $$F_{trip} = n \cdot F_{spring} = 1.2 \cdot 10 = 12 \text{ N}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for trip pressure $$P_{trip} = \frac{F_{trip}}{A} = \frac{12}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\boxed{P_{trip} = 24{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 2.4 \text{ bar (gauge)}}$$ This falls within the typical R-410A high-pressure cut-out range of **24 – 28 bar**, confirming a realistic design. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Control Devices | Application | Recommended Sensor | Cut-Out Setting | Relay Type | |---|---|---|---| | Window AC | Bimetallic thermostat | — | Mechanical relay | | Split AC (residential) | Electronic thermistor | HPCO: 28 bar | PCB-mounted SSR | | Chilled water plant | RTD (Pt100) + PI controller | LPCO: 2 bar | Contactor + overload | | Cleanroom | Thermistor + capacitive RH | Duct ΔP: 25 mm w.g. | Solid-state relay | | Cold storage | NTC thermistor | LPCO: 0.5 bar | Latching step relay | | AHU (Hospital) | Multi-sensor DDC | HEPA ΔP: 25 → 50 mm w.g. | Time-delay relay | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — HVAC Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conditioned Space
Temperature and Humidity] -->|Measured value| B[Sensor Layer
Thermostat, Humidistat] B -->|Low-power signal| C[Controller
PID Logic, DDC] C -->|Control pulse| D[Relay Bank
EMR, SSR, Contactor] D -->|High-power switching| E[Actuator Layer
Compressor, Heater, Damper Motor] E -->|Modulates| F[Process Variable
Refrigerant flow, Air flow] F -->|Affects| A P[Pressure Cut-Out
Safety Loop] -.->|Trip signal interrupts| D S[Setpoint Input
User or BMS] --> C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The solid arrows depict the **forward control loop** (Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process → Space). - The dotted line from the **Pressure Cut-Out** represents the **safety override**, which can disable the relay bank regardless of controller command. --- ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Control Device Selection for an AC Unit ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Choose Control Device] --> B{Control Parameter?} B -->|Temperature| C[Thermostat] B -->|Humidity| D[Humidistat] B -->|Airflow| E[Control Damper] B -->|Safety Pressure| F[Pressure Cut-Out] B -->|Electrical Switching| G[Relay] C --> C1{Application Scale?} C1 -->|Residential| C2[Bimetallic or Electronic] C1 -->|Industrial| C3[RTD or Thermistor with PID] D --> D1{Sensor Medium?} D1 -->|Hair/Nylon| D2[Mechanical Humidistat] D1 -->|Polymer Capacitor| D3[Electronic Humidistat] E --> E1{Flow Modulation Type?} E1 -->|Linear| E2[Parallel Blade Damper] E1 -->|Equal Percentage| E3[Opposed Blade Damper] F --> F1{Position in Circuit?} F1 -->|Discharge Line| F2[High Pressure Cut-Out] F1 -->|Suction Line| F3[Low Pressure Cut-Out] G --> G1{Switching Current?} G1 -->|< 5 A| G2[Solid State Relay] G1 -->|5 to 50 A| G3[Contactor Relay] ``` --- ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Sequential Starting of Chilled-Water Plant ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T as Thermostat participant R1 as Time-Delay Relay 1 participant C1 as Chiller Contactor participant PC as Pressure Cut-Out participant R2 as Time-Delay Relay 2 participant CP as Chilled Water Pump participant R3 as Time-Delay Relay 3 participant AHU as AHU Fan Note over T: Space temperature rises above setpoint T->>R1: Energize (after 30s delay) R1->>C1: Close chiller contactor C1->>PC: Chiller starts, pressure builds PC-->>C1: Pressure OK signal confirmed Note over C1: Compressor RUNNING C1->>R2: Trigger pump start (after 60s) R2->>CP: Energize chilled water pump CP->>R3: Trigger after 30s flow proven R3->>AHU: Energize AHU fan Note over AHU: Full system ON ``` This sequence prevents simultaneous high-inrush starting and protects against water-hammer. --- ## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Pressure Cut-Out Logic in Refrigerant Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Refrigerant Circuit C[Compressor] -->|Discharge| D1[High Pressure Switch HPCO] D1 -->|Trip at 28 bar| S1[Cut-Out Coil Trip] D1 -->|Pass through| Cd[Condenser] Cd --> R1[Receiver] R1 --> Ev[Expansion Valve] Ev -->|Suction| D2[Low Pressure Switch LPCO] D2 -->|Trip at 2 bar| S1 D2 -->|Pass through| Evap[Evaporator] Evap -->|Suction returns| C end S1 -.->|Open circuit| C Set[HPCO Cut-in 21 bar, LPCO Cut-in 4 bar] -.-> D1 Set -.-> D2 ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the working principle of a bimetallic thermostat with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** A bimetallic thermostat consists of two strips of metals having **different coefficients of thermal expansion** (e.g., brass and invar) bonded together throughout their length. When the temperature of the surroundings changes, the high-expansion metal (brass) expands more than the low-expansion metal (invar). This differential expansion causes the strip to **bend towards the invar side**. The free end of the strip is mechanically linked to an **electrical contact**. When the strip bends sufficiently due to temperature rise, it opens (or closes) the contacts, thereby switching the cooling/heating equipment ON or OFF. **Key Features:** - Simple, robust, and inexpensive - Used in window ACs and residential thermostats - Differential expansion coefficient governs sensitivity ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between a humidistat and a thermostat. State two applications of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Thermostat | Humidistat | |---|---|---| | **Sensing quantity** | Temperature | Relative humidity | | **Sensing element** | Bimetallic strip / thermistor | Hair / nylon / polymer | | **Output action** | Controls heating/cooling | Controls humidifier/dehumidifier | | **Setpoint range** | 15–30°C typical | 20–80% RH typical | **Applications of Thermostat:** (i) Window AC temperature control, (ii) Cold storage temperature regulation. **Applications of Humidistat:** (i) Hospital OT humidity control, (ii) Textile mill humidification. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Module Coverage **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** — *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of:** (i) Bimetallic thermostat (ii) Opposed-blade control damper **Model Solution:** **(i) Bimetallic Thermostat (3.5 marks):** Already covered in detail under Section 2.1. Key points to write: - Two metals with different $\alpha$ bonded together - Deflection $\delta = K \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2/t$ - Mechanical amplification → mercury switch or microswitch - Setpoint adjustment via screw changing spring pre-load - Differential gap prevents short-cycling **Sketch description (for board exam):** Show two horizontal layers labelled "Brass" and "Invar", fixed at one wall end, with the free end connected to a switch lever. Indicate bending direction when heated. **(ii) Opposed-Blade Control Damper (3.5 marks):** - Multiple rectangular blades mounted on a common shaft - **Adjacent blades rotate in opposite directions** (key feature) - Driven by a **damper actuator** (electric or pneumatic) - When closed: blades seal against each other and frame - When partially open: air is forced through tortuous path - Provides **equal-percentage flow characteristic** — ideal for mixing dampers in AHUs **Sketch description:** Show a duct cross-section with 4–5 parallel blades rotating in opposite directions, arrows indicating airflow. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A refrigerated cold storage has R-410A refrigerant. The high-pressure cut-out is set to trip at 28 bar. The diaphragm area is 6 cm². Calculate the spring force required for the trip mechanism. Also, explain the role of differential pressure setting.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units** $$P = 28 \text{ bar} = 28 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Convert area to SI units** $$A = 6 \text{ cm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute trip force** $$F_{trip} = P \cdot A = 2.8 \times 10^6 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{F_{trip} = 1680 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 4 — Spring design** To ensure reliability, the spring pre-load should be marginally less than $F_{trip}$. Choosing a safety factor of 0.85: $$F_{spring} = 0.85 \times 1680 = 1428 \text{ N}$$ **Role of differential pressure setting (3 marks for theory):** - **Differential** = (Cut-out pressure) − (Cut-in pressure) - Prevents **short-cycling** of compressor - Typical differential for R-410A: **3 – 5 bar** - Example: HPCO trips at 28 bar; cuts back in at 24 bar - Provides time for system to stabilize after a trip event --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternate Question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of an electromagnetic relay as used in air-conditioning control circuits. State the role of relays in starting a compressor.** **Model Solution:** **Construction (2 marks):** An electromagnetic relay consists of: 1. **Coil** (copper wire wound on a former) — energized by low-power control signal 2. **Iron core** — concentrates magnetic flux 3. **Armature** — movable iron part attracted when coil is energized 4. **Spring** — returns armature to rest position when coil de-energized 5. **Contacts** — Normally Open (NO), Normally Closed (NC), and Common (COM) **Operation (3 marks):** When the thermostat calls for cooling, it energizes the relay coil (typically 24 V AC). Current $I$ flows through the coil, producing magnetic flux $\Phi$: $$\Phi = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}}$$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is magnetic reluctance. The flux creates a force on the armature $F \propto \Phi^2$. When $F$ exceeds the spring force, the armature moves, closing the NO contacts. This closes a separate high-current circuit (e.g., 230 V AC) that energizes the compressor contactor. The compressor starts. **Role in compressor starting (2 marks):** - **Electrical isolation** between low-power control and high-power load - **Amplification** — milliamps of thermostat current → amperes of compressor current - **Multiple contact handling** — single relay can switch compressor + condenser fan + crankcase heater - **Safety interlock** — relay can be wired to trip on safety conditions **Sketch:** Show 24 V control loop (thermostat → relay coil) on the left, and 230 V power loop (contactor coil → compressor) on the right, with the relay's NO contacts bridging them. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A thermostat uses a bimetallic strip of length 80 mm and thickness 1.5 mm. The expansion coefficients are $\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$/°C and $\alpha_2 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/°C. Calculate the deflection for a 25°C temperature rise. Take K = 0.75.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ $t = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$ $\Delta T = 25°C$ $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (23 - 2) \times 10^{-6} = 21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$ $K = 0.75$ **Step 1 — Apply formula** $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $$\delta = 0.75 \cdot \frac{(21 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (25) \cdot (0.08)^2}{0.0015}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator** $$\text{Numerator} = 0.75 \cdot 21 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25 \cdot 0.0064$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 21 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.16$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 3.36 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Divide by $t$** $$\delta = \frac{2.52 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0015} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mm** $$\boxed{\delta = 1.68 \text{ mm}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** A 1.68 mm deflection is sufficient to actuate a typical microswitch. If a larger deflection is required, the designer may increase $L$, increase $\Delta \alpha$ (use better material pair), or use a longer cantilever. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Module 3 — Common Controls)** > > 1. **Confusing thermostat types:** Many students write "mercury thermostat" when the question asks for "bimetallic." Always name the **specific type** (bimetallic / gas-filled / electronic). > > 2. **Units inconsistency:** Pressure cut-out problems must use **SI units (Pa, m²)** throughout. Marks deducted if bar is mixed with cm² without conversion. > > 3. **Forgetting differential setting:** In pressure cut-out numerical problems, examiners specifically look for the **differential (hysteresis)** explanation. A correct trip pressure alone may fetch only partial marks. > > 4. **Relay direction wrong:** Writing "thermostat directly switches the compressor" loses **3–4 marks**. Always emphasize the relay's role as an **amplifier/isolator**. > > 5. **Damper flow equation:** Students forget that $C_d$ is **empirical** (not theoretical). Don't derive $C_d$ — accept it as given. > > 6. **No sketch:** Even a rough block diagram with labelled components earns **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Common Controls (Module 3)** > > **Thermostats:** > - [ ] Bimetallic strip uses differential expansion ($\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$) > - [ ] Deflection formula: $\delta = K \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2/t$ > - [ ] Electronic versions use thermistors/RTDs with PID control > - [ ] Modern ACs use **inverter-driven** thermostats with continuous modulation > > **Humidistats:** > - [ ] Sensing element: hair, nylon, polymer > - [ ] Definition: $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}) \times 100\%$ > - [ ] Dew-point temperature is where condensation begins > - [ ] Chilled-mirror sensors are the most accurate (used in labs) > > **Control Dampers:** > - [ ] Opposed-blade → equal-percentage (good for mixing) > - [ ] Parallel-blade → linear (good for throttling) > - [ ] Flow: $Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ > - [ ] VAV boxes are pressure-independent (constant $\Delta P$ across box) > > **Pressure Cut-Outs:** > - [ ] HPCO on discharge line (R-410A ≈ 24–28 bar) > - [ ] LPCO on suction line (R-410A ≈ 2–3 bar) > - [ ] Differential setting prevents short-cycling > - [ ] Trip force: $F = P \cdot A_{diaphragm}$ > > **Relays:** > - [ ] Low-power control → high-power load switching > - [ ] Magnetic force: $F \propto (NI)^2$ > - [ ] Types: EMR, SSR, Contactor, Time-delay, Step > - [ ] Essential for compressor starting and multi-stage control > > **Cross-Cutting Concepts:** > - [ ] All controls operate on closed-loop feedback principle > - [ ] Safety controls (pressure cut-outs) **override** comfort controls (thermostat) > - [ ] KTU board exams expect **numerical + descriptive mix** > - [ ] Always carry **3 significant figures** in numerical answers > - [ ] Sketch + Label = Guaranteed 2–3 extra marks in 14-mark questions

topics.title: Heating Systems in Automotives: Automotive Heaters, Types, and Engine Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heating Systems in Automotives: Automotive Heaters, Types, and Engine Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Automotive Heating Systems

An **Automotive Heating System** is an integrated thermal management subsystem within a vehicle that conditions the passenger cabin air and, in certain configurations, also warms up the engine and its vital fluids. In the context of KTU syllabus (PEMUT742), it specifically refers to the assembly of components — primarily the **heater core**, **blower fan**, **coolant circulation loop**, **thermostatic control valves**, and the **air-distribution ducting** — that utilize waste heat from the internal combustion (IC) engine coolant to deliver warm air to the cabin for occupant comfort and windshield defogging.

In modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), the same functional objective is achieved through **Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) heaters**, **heat pumps**, or **fuel-operated auxiliary heaters (FOAHs)** since there is no IC engine to provide waste heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 outcome expects students to *identify, classify, and evaluate* heating systems for automotive applications, including the heat-transfer physics of the heater core and the thermodynamic justification for engine warm-up strategies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radiator Inside Your Dashboard"

Imagine you are sitting in your living room on a chilly morning. To warm up, you have a small radiator hidden behind the sofa. Hot water from a central boiler (think of it as the car engine) circulates through that radiator, and a fan (the blower) pushes room air across the radiator's fins. The air heats up and gently flows out through grilles near your feet and the windshield.

That is exactly what an automotive heater does:

| Component | Home Analogy | Automotive Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Boiler | Furnace | Internal Combustion Engine |
| Hot water pipes | Plumbing | Coolant hoses |
| Small radiator | Heater | **Heater Core** (aluminium core with fins) |
| Fan | Ceiling fan | **Blower Motor + Squirrel-cage fan** |
| Thermostat knob | Wall thermostat | **HVAC control head / blend door actuator** |

The key insight is that **the heater core is a small secondary heat exchanger** that "steals" a fraction of the engine's waste heat (which would otherwise be dumped to the environment by the main radiator) and transfers it into the cabin.

## 1.3 Why is Heating Required in a Vehicle?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Objectives of an Automotive Heater:**
> 1. **Occupant Thermal Comfort:** Maintain cabin air temperature between **20 °C and 26 °C** (ASHRAE 55 adaptive comfort range).
> 2. **Defogging / Defrosting:** Prevent water-vapour condensation on the inner surface of the windshield (critical for driver visibility — a **safety** requirement mandated by ECE R21 / FMVSS 103).
> 3. **Engine Warm-up Assistance:** In cold climates, pre-heating coolant and lubricant reduces mechanical wear, lowers hydrocarbon (HC) emissions during cold start, and shortens the time to reach the catalytic-light-off temperature of ~**250 °C – 300 °C**.

## 1.4 The Physics of "Waste Heat Recovery"

A typical gasoline engine converts only about **25 % – 30 %** of the fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work at the crankshaft. The remaining **70 % – 75 %** is rejected as waste heat:

$$
Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{brake}} + Q_{\text{coolant}} + Q_{\text{exhaust}} + Q_{\text{radiation}}
$$

- Roughly **30 %** leaves via the **exhaust gas**.
- Roughly **30 % – 35 %** is absorbed by the **engine coolant** (jacket water).
- Roughly **5 % – 10 %** is lost as **radiation and convection** from the engine block surface.

The heater core taps into the **coolant stream** — the second-largest waste-heat sink — and recovers a portion of it for cabin heating. This makes the automotive heater an elegant example of **passive heat recovery with zero additional fuel penalty**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Industry Bench):** A mid-size passenger car typically requires a **heater core thermal capacity between 3 kW and 7 kW** to raise cabin air from −10 °C ambient to 25 °C within 5 – 10 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Coolant-Based Automotive Heater

The heater core is fundamentally a **liquid-to-air cross-flow heat exchanger** of compact construction. The hot engine coolant (typically **85 °C – 105 °C** in normal operation) flows through tubes, while cabin air is forced across the external finned surface by the blower.

### 2.1.1 The Two Heat-Transfer Loops

1. **Hot-Side (Coolant Loop):** Coolant enters the heater core at temperature $T_{h,in}$, gives up sensible heat, and exits at $T_{h,out}$. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}_h$.
2. **Cold-Side (Air Loop):** Cabin air enters at $T_{c,in}$ (which may be ambient or partially recirculated), gains sensible heat, and exits as supply air at $T_{c,out}$. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$.

The heat-transfer rate $Q$ is governed by:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (typically **100 – 300 W/m²·K** for compact automotive heater cores)
- $A$ = effective heat-transfer surface area of the core (typically **0.05 m² – 0.20 m²**)
- $c_{p,h}$ = specific heat of ethylene-glycol coolant mix ≈ **3.6 kJ/kg·K** (50:50 mix)
- $c_{p,a}$ = specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**

## 2.2 Classification of Automotive Heaters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification — Memorize This Table:**

| S.No. | Heater Type | Energy Source | Typical Application | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coolant-Based Heater Core** | Engine coolant waste heat | All IC-engine passenger cars | 60 – 80 % |
| 2 | **Fuel-Operated Auxiliary Heater (FOAH)** | Diesel / Petrol burner | Diesel trucks, RVs, cold-start aid | 85 – 95 % |
| 3 | **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Electric Heater** | High-voltage battery (400 V / 800 V) | Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) | 95 – 99 % |
| 4 | **Heat Pump (Reversed AC Cycle)** | Refrigerant cycle | Modern EVs (Tesla, Nissan Leaf) | 200 – 300 % COP |
| 5 | **Catalytic Heater** | Catalytic oxidation of fuel | Military vehicles, ambulances | 90 % |
| 6 | **Exhaust Gas Heater** | Engine exhaust | Some heavy trucks | Variable |

## 2.3 Engine Heating Systems (Cold-Start Aids)

Engine heating is the complementary problem: when the ambient temperature is very low, the engine oil becomes viscous, battery cranking power drops, and fuel atomization worsens. **Engine heaters** pre-warm the engine block, oil, or coolant before start.

### 2.3.1 Types of Engine Heaters

| Heater | Mounting Location | Power Rating | Warm-up Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block heater (immersion type)** | Freeze-plug in engine block | **400 W – 1500 W** | 2 – 4 hours |
| **Oil pan heater (silicone pad)** | Bonded to oil pan | **50 W – 200 W** | 4 – 6 hours |
| **Coolant heater (circulating type)** | Inline in lower radiator hose | **1500 W – 3000 W** | 30 – 60 min |
| **Intake air heater (grid/thermistor)** | Air intake manifold | **800 W – 2000 W** (diesel) | < 30 s |
| **Glow plug (diesel)** | Cylinder head per cylinder | **6 V / 12 V / 24 V**, 5 – 10 s | Pre-start only |

## 2.4 Thermodynamic Analysis — The "Why" Behind the Heat Transfer

### 2.4.1 Sensible Heat Load of the Cabin

The cabin loses heat to the outside through the windshield, side glass, body panels, and due to ventilation/infiltration. The heater must supply this loss to maintain set-point temperature.

$$
Q_{\text{loss}} = (U_{\text{glass}} A_{\text{glass}} + U_{\text{body}} A_{\text{body}} + \dot{m}_{\text{inf}} c_{p,a}) \cdot (T_{\text{cabin}} - T_{\text{ambient}})
$$

### 2.4.2 Heat-Transfer Rate Through Composite Body

For the curved windshield (laminated glass with PVB interlayer) the U-value is approximately **5.5 W/m²·K**, while for the steel body panels it is roughly **3.0 – 4.0 W/m²·K** after including convective surface resistances.

### 2.4.3 Why Heater Cores Use Aluminium, Not Copper

Aluminium has:
- Thermal conductivity $k_{Al} \approx 237 \text{ W/m·K}$ (vs. copper's 401 W/m·K) — lower, but…
- Density $\rho_{Al} \approx 2700 \text{ kg/m³}$ (vs. copper's 8960 kg/m³) — **3.3× lighter**
- Lower cost, and forms a corrosion-resistant oxide layer that suits ethylene-glycol coolant.

This trade-off favours aluminium for mass-sensitive automotive applications.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these eight equations — they cover > 90 % of numerical questions.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} c_{p} \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate (any fluid) |
| 2 | $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Heat exchanger sizing (NTU / LMTD method) |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference (counter-flow) |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{loss}} = U A \, \Delta T$ | Cabin heat loss through walls / glass |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_a c_{p,a} \Delta T}$ | Required blower air volume flow rate |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}$ | Required coolant mass flow through heater core |
| 7 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{\min}}$ | Effectiveness-NTU method parameter |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1+C_r)}$ | Effectiveness of single-pass cross-flow heater |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive OEM design** | Sizing heater core for cold-start cabin bring-up in ≤ 5 min |
| **EV thermal management** | Replacing PTC with heat pumps to extend driving range by 10 – 20 % |
| **Cold-climate logistics** | Engine block heaters in fleet trucks (reduce DPF regeneration cycles) |
| **Defence / Aerospace** | Catalytic heaters in armoured vehicles (silent operation, no flame) |
| **Emissions control** | Faster catalyst light-off → lower HC and CO cold-start emissions |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Blower Air-Flow Rate

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car cabin requires **Q = 4.5 kW** of heating power. The blower supplies air at $T_{c,in} = 5 \, °C$ (ambient), and the heater core must deliver supply air at $T_{c,out} = 45 \, °C$. Calculate the required mass-flow rate and volume-flow rate of air.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known numerical values.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \times 1005 \, \frac{J}{kg \cdot K} \times (45 - 5) \, K
$$

$$
4500 \, W = \dot{m}_a \times 1005 \times 40
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mass-flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{4500}{1005 \times 40} = \dfrac{4500}{40200} = 0.1119 \, \frac{kg}{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass-flow to volume-flow using air density at ~25 °C, $\rho_a = 1.184$ kg/m³.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{0.1119}{1.184} = 0.0945 \, \frac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in CFM (cubic feet per minute) for industry reference.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = 0.0945 \times 2118.88 = 200.2 \, \text{CFM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A ~200 CFM blower is the typical rating for a mid-size sedan. This corresponds to a complete cabin air change roughly every **30 – 60 seconds**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Coolant Mass Flow Through Heater Core

**Problem Statement:** The same heater core receives coolant at $T_{h,in} = 90 \, °C$ and must supply Q = 4.5 kW. The coolant leaves at $T_{h,out} = 75 \, °C$. The coolant is 50:50 ethylene-glycol-water with $c_{p,h} = 3.6 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$. Find $\dot{m}_h$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the energy balance on the coolant side.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values.**

$$
4500 \, W = \dot{m}_h \times 3600 \, \frac{J}{kg \cdot K} \times (90 - 75) \, K
$$

$$
4500 = \dot{m}_h \times 3600 \times 15 = 54000 \, \dot{m}_h
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\dot{m}_h$.**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{4500}{54000} = 0.0833 \, \frac{kg}{s} = 5.0 \, \frac{kg}{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/min using coolant density $\rho_h \approx 1.07$ kg/L.**

$$
\dot{V}_h = \dfrac{0.0833}{1.07} = 0.0779 \, \frac{L}{s} \approx 4.68 \, \frac{L}{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-Balance Cross-Check (Reynolds Analogy):** The ratio of air-side to coolant-side mass flow is $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_h = 0.1119 / 0.0833 \approx 1.34$. Since $c_{p,h}/c_{p,a} = 3600/1005 \approx 3.58$, the heat-capacity rate ratio $C_{\min}/C_{\max} = (1005 \times 0.1119)/(3600 \times 0.0833) = 0.374$ — indicating the **air-side is the limiting (minimum) stream**, a classic design insight.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LMTD for the Heater Core (Counter-Flow Approximation)

For a compact heater core with **crossed streams** (coolant flows through tubes perpendicular to air flow across fins), the **counter-flow LMTD** is used as an upper-bound estimate and corrected with a configuration factor $F$ (typically 0.75 – 0.90).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the four terminal temperatures.**

$$
T_{h,in}, \, T_{h,out}, \, T_{c,in}, \, T_{c,out}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the two end-differences (counter-flow orientation).**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the LMTD integral.** Starting from a differential element $dA$:

$$
dQ = U \, dA \, (T_h - T_c)
$$

Assuming linear temperature profiles (a valid approximation for fluids with constant $c_p$ and negligible phase change):

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{T_h - T_c} = \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \, dA \, \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_a c_{p,a}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between inlet and outlet.**

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_a} \right) \int_0^{A} dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U A \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_a} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the standard LMTD expression.**

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example using previous data.**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 45 = 45 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 75 - 5 = 70 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{45 - 70}{\ln(45 / 70)} = \dfrac{-25}{-0.4418} = 56.59 \, °C
$$

**Step 7 — Apply cross-flow correction factor $F = 0.85$.**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{actual}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 0.85 \times 56.59 = 48.10 \, °C
$$

**Step 8 — Required heat-transfer area (assume $U = 180$ W/m²·K).**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{4500}{180 \times 48.10} = 0.520 \, m^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A heater core of **~0.52 m² effective surface area** (typical for a sedan) is required. This corresponds to a physically compact core of dimensions roughly **200 mm × 130 mm × 30 mm** with high-density louvered fins.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effectiveness-NTU Verification

Using the data above, validate the heat-exchanger effectiveness.

**Step 1 — Compute heat-capacity rates.**

$$
C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 0.0833 \times 3600 = 299.9 \, W/K
$$

$$
C_a = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a} = 0.1119 \times 1005 = 112.5 \, W/K
$$

**Step 2 — Identify $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$.**

$$
C_{\min} = C_a = 112.5 \, W/K \quad ; \quad C_{\max} = C_h = 299.9 \, W/K
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity ratio.**

$$
C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} = \dfrac{112.5}{299.9} = 0.375
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum possible heat transfer.**

$$
Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 112.5 \times (90 - 5) = 9562.5 \, W
$$

**Step 5 — Effectiveness.**

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \dfrac{4500}{9562.5} = 0.471 \; (\text{or } 47.1 \, \%)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute NTU.**

$$
\text{NTU} = -\dfrac{\ln[1 - \varepsilon(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r} = -\dfrac{\ln[1 - 0.471 \times 1.375]}{1.375}
$$

$$
\text{NTU} = -\dfrac{\ln(0.352)}{1.375} = \dfrac{1.044}{1.375} = 0.759
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity-check $UA$ product.**

$$
UA = \text{NTU} \times C_{\min} = 0.759 \times 112.5 = 85.4 \, W/K
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{UA}{U} = \dfrac{85.4}{180} = 0.474 \, m^2
$$

The small discrepancy with the LMTD-derived value (0.520 m²) arises from the configuration factor $F = 0.85$ — both methods are internally consistent.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Summary

| Variable | Change | Effect on Required Heater Core Area | Design Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ambient $T_{amb} \downarrow$ | Cabin $\Delta T$ rises | Area must increase | Add PTC booster in EVs |
| Coolant inlet $T_{h,in} \uparrow$ | Larger $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Area decreases | Use coolant control valve for hotter flow |
| Air flow $\dot{m}_a \uparrow$ | $C_{\min}$ rises → $C_r$ falls | Area decreases but noise rises | Cap blower at ~250 CFM |
| Coolant flow $\dot{m}_h \downarrow$ | $C_r$ rises → area increases | More area needed | Specify minimum coolant circulation |
| Glass area $A_{\text{glass}} \uparrow$ | Cabin heat loss rises | Larger Q → larger core | Apply low-e coating to windshield |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an automotive coolant-based heater."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Component identification (1 mark):** The system consists of an IC engine, a heater core (a small liquid-to-air heat exchanger), a blower motor, hoses, and a thermostatic blend door.

2. **Working principle (1.5 marks):** Hot coolant (~**90 °C**) leaving the engine block is routed through the heater core via a control valve. Simultaneously, the blower pushes cabin air across the external fins of the core. Heat transfers from the coolant (through tubes and fins) to the air, raising its temperature from ambient (~**5 °C**) to about **45 °C**. The blend door mixes this heated air with cold fresh air to achieve the desired outlet temperature.

3. **Flow continuity (0.5 mark):** The cooled coolant returns to the water pump inlet, completing the loop. The system uses **waste heat** from the engine, so there is **no additional fuel penalty**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"List and briefly explain any three types of engine heaters used for cold-start assistance."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Block Heater (1 mark):** An electrical resistance heater (typically **750 W – 1500 W**) screwed into a freeze-plug hole on the engine block. Plugged into mains power 2 – 4 hours before start; warms the coolant jacket and cylinder walls to ~**30 – 40 °C**.

2. **Oil Pan Heater (1 mark):** A silicone-rubber resistive pad bonded to the underside of the oil pan. Power rating **50 – 200 W**. It lowers oil viscosity, reducing cranking torque and wear during start.

3. **Intake Air Heater (1 mark):** A grid or thermistor element in the intake manifold of diesel engines. Energised for **5 – 10 seconds** before cranking, it heats the inducted air to **50 – 80 °C**, improving atomisation and reducing white-smoke emissions during cold start.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 — Internal Choice (Choose either Question A or Question B).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

> **(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) heater** used in battery electric vehicles. Why is it preferred over a resistive coil heater? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A heater core in a passenger car receives coolant at $T_{h,in} = 95 \, °C$ and discharges it at $T_{h,out} = 78 \, °C$. The blower delivers air at the rate of **220 CFM** at **8 °C** and **101.325 kPa**. The air leaves the heater at **48 °C**. Compute:
> (i) the heat-transfer rate $Q$,
> (ii) the air-side mass flow rate,
> (iii) the coolant mass flow rate (take $c_{p,h} = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of PTC Heater (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A PTC heater consists of a **ceramic element** (typically barium titanate, $BaTiO_3$, doped with rare-earth elements like lanthanum or yttrium).
- The ceramic pellets are arranged in **parallel-flow fins** between two metallised electrodes.
- A **blower fan** is integrated downstream to drive cabin air across the fins.
- The unit operates from the **traction battery** (typically **400 V** or **800 V DC** in modern EVs).
- Total power per module: **2 kW – 7 kW**, often multiple modules stacked for higher capacity.

**Working Principle (2.5 marks):**
- Below the **Curie temperature** ($T_c \approx 120 \, °C$ for automotive PTC), the ceramic has low resistance → high current → rapid self-heating.
- As temperature rises above $T_c$, the resistance **increases exponentially** (by **3 – 5 orders of magnitude**), automatically limiting the current and stabilising the temperature.
- This **self-regulating** behaviour eliminates the need for a separate thermal cut-out or thermostat — a critical safety advantage at high voltages.

**Why PTC over Resistive Coil? (1.5 marks):**
- **Inherent safety:** Self-limiting temperature prevents thermal runaway and fire risk.
- **No red-hot filament:** Reduced combustion risk in case of blower failure.
- **Compact and lightweight:** Higher power density (~**10 W/cm²** vs. 5 W/cm²).
- **Long life:** No filament burnout; > **10 000 hours** typical.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\dot{V}_a = 220$ CFM; $T_{c,in} = 8 \, °C$; $T_{c,out} = 48 \, °C$; $T_{h,in} = 95 \, °C$; $T_{h,out} = 78 \, °C$; $c_{p,h} = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Convert CFM to SI volume-flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = 220 \, \dfrac{ft^3}{min} \times \dfrac{0.02832 \, m^3}{ft^3} \times \dfrac{1}{60} \, \dfrac{min}{s} = 0.1038 \, \dfrac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute air density at inlet conditions (~8 °C, 1 atm).**

$$
\rho_a = \dfrac{P}{R T} = \dfrac{101325}{287 \times 281} = 1.256 \, \dfrac{kg}{m^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Air-side mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_a = 1.256 \times 0.1038 = 0.1304 \, \dfrac{kg}{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat-transfer rate Q.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 0.1304 \times 1005 \times (48 - 8)
$$

$$
Q = 0.1304 \times 1005 \times 40 = 5242 \, W \approx 5.24 \, kW
$$

**Step 5 — Coolant mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})} = \dfrac{5242}{3600 \times (95 - 78)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{5242}{61200} = 0.0856 \, \dfrac{kg}{s} \approx 5.14 \, \dfrac{kg}{min}
$$

**Step 6 — Coolant volume flow (density ≈ 1.07 kg/L).**

$$
\dot{V}_h = \dfrac{0.0856}{1.07} = 0.0800 \, \dfrac{L}{s} \approx 4.80 \, \dfrac{L}{min}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

> **(a)** Compare **fuel-operated auxiliary heaters (FOAH)** and **heat pump systems** as cabin-heating solutions for modern electric vehicles. Discuss their respective Coefficient of Performance (COP), range impact, and packaging. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A heater core has an overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 200 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$ and an effective area $A = 0.45 \, m^2$. Using the LMTD method, compute the heat-transfer rate for counter-flow operation with the following terminal temperatures:
> $T_{h,in} = 92 \, °C$, $T_{h,out} = 76 \, °C$, $T_{c,in} = 6 \, °C$, $T_{c,out} = 50 \, °C$.
> State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — FOAH vs Heat Pump Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | FOAH (Fuel-Operated Auxiliary Heater) | Heat Pump (Reversed AC Cycle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Diesel / petrol from tank | Traction battery + refrigerant |
| **COP** | **0.85 – 0.95** (thermal / fuel) | **2.0 – 3.0** at 0 °C, drops to **1.2 – 1.5** at −20 °C |
| **Range impact on EV** | **Zero** (uses fuel), but adds mass **+5 – 8 kg** | Reduces EV range by **10 – 20 %** in winter |
| **Packaging** | Burner + combustion chamber + exhaust pipe | Same hardware as AC + reversible valve + accumulator |
| **Warm-up time** | **2 – 4 minutes** to full heat | **30 – 60 seconds** |
| **Noise / vibration** | Combustion roar (~**55 dBA**) | Quiet (~**40 dBA**) |
| **Maintenance** | Spark plug, fuel pump, exhaust | Same as AC system |
| **Use case** | Cold-climate EVs, range-extender EVs | Mainstream EVs (Tesla Model Y, Hyundai Ioniq 5) |

**Key Insights (1 mark):**
- Modern OEMs increasingly prefer **heat pumps** for environmental reasons (no on-board combustion) and because the **multiplied COP** (delivering 2 – 3 kWh of heat per 1 kWh of electricity) is more efficient than resistive PTC heating.
- However, at ambient temperatures below **−10 °C**, heat pump performance degrades sharply, and OEMs (e.g., Nissan, BMW) revert to PTC boost.

**Part (b) — LMTD Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. Constant $c_p$ for both fluids.
3. No heat loss to surroundings (well-insulated core).
4. No phase change on either side.
5. Counter-flow arrangement (true LMTD formula applies).

**Step 1 — Calculate end-temperature differences (counter-flow).**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 92 - 50 = 42 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 76 - 6 = 70 \, °C
$$

**Step 2 — Apply LMTD formula.**

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{42 - 70}{\ln(42 / 70)} = \dfrac{-28}{-0.5108} = 54.82 \, °C
$$

**Step 3 — Apply correction factor for cross-flow.**

For a cross-flow heat exchanger with both fluids unmixed, use $F \approx 0.88$.

$$
\Delta T_{\text{actual}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 0.88 \times 54.82 = 48.24 \, °C
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat-transfer rate.**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{actual}} = 200 \times 0.45 \times 48.24
$$

$$
Q = 4341.6 \, W \approx 4.34 \, kW
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion (1 mark).**

This heat-transfer rate is sufficient for a compact passenger car cabin under moderate winter conditions (−5 °C to +5 °C ambient). For harsher climates, either a larger area or higher coolant inlet temperature (e.g., from a supplementary PTC) would be required.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks Every Semester:**
> 1. **Confusing air-side and coolant-side mass flow** — students often swap $\dot{m}_a$ and $\dot{m}_h$. Always identify which fluid is the **minimum** capacity stream ($C_{\min}$) first.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert CFM to SI units** — 1 CFM ≠ 1 m³/s. Conversion factor: **1 ft³/min = 4.719 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s**.
> 3. **Using dry-air $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K** instead of $c_p \approx 1010$ J/kg·K at higher temperatures — a 0.5 % error, often acceptable, but check question context.
> 4. **Missing the cross-flow correction factor F** — pure LMTD gives an *ideal* value; for real automotive cores use $F = 0.75 - 0.90$.
> 5. **Ignoring the heat-capacity ratio $C_r$** in effectiveness-NTU derivations — many textbook solutions assume $C_r = 0$ (single-stream dominance), which is **not** valid for low coolant flow conditions.
> 6. **Confusing PTC with resistance wire** — PTC ceramic is **self-regulating** by its ferroelectric Curie transition; it is NOT a simple resistor.
> 7. **Mixing up heat pump and AC cycle directions** — in heating mode the refrigerant condenses **inside** the cabin coil (releases heat indoors) and evaporates outside.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Automotive heater = passive waste-heat recovery device** that uses ~30 % of fuel energy rejected via coolant.
- **Heater core** = compact **liquid-to-air cross-flow heat exchanger**, typically aluminium, $A = 0.05 - 0.20$ m².
- **Typical thermal capacity:** **3 – 7 kW** for a mid-size sedan.
- **Standard coolant temperatures:** inlet **85 – 95 °C**, outlet **70 – 80 °C**.
- **Air supply temperature:** **40 – 50 °C** at the plenum under full-heater conditions.
- **Blower rating:** **150 – 250 CFM** typical for passenger cars.
- **Master formula:** $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.
- **Six types of automotive heaters:** Coolant-based, FOAH, PTC, Heat Pump, Catalytic, Exhaust-gas.
- **Five types of engine heaters:** Block, Oil-pad, Coolant-circulating, Intake-air, Glow-plug.
- **PTC heater advantage:** Self-regulating via Curie transition ($T_c \approx 120 °C$); no thermal runaway.
- **Heat pump COP:** **2.0 – 3.0** at 0 °C ambient; drops to ~**1.2** at −20 °C.
- **Effectiveness of typical heater core:** **40 – 60 %**.
- **Cabin comfort set-point:** **20 – 26 °C** as per ASHRAE 55.
- **Windshield U-value:** ~**5.5 W/m²·K** for laminated glass.
- **Defogging is a safety-critical function** governed by ECE R21 / FMVSS 103.
- **EVs have no engine waste heat** → require PTC, heat pump, or FOAH.
- **Always include the cross-flow correction factor $F$** in LMTD-based heater-core sizing.
- **Standard ambient test conditions:** **−10 °C to +5 °C** for cold-start performance rating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
