topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Service: Maintenance, Causes of Failure, Leak Testing, Discharging, Evacuating, Charging, Servicing Heater System, Removing and Replacing Components, Troubleshooting, Compressor Service, Methods of Dehydration, and Charging and Testing

# Air Conditioning Service: Maintenance, Diagnosis & Repair Procedures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Air Conditioning Service** refers to the systematic set of maintenance, diagnostic, and repair procedures performed on a refrigeration-based HVAC system to ensure optimal thermal comfort, energy efficiency, refrigerant integrity, and component longevity. It encompasses preventive maintenance, fault diagnosis, leak detection, refrigerant recovery, system evacuation, dehydration, charging, and performance testing of all major subsystems — including the compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion device, heater core, blower assembly, and associated controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **service technician's procedural knowledge** — meaning students must know the *exact step-by-step sequence* for discharging, evacuating, charging, leak testing, and troubleshooting, along with the *physical/thermodynamic reasons* behind each step.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Bloodstream of the Car":**
> Imagine your car's AC system is like the **human circulatory system**. The **refrigerant** is the *blood*, the **compressor** is the *heart*, the **condenser and evaporator** are the *lungs* (where heat exchange happens), and the **hoses and connections** are the *blood vessels*. Now, if a blood vessel leaks (refrigerant leak), the patient becomes weak. If the heart fails (compressor failure), circulation stops. AC service is essentially the **medical diagnosis and surgery** on this circulatory system — checking pressure (blood pressure), testing for leaks (bleeding), flushing impurities (blood transfusion), and refilling refrigerant (blood donation).
>
> Just as a doctor must follow a strict protocol — diagnose → stabilize → operate → recover — an AC technician must follow: **Inspect → Leak Test → Discharge → Evacuate → Dehydrate → Charge → Test → Verify**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in AC Service

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Service Metrics (Memorize These):**
> - **Standard Test Conditions:** **Ambient temperature = 25 °C (77 °F)**, **Relative Humidity = 50 %**, **Engine speed = 1500 rpm** (for automotive AC).
> - **High-side gauge pressure (R-134a):** **1.4 to 1.6 MPa** (≈ 200–230 psi) at normal operation.
> - **Low-side gauge pressure (R-134a):** **0.15 to 0.25 MPa** (≈ 22–36 psi) at normal operation.
> - **Static refrigerant pressure (system off, 25 °C):** **≈ 0.65 MPa** (≈ 94 psi) for R-134a.
> - **Vacuum level required during evacuation:** **≤ 500 microns (0.5 Torr)** held for **at least 15 minutes**.
> - **Boiling point of water at standard atmosphere:** **100 °C**.
> - **Atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar / 14.696 psi)**.

### 1.4 Why AC Service Matters — Engineering Perspective

Modern automotive and residential AC systems operate with **tight tolerances** — refrigerant charges accurate to within **± 15 grams** in some systems, and clearances in the compressor of **less than 0.05 mm**. Any contamination (moisture, air, non-condensables, particulate) can cause:

- Compressor valve damage
- Acid formation in refrigerant oil
- Loss of cooling capacity (C.O.P drop of 20–40 %)
- Premature component failure

Hence, **service procedure discipline** is not optional — it is the engineering backbone of HVAC reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of AC Service (Logical Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> Every AC service operation — whether on a car, split AC, or chiller — can be broken down into these five logical pillars. KTU examiners frequently frame theory questions around this sequence.

### Pillar 1 — **Diagnosis & Leak Testing**
The technician must first **identify** whether the system is undercharged, overcharged, contaminated, or mechanically faulty.

- **Why?** Because blindly adding refrigerant to a leaking system wastes money, harms the environment (HFC regulations), and may mask the real fault.
- **How?** Through pressure gauges, electronic leak detectors, UV dye, soap-bubble solutions, and electronic sniffer probes.

### Pillar 2 — **Recovery & Discharging**
Before opening any part of the sealed system, the refrigerant **must be recovered** into an approved cylinder using a recovery machine (per SAE J2788 / EPA Section 608).

- **Why?** Venting refrigerants to atmosphere is **illegal** under the Montreal Protocol (1987) and KTU expects students to know this.
- **How?** Connect recovery machine to high-side and low-side service ports, run the compressor, and recover ≥ **95 %** of the charge.

### Pillar 3 — **Evacuation & Dehydration**
The system is pulled down to a **deep vacuum** (≤ 500 microns) to remove:
- **Non-condensable gases** (air, nitrogen)
- **Moisture** (water vapor)

- **Why?** Air in the system causes **excessive head pressure**; moisture causes **acid formation** and **ice plugging** at the expansion device.
- **How?** A vacuum pump runs for **30–60 minutes**, then the system is **isolated** and observed for **15 minutes** — if pressure rises, moisture or a leak is present.

### Pillar 4 — **Charging**
Refrigerant is added either by:
- **Weight charging** (most accurate — for R-134a, e.g., 600–900 g for a typical sedan)
- **Pressure charging** (using gauge pressure-temperature charts)
- **Liquid charging at the high side** (fastest, for bulk fill)
- **Vapor charging at the low side** (safest, for top-up)

### Pillar 5 — **Testing & Verification**
After charging, system performance is verified by:
- Measuring **high-side and low-side pressures**
- Measuring **vent temperature** (should be **3–8 °C** at the center vent for automotive)
- Checking **superheat** and **subcooling**
- Visual inspection for leaks after 15 minutes of operation.

---

## 2.2 Causes of AC System Failure — The "12-Fault Matrix"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"List the common causes of AC system failure and their remedies."* This table is **exam-ready gold**.

| # | Failure Cause | Symptom | Diagnostic Method | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigerant leak | Warm air, low pressure on both sides | Electronic sniffer, UV dye, soap solution | Repair leak, evacuate, recharge |
| 2 | Compressor failure (seized/worn) | No cooling, no clutch engagement | Clutch voltage test, ohm meter, manual rotation | Replace compressor, flush system |
| 3 | Clogged expansion valve / orifice tube | Frost on evaporator, low suction | Feel evaporator inlet temperature | Replace expansion device, recover & recharge |
| 4 | Blocked condenser (debris, bugs) | High head pressure, warm air | Visual inspection, pressure reading | Clean condenser fins, wash |
| 5 | Evaporator freeze-up | Intermittent cooling, ice on lines | Sight glass, suction pressure drops | Replace thermostat switch, check TXV |
| 6 | Blower motor failure | No airflow at vents | Voltage check, listen for motor | Replace blower motor or resistor |
| 7 | Blend door actuator fault | Cold on one side, hot on other | Actuator motor test | Replace actuator |
| 8 | Vacuum leakage in cabin | Whistling noise, dust ingress | Smoke test, listening | Replace door seals |
| 9 | Contaminated refrigerant (R-12/R-134a mix) | Erratic pressures, poor cooling | Refrigerant identifier (RI) | Recover all, evacuate, recharge pure |
| 10 | Moisture in system | High head pressure, acid smell | Sight glass bubbles, vacuum decay | Evacuate deeply, replace receiver/drier |
| 11 | Air in system | Excessive high-side pressure | Pressure-temperature mismatch | Evacuate to ≤ 500 microns |
| 12 | Electrical faults (relay, fuse, switch) | Compressor won't engage | Multimeter, test light | Replace fuse/relay, repair wiring |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical.** Pay special attention to the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Units | Boundary / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{evap} = f(P_{low})$ | Evaporation temperature from low-side pressure | °C | Use P-T chart for refrigerant (e.g., R-134a) |
| 2 | $T_{cond} = f(P_{high})$ | Condensation temperature from high-side pressure | °C | Should be **10–15 °C above ambient** |
| 3 | $SH = T_{suction\,line} - T_{sat}(P_{low})$ | Superheat (compressor protection) | °C | **Target: 5–12 °C** for TXV systems |
| 4 | $SC = T_{sat}(P_{high}) - T_{liquid\,line}$ | Subcooling (liquid line protection) | °C | **Target: 5–10 °C** |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{c}}{h_{fg}}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | kg/s | $h_{fg}$ = latent heat at evaporator |
| 6 | $m_{charge} = V_{sys} \times \rho_{ref}$ | Refrigerant charge from system volume | kg | Use refrigerant datasheet |
| 7 | $Q_{leak} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t}$ | Leak rate estimate (pressure decay) | Pa·m³/s | ISO 8573 standard |
| 8 | $P_{vac} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{N_{pump\,stage}}$ | Ultimate vacuum of multi-stage pump | Pa | 2-stage = ≈ 15 microns |
| 9 | $h_{1} + w_{comp} = h_{2}$ | Compressor energy balance (1st law) | kJ/kg | Adiabatic assumption |
| 10 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{L}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | Typical 2.5–3.5 for vapor compression |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

These service procedures are used daily by:
- **Automotive service technicians** (dealership workshops, independent garages)
- **HVAC-R contractors** (residential split AC, chillers)
- **Refrigerated transport fleet operators** (reefer trucks, cold-chain logistics)
- **Industrial refrigeration maintenance crews** (cold storage, food processing)

A certified AC technician must understand **both the "how" and the "why"** — KTU tests both.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Leak Rate Derivation (Pressure Decay Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Objective:** Express the refrigerant leak rate in grams per second using a pressure-decay test, ideal for sealed-system integrity verification.

### Step 1 — Setup
A refrigerant-charged system of internal volume $V$ is isolated, and the pressure is observed to decay from $P_1$ to $P_2$ over time $\Delta t$ at constant temperature $T$.

### Step 2 — Apply Ideal Gas Law
Initial mass of refrigerant inside:

$$
m_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R_{specific} T}
$$

Final mass of refrigerant inside:

$$
m_2 = \dfrac{P_2 V}{R_{specific} T}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass Lost = Mass Difference
Mass leaked out of system:

$$
\Delta m = m_1 - m_2 = \dfrac{V(P_1 - P_2)}{R_{specific} T}
$$

### Step 4 — Leak Rate
Dividing by time interval:

$$
\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{V(P_1 - P_2)}{R_{specific} T \cdot \Delta t}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Useful Units
Multiply by **1000** to get grams per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{leak} \, \left[ g/s \right] = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V \, (P_1 - P_2)}{R_{specific} \, T \, \Delta t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU-canonical):** If $\dot{m}_{leak} \leq 14$ g/year for R-134a, the system passes (per SAE J1628 standards). Anything higher mandates leak repair.

---

## 3.2 Evacuation Time Derivation

### Step 1 — Pump Down Equation
The pressure inside the system under vacuum follows:

$$
P(t) = P_{atm} \cdot e^{-\dfrac{Q_{pump} \cdot t}{V}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{pump}$ = effective pumping speed of vacuum pump (m³/min)
- $V$ = system volume
- $t$ = evacuation time

### Step 2 — Solve for Time to Reach Target Vacuum
Rearranging:

$$
t = -\dfrac{V}{Q_{pump}} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{target}}{P_{atm}}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Plug in Typical Values
For $V = 0.005$ m³ (typical car AC), $Q_{pump} = 0.05$ m³/min, $P_{target} = 500 \, \mu m = 0.0667$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa:

$$
t = -\dfrac{0.005}{0.05} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{0.0667}{101.325}\right)
$$

$$
t = -0.1 \cdot \ln(0.000658) = -0.1 \cdot (-7.326) = 0.733 \text{ min} = 44 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we wait 30–60 minutes despite math saying 44 s?** Because the math assumes **ideal gas with no moisture release**. In reality, water trapped in the desiccant and oil slowly desorbs over time — hence the rule of thumb **"minimum 30 minutes per kg of system capacity, never less than 30 minutes"**.

---

## 3.3 Refrigerant Charge Calculation (Weight Method)

### Step 1 — System Volume Identification
From manufacturer specs (e.g., for a Maruti Suzuki Swift AC):

$$
V_{sys} = V_{evap} + V_{cond} + V_{hoses} + V_{receiver-drier} = 0.0035 \, m^3
$$

### Step 2 — Refrigerant Density Lookup
For R-134a entering as liquid at 25 °C:

$$
\rho_{ref} = 1206 \, kg/m^3 \quad \text{(from datasheet)}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Charge

$$
m_{charge} = V_{sys} \times \rho_{ref}
$$

$$
m_{charge} = 0.0035 \times 1206 = 4.221 \, kg
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student mistake:** Using vapor density (≈ 5.25 kg/m³) instead of liquid density (≈ 1206 kg/m³). Always use **liquid density** when charging by weight from a liquid cylinder.

---

## 3.4 Superheat & Subcooling Computation (Service Bay Use)

### Superheat Derivation
Superheat is the difference between actual suction-line temperature and saturation temperature at low-side pressure:

$$
SH = T_{suction\,line} - T_{sat}(P_{low})
$$

**Example:** Suction line reads **8 °C**, low-side gauge reads **0.2 MPa**. From R-134a P-T chart: $T_{sat}(0.2\,MPa) = -10\,°C$.

$$
SH = 8 - (-10) = 18 \, °C
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $SH = 18\,°C$ is **excessive superheat** → indicates **low charge or restricted TXV**. Normal range: **5–12 °C**.

### Subcooling Derivation
Subcooling is the difference between saturation temperature at high-side pressure and actual liquid-line temperature:

$$
SC = T_{sat}(P_{high}) - T_{liquid\,line}
$$

**Example:** High-side gauge **1.5 MPa** → $T_{sat} = 53\,°C$. Liquid line measures **45 °C**.

$$
SC = 53 - 45 = 8 \, °C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **8 °C subcooling is healthy** — confirms liquid line is fully condensed and slightly subcooled before the expansion device.

---

## 3.5 Compressor Service — Clutch Air-Gap Analysis

The electromagnetic clutch has a critical **air gap** of typically **0.4 to 0.8 mm**. If too large, the clutch slips and won't engage fully. If too small, it drags and burns out.

### Step 1 — Measure Air Gap

$$
g_{air} = d_{rotor} - d_{hub\,flat}
$$

Use a **feeler gauge** to confirm in **3 equispaced points** (12, 4, 8 o'clock positions).

### Step 2 — Acceptance Check

$$
0.4 \, mm \leq g_{air} \leq 0.8 \, mm
$$

If out of spec, **shim adjustment** is performed by adding/removing shim washers behind the clutch plate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **3-point measurement rule** — uneven gaps cause clutch wobble and premature wear.

---

## 3.6 Methods of Dehydration — Comparative Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Expected:** Compare dehydration methods, their effectiveness, and applicability.

| Method | Principle | Moisture Removed | Time | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deep Vacuum (Triple Evacuation)** | Pressure reduction causes water to boil at low temp | Up to **99.9 %** | 3 × 30 min | All refrigeration systems |
| **Heat + Vacuum** | Vacuum + heater on receiver/drier | Up to **99.99 %** | 1–2 hours | Large chillers |
| **Desiccant (Molecular Sieve)** | Adsorption by 3Å zeolite | Removes down to **− 60 °C dew point** | Continuous | Receiver-driers, driers in liquid line |
| **Purge with Dry Nitrogen** | Displaces moist air | Up to **95 %** | 10–20 min | Industrial systems |
| **Refrigerant Flush (R-141b)** | Liquid solvent dissolves water | Up to **98 %** | 30 min | Severe contamination cases |

---

## 3.7 Charging Procedures — Step-by-Step (Weight Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **This procedure is **exam-favorite** for 7-mark questions.**

### Procedure A — Liquid Charging at High Side

1. Recover refrigerant (if any remains).
2. Evacuate system to **≤ 500 microns** for **30 min minimum**.
3. Isolate vacuum pump and observe for **15 min** — pressure must not rise above **1000 microns**.
4. Connect refrigerant cylinder to **high-side service port** via charging hose (cylinder upright, valve up for liquid).
5. Open cylinder valve and **purge charging hose** to remove air.
6. Open high-side manifold valve slowly — liquid refrigerant flows into system under pressure.
7. Charge by **weight** on cylinder scale until target mass (e.g., **650 g** for R-134a) is dispensed.
8. Close cylinder valve, close manifold valve, run engine + AC for 5 min, verify pressures.
9. Leak test with electronic sniffer — no leak → reassemble.

### Procedure B — Vapor Charging at Low Side

Used for **topping up** an undercharged system:
1. Run engine at 1500 rpm, AC on MAX, blower HIGH.
2. Connect cylinder to **low-side port** (cylinder upright for vapor draw).
3. Charge slowly in **50 g increments**, waiting **30 s** between additions.
4. Monitor low-side gauge — stop when in **0.15–0.25 MPa** range.
5. Disconnect, re-test.

> [!WARNING]
> **Never charge liquid into the low-side port** — liquid refrigerant entering the compressor causes **liquid slugging** and destroys the reed valves.

---

## 3.8 Heater System Service Procedure

The heater core is part of the **engine cooling circuit** and serves as the AC's heating source in winter.

### Steps:
1. **Drain coolant** from radiator (capture in clean container).
2. Disconnect heater hoses at firewall.
3. Remove dashboard trim and **heater box assembly** (typical 2–4 hours of labor).
4. Inspect heater core for:
   - Leaks (pressure test with **1.0 bar compressed air** under water)
   - Blockage (reverse-flush with water)
5. If leaking, **replace heater core**. If blocked, attempt **reverse flush** with a garden hose.
6. Reassemble, refill coolant, **bleed air** from system.
7. Verify hot air at vents after 10 minutes of engine warm-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** Heater system failure is often misdiagnosed as "AC fault" by students — remember heater core uses **engine coolant**, **not refrigerant**.

---

## 3.9 Removing & Replacing Components — Generic Procedure

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Recover refrigerant completely (≥ 95 % per EPA) |
| 2 | Disconnect electrical connectors, label them |
| 3 | Remove mounting bolts in **reverse order** of installation |
| 4 | Cap open lines immediately to prevent contamination |
| 5 | Replace component (compressor, condenser, evaporator, TXV) |
| 6 | Replace **all O-rings**, lubricate with **PAG oil** for R-134a |
| 7 | Reconnect lines, torque to spec (typically **10–25 Nm**) |
| 8 | Evacuate, leak test, charge, performance test |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master AC Service Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Complaint: AC Not Cooling] --> B[Visual Inspection]
    B --> C[Connect Manifold Gauges]
    C --> D{Read Pressures}
    D -->|Both Low| E[Leak Test - Sniffer/Dye]
    D -->|Both High| F[Check Condenser Airflow]
    D -->|Low Side High| G[Compressor Failure]
    D -->|Normal Pressures| H[Blower/Blend Door Fault]
    E --> I{Leak Found?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Recover Refrigerant]
    I -->|No| K[Check Charge Weight]
    J --> L[Repair Leak - Replace O-ring/Component]
    L --> M[Evacuate System <=500 microns]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Hold Vacuum 15 min - Decay Test]
    N --> O{Vacuum Holds?}
    O -->|No| P[Moisture or Leak - Redo]
    O -->|Yes| Q[Charge by Weight - e.g. 650g R-134a]
    Q --> R[Run Engine 1500 rpm AC MAX]
    R --> S[Verify Pressures and Vent Temp]
    S --> T[Final Leak Test]
    T --> U[Customer Delivery - Job Done]
```

## 4.2 Leak Testing Methods — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspected Refrigerant Leak] --> B{System Charged?}
    B -->|Yes - Operating| C[Electronic Sniffer Probe]
    B -->|No - Empty| D[Pressure Decay Test with N2]
    C --> E{Sniffer Detects?}
    E -->|Yes - High Concentration| F[UV Dye Injection for Location]
    E -->|Yes - Trace Leak| G[Soap Bubble Solution]
    E -->|No| H[System OK - Check Other Faults]
    D --> I{Pressure Decay > 0.05 bar in 10 min?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Major Leak - Disassemble and Find]
    I -->|No| K[Acceptable - Proceed to Charge]
    F --> L[UV Light Inspection - Mark Leak]
    G --> L
    J --> M[Replace Faulty Component]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Evacuate and Recharge]
```

## 4.3 Compressor Service Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Symptoms] --> B{Engages at All?}
    B -->|No| C[Check Voltage at Clutch Coil]
    B -->|Yes - No Cooling| D[Check Internal Pressure]
    C --> E{Voltage Present?}
    E -->|Yes - 12V| F[Air Gap > 0.8mm - Shimming Needed]
    E -->|No| G[Check Relay and Fuse]
    D --> H{Discharge Pressure?}
    H -->|Very Low| I[Seized Compressor - Replace]
    H -->|Normal| J[Slipping Clutch - Inspect]
    F --> K[Adjust Air Gap to 0.4-0.8mm]
    G --> K
    I --> L[Flush System - Replace Receiver-Drier]
    J --> M[Replace Clutch Assembly]
    L --> N[Evacuate and Recharge]
    M --> N
```

## 4.4 Refrigerant Charging Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Empty Evacuated System] --> B{Charge Method?}
    B -->|Weight Most Accurate| C[Liquid Charge via High Side]
    B -->|Top-up Only| D[Vapor Charge via Low Side]
    C --> E[Cylinder on Scale Upright Valve Up]
    E --> F[Purge Charging Hose]
    F --> G[Open High Side Manifold Slowly]
    G --> H[Monitor Cylinder Mass Drop]
    H --> I{Target Mass Reached?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Close Valves - Disconnect]
    I -->|No| H
    J --> K[Run Engine Verify Performance]
    D --> L[Engine Running AC MAX]
    L --> M[Cylinder Vapor Draw]
    M --> N[Charge in 50g Increments]
    N --> O{Pressure in 0.15-0.25 MPa?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Stop - Wait - Recheck]
    O -->|No| N
    P --> Q[Final Leak Test]
    Q --> R[Service Complete]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Triple Evacuation" in AC servicing. Why is it preferred for moisture removal?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Triple evacuation is a **moisture-removal technique** where the system is evacuated three times in succession, with each cycle broken by a **dry nitrogen charge** at low pressure (≈ 0.5 bar) to agitate and release trapped moisture, followed by another vacuum pull. This is preferred because:

1. A single vacuum cannot fully desorb moisture bound to the desiccant and metal surfaces.
2. The nitrogen "burst" between vacuum cycles **mechanically displaces** moisture molecules.
3. The third vacuum stage achieves a final pressure **≤ 200 microns**, ensuring dew point below **− 40 °C**.

---

### Question 2: List any three causes of high-side pressure being abnormally high.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Three causes of abnormally high high-side pressure:

1. **Overcharge of refrigerant** — excess mass raises condensing temperature.
2. **Condenser airflow restriction** — blocked fins, failed cooling fan, debris accumulation.
3. **Non-condensable gases (air) in system** — air doesn't condense and adds partial pressure.
4. *(Bonus)* Condenser fan motor failure or ambient temperature too high.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the complete procedure for **leak testing** of an automotive AC system. Compare any **four methods** of leak detection with their relative sensitivity.

**Q. (b)** Describe the **causes of failure** in an AC system. For each cause, state **one diagnostic test** and **one corrective action**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — Leak Testing (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Visual Inspection (1 mark)**
Before any test, inspect all hose connections, compressor shaft seal, condenser, and evaporator for oil traces. Refrigerant leaks are always accompanied by **PAG oil leakage** — visible as a wet, oily film.

**Step 2 — Pressure Decay Test (1 mark)**
Charge the system with **OFN (Oxygen-Free Nitrogen)** to **1.0–1.5 MPa**. Isolate and observe pressure for **10 minutes**. If pressure drops more than **0.05 MPa**, a leak exists.

**Step 3 — Leak Detection Methods Comparison (5 marks)**

| Method | Sensitivity | Type of Leak Detected | Skill Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Halide Torch** | 1 oz/year | Large leaks only | High (uses flammable gas) |
| **Electronic Sniffer** | 0.1 oz/year | All sizes | Low |
| **UV Dye + Blacklight** | 0.25 oz/year | Medium-large | Low |
| **Soap Bubble Solution** | 0.5 oz/year | Medium-large | Very low |
| **Nitrogen Pressure Test** | Gross leaks | Pre-charging check | Low |

**Step 4 — Final Verification (0 mark additional)**
After repair, repeat leak test to confirm zero leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme note:** Examiner awards 1 mark for procedure overview, 5 marks for comparison table (1 per row of comparison + 1 for sensitivity ranking), 1 mark for concluding statement.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — Causes of Failure (7 Marks)

Present the **12-Fault Matrix from Section 2.2** of these notes. The student must:

1. **List at least 6 causes** with symptom (2 marks)
2. **Provide diagnostic method for each** (2 marks)
3. **Provide corrective action for each** (2 marks)
4. **Concluding summary statement** (1 mark)

**Sample answer excerpt:**

> *Cause: Refrigerant leak — Symptom: Warm air from vents, low gauge pressure on both sides. Diagnostic: Electronic sniffer probe or UV dye injection. Corrective action: Locate and repair leak (typically replace O-ring or hose), evacuate system to ≤ 500 microns, recharge with correct refrigerant weight.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - ❌ Confusing **symptoms** with **causes** (e.g., writing "warm air" as a cause — it is a symptom).
> - ❌ Omitting the **diagnostic test** — examiners specifically look for "how would you confirm this fault?"
> - ❌ Failing to mention **preventive actions** (e.g., "replace receiver-drier whenever system opened for > 15 minutes").

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **complete procedure for evacuating, dehydrating, and charging** an automotive AC system using R-134a. State the standard vacuum level and minimum evacuation time.

**Q. (b)** Describe the **service procedure for the heater system** of a vehicle. How does it differ from the refrigerant-based AC system?

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — Evacuation, Dehydration, Charging (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Pre-Evacuation (1 mark)**
- Confirm all components installed and torqued.
- Confirm high-side and low-side service valves closed.
- Connect manifold gauges: blue (low) to low-side port, red (high) to high-side port.
- Center hose to vacuum pump inlet.

**Step 2 — Evacuation (2 marks)**
- Start vacuum pump.
- Open both manifold valves slowly.
- Monitor vacuum gauge — pressure should drop steadily.
- Pump down to **≤ 500 microns (0.5 Torr)**.
- Run pump for **minimum 30 minutes** (rule of thumb: 30 min per kg system capacity, never less than 30 min).

**Step 3 — Dehydration via Vacuum Decay Test (1 mark)**
- Close manifold valves, turn off pump.
- Wait **15 minutes**.
- If pressure rises above **1000 microns** → moisture present or leak → repeat evacuation.
- If holds steady → system is dry.

**Step 4 — Charging (2 marks)**
- Connect R-134a cylinder to **high-side port** for liquid charging.
- Cylinder upright (liquid at outlet).
- Purge charging hose.
- Open cylinder valve, then high-side manifold valve.
- Charge by **weight on cylinder scale** — e.g., 600–900 g depending on vehicle.
- Stop when target mass dispensed.

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark)**
- Start engine, AC on MAX, blower HIGH.
- Read pressures: high **1.4–1.6 MPa**, low **0.15–0.25 MPa**.
- Center vent temperature **3–8 °C**.

**Flow Diagram (refer Section 4.4 of these notes)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — Heater System Service (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Heater System Architecture (2 marks)**
The vehicle heater system uses the **engine coolant** as the heating medium. Hot coolant from the engine block circulates through the **heater core** (a small radiator behind the dashboard). A **heater control valve** regulates flow. A **blower fan** forces cabin air through the heater core, delivering hot air via vents.

**Step 2 — Service Procedure (3 marks)**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Confirm coolant is cold; drain radiator partially |
| 2 | Disconnect heater hoses at firewall |
| 3 | Remove dashboard trim and heater box (2–4 hours) |
| 4 | Pressure test heater core at 1.0 bar under water |
| 5 | If leaking → replace; if blocked → reverse-flush |
| 6 | Reinstall, refill coolant, bleed air |

**Step 3 — Comparison with Refrigerant AC (2 marks)**

| Aspect | Refrigerant AC | Heater System |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | R-134a refrigerant | Engine coolant (ethylene glycol + water) |
| Energy source | Compressor (mechanical) | Waste engine heat |
| Operating temperature | 0–60 °C | 80–95 °C |
| Service medium | Refrigerant oil (PAG) | Coolant additive (corrosion inhibitor) |
| Leak test gas | OFN at 1.5 MPa | OFN at 0.5 bar |
| Toxicity | High (HFC) | Low (glycol-based) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - ❌ Students write "heater uses refrigerant" — **wrong**; heater uses coolant.
> - ❌ Students skip the **air-bleeding step** — trapped air causes heater core to overheat and fail prematurely.
> - ❌ Students confuse **heater control valve** with **expansion valve** — they are unrelated components.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision notes — read these 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### 🔑 Must-Know Facts
- ✅ **R-134a normal operating pressures:** High **1.4–1.6 MPa**, Low **0.15–0.25 MPa**
- ✅ **Static pressure at 25 °C:** **≈ 0.65 MPa**
- ✅ **Vacuum standard:** **≤ 500 microns**, hold **15 min** for decay test
- ✅ **Evacuation rule:** **30 min minimum**, or **30 min per kg system capacity**
- ✅ **Clutch air gap:** **0.4–0.8 mm**
- ✅ **Superheat target:** **5–12 °C**
- ✅ **Subcooling target:** **5–10 °C**
- ✅ **Center vent temp:** **3–8 °C** (automotive, R-134a)
- ✅ **Leak rate acceptance:** **≤ 14 g/year** for R-134a
- ✅ **Liquid charging** at high side only; **vapor charging** at low side only

### 🔑 Must-Remember Procedures
- ✅ **Service sequence:** Diagnose → Recover → Repair → Evacuate → Charge → Test
- ✅ **Triple evacuation** uses **dry nitrogen** between vacuum cycles
- ✅ **Always replace receiver-drier** when system is opened for **> 15 minutes**
- ✅ **Always use new O-rings**, lubricate with **PAG oil** (mineral oil for R-12, PAG/POE for R-134a)
- ✅ **Never vent refrigerant** — illegal under **Montreal Protocol, 1987**
- ✅ **Heater system uses coolant, NOT refrigerant**
- ✅ **Liquid slugging** destroys compressor — never charge liquid into low-side port

### 🔑 Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing high-side vs low-side service ports
- ❌ Using liquid density instead of vapor density in calculations (or vice versa)
- ❌ Skipping the **vacuum decay test**
- ❌ Forgetting to **label** electrical connectors during disassembly
- ❌ Overlooking **non-condensable air** as a failure cause
- ❌ Mixing up **expansion valve** (TXV) with **heater control valve**

### 🔑 Quick Formula Recall
- $\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{V(P_1 - P_2)}{R_{specific} \, T \, \Delta t}$
- $m_{charge} = V_{sys} \times \rho_{ref}$
- $SH = T_{suction\,line} - T_{sat}(P_{low})$
- $SC = T_{sat}(P_{high}) - T_{liquid\,line}$
- $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{comp}}$ (typically **2.5–3.5**)

### 🔑 Tools a Service Technician Must Know
- ✅ Manifold gauge set (R-134a couplers — different from R-12!)
- ✅ Vacuum pump (2-stage, ≤ 15 microns ultimate)
- ✅ Recovery machine (per SAE J2788)
- ✅ Electronic refrigerant leak detector (sniffer)
- ✅ UV dye and blacklight lamp
- ✅ PAG oil and O-ring kit
- ✅ Cylinder scale (for weight charging)
- ✅ Thermometer (for superheat/subcooling)
